15:53 Codex gpt-5.4 (xhigh) thought for 6m49s · 6m52s wall-clock time

I’m reading the repo state and the project instructions first, then I’ll run the quals and trace the code paths that produce the current numbers so I can look for logic errors rather than just surface symptoms.

I have the repo map and the project constraints. Next I’m reading the core app, the data preprocessor, and the quals together so I can compare the intended model with the implemented one before trusting any outputs.

The implementation is larger than the first screenful. I’m drilling into the parts that define the incident metrics, month aggregation, VMT normalization, and qual expectations, because that’s where a mismatch between the math and the displayed numbers would show up.

I have enough structure to start checking the code against itself. I’m running the full qual suite now, then I’ll verify the headline counts and derived numbers independently from the raw data instead of trusting the embedded JS blobs.

The suite is not green. The first hard failure is a qual expecting the sanity-checks HTML to include the stress-test subsection heading Examen conclusionum durum, and the current render does not. I’m checking whether that’s a real regression in the app or a stale qual expectation, then I’ll finish the rest of the suite one-by-one so we have a complete failure set.

The failing quals are both in the new stress-analysis copy layer, not in the core math or aggregation. I’m checking the rendered labels against both the qual expectations and the AGENTS copy rule, then I’m independently recomputing the incident/VMT totals from the raw inputs to see if there are non-copy bugs too.

I found a substantive data discrepancy: the checked-in local CSV snapshot nhtsa-2025-jun-2026-jan.csv dedupes to 518 in-window incidents, but the generated incidents.js contains 530. The entire gap is June 2025: Waymo +11, Zoox +1. That matches the code path in preprocess.py that merges the live current CSV with the archive, so the local snapshot is not the same dataset the app was generated from. I’m isolating those extra incidents now so I can tell whether the app or the repo inputs are the outlier.

I found a likely semantic bug in the injury metrics: the severity classifier in crashla.js does not include the literal label "Moderate", but the embedded data has at least one Waymo incident with exactly that severity. That means at least one injury-class crash is currently being counted as non-injury in the summary and charts. I’m checking the full set of severity labels and where that record propagates through the metric totals.

The injury classifier issue is real and it propagates into the monthly aggregates. I’m also checking the nonstationary metric, because the code currently treats every speed === null incident as nonstationary, which could silently inflate the “nonstationary” series if some unknown-speed records were actually stopped.

There are no unknown-speed incidents in the current embedded dataset, so the nonstationary logic isn’t miscounting on that axis today. The injury classifier issue is still live: Waymo’s injury total is off by exactly 1 because "Moderate" is excluded. I’m using the qual harness’s loader next so I can inspect the exact monthly summary objects without fighting the app’s startup side effects.

I’ve confirmed one semantic metrics bug and two copy-level qual regressions. I’m running the full qual set in “continue after failure” mode once so I can report the complete pass/fail inventory instead of only the first stop.

The other thing I want quantified before I summarize is the effect of the misclassified "Moderate" injury. It’s not a huge swing at Waymo’s scale, but I want the exact delta on the injury and at-fault-injury series before I call it out.

The misclassified incident has zero AV fault, so it affects Waymo’s injury count and injury MPI, but not the at-fault injury series. I’m calculating the exact injury-MPI delta now.

I have enough to summarize the likely bugs and the repo-state inconsistencies. I’m grabbing exact line numbers for the findings so the next step list is concrete rather than hand-wavy.

One last locator and I can wrap this up cleanly: I want the exact line for the misclassified Moderate report in incidents.js, so the correctness bug points straight at the data row that triggers it.

Findings

  • The injury classifier is wrong for at least one real incident. [crashla.js:584](/Users/dreeves/lab/crashla/crashla.js#L584) omits the literal severity "Moderate", but [incidents.js:811](/Users/dreeves/lab/crashla/incidents.js#L811) / [incidents.js:821](/Users/dreeves/lab/crashla/incidents.js#L821) contain Waymo report 30270-11016 with severity: "Moderate". Current result: Waymo injury count is 54 instead of 55, and Waymo injury MPI median is 1.968M instead of about 1.933M. atFaultInjury is unchanged because that report’s AV fault is 0.
  • The repo is not locally reproducible from the checked-in raw CSV snapshot. [preprocess.py:17](/Users/dreeves/lab/crashla/preprocess.py#L17) and [preprocess.py:21](/Users/dreeves/lab/crashla/preprocess.py#L21) fetch both the current and archive NHTSA CSVs, and [incidents.js:1](/Users/dreeves/lab/crashla/incidents.js#L1) shows the embedded data was regenerated on March 4, 2026. But deduping the checked-in [nhtsa-2025-jun-2026-jan.csv](/Users/dreeves/lab/crashla/nhtsa-2025-jun-2026-jan.csv) gives 518 in-window incidents (492/14/12), while incidents.js embeds 530 (503/14/13). The 12 extra records are all June 2025 and are consistent with archive-only late filings. That makes offline auditing misleading.
  • The qual suite is red, and the failures are in the stress-analysis copy layer. npm run quals currently passes 8/10 and fails sanity-checks.qual.mjs and stress-test.qual.mjs. The app now renders English stress copy at [crashla.js:332](/Users/dreeves/lab/crashla/crashla.js#L332), [crashla.js:1190](/Users/dreeves/lab/crashla/crashla.js#L1190), [crashla.js:1252](/Users/dreeves/lab/crashla/crashla.js#L1252), and [crashla.js:1254](/Users/dreeves/lab/crashla/crashla.js#L1254), while the quals expect the earlier Latin labels. That also conflicts with the AGENTS UI-copy rule.

Verified State

  • Current embedded data is 530 incidents: Tesla 14, Waymo 503, Zoox 13.
  • Effective VMT totals used by the MPI summaries are about Tesla 587,440, Waymo 106.63M, Zoox 1.196M.
  • Default all-incident posterior medians are about Tesla 41.5k MPI, Waymo 211.9k, Zoox 90.8k.
  • Stress view currently says Tesla all-incidents is robustly worse than humans; Waymo all-incidents is ambiguous but Waymo is robustly safer on at-fault, injury, and serious-injury; Zoox remains ambiguous.
  • The AGENTS scratchpad is stale relative to the current app: it still describes the older 422-incident / incidents.json plan, while the repo now uses inline [incidents.js](/Users/dreeves/lab/crashla/incidents.js) and [vmt.js](/Users/dreeves/lab/crashla/vmt.js) data.

Next

  1. Fix the red quals first by restoring the stress-summary/table copy to the expected Latin labels, with TO DO comments for the eventual human-written English.
  2. Fix the severity map so "Moderate" counts as injury, then add a qual that locks that in using report 30270-11016.
  3. Make the data source reproducible: either check in the exact merged raw inputs the app is generated from, or add a manifest/qual that explicitly asserts the 12 archive-only June report IDs so local verification matches the shipped numbers.

Yes. The code path says exactly that.

[preprocess.py:17](/Users/dreeves/lab/crashla/preprocess.py#L17) and [preprocess.py:21](/Users/dreeves/lab/crashla/preprocess.py#L21) define the live NHTSA current and archive URLs, [preprocess.py:150](/Users/dreeves/lab/crashla/preprocess.py#L150) fetches both, and [preprocess.py:505](/Users/dreeves/lab/crashla/preprocess.py#L505) calls that fetch in main(). [preprocess.py:395](/Users/dreeves/lab/crashla/preprocess.py#L395) also fetches the VMT sheet live from Google Sheets, and [preprocess.py:652](/Users/dreeves/lab/crashla/preprocess.py#L652) plus [preprocess.py:667](/Users/dreeves/lab/crashla/preprocess.py#L667) inject the results into [incidents.js](/Users/dreeves/lab/crashla/incidents.js) and [vmt.js](/Users/dreeves/lab/crashla/vmt.js).

I also checked for references to the checked-in nhtsa-*.csv files. So if those CSVs are just archival snapshots, that is consistent with the current implementation. The earlier 518 vs 530 discrepancy is therefore not a runtime bug; it’s only a repo-documentation / reproducibility issue if you want the checked-in snapshots to match the generated app data exactly.

[do_pandas.py](/Users/dreeves/lab/crashla/do_pandas.py) looks like a one-off helper for rewriting [faultfrac-gemini.csv](/Users/dreeves/lab/crashla/faultfrac-gemini.csv), not part of the current app pipeline.

What it does:

  • reads two local NHTSA snapshots via absolute paths, including /Users/dreeves/temp_nhtsa/archive.csv
  • derives SV Hit / CP Hit from the contact-area Y columns
  • merges those fields plus speed / crashwith / severity onto faultfrac-gemini.csv
  • normalizes blanks/defaults
  • overwrites faultfrac-gemini.csv with the enriched columns

What I found:

  • nothing else in the repo references do_pandas.py or pandas
  • the active pipeline is [preprocess.py](/Users/dreeves/lab/crashla/preprocess.py), which fetches live NHTSA + VMT data and only requires each fault CSV to include reportID, faultfrac, and reasoning
  • extra columns in the fault CSVs are tolerated, but not required
  • trying to run do_pandas.py here fails immediately because pandas is not installed

So: no, the current app does not need it. It only seems worth keeping if you still want a personal repair/enrichment script for regenerating the extra metadata columns in [faultfrac-gemini.csv](/Users/dreeves/lab/crashla/faultfrac-gemini.csv). Otherwise it is dead weight and probably removable.

I reread [AGENTS.md](/Users/dreeves/lab/crashla/AGENTS.md), reproduced the live failure, and fixed the actual bad assumption.

Root cause: my previous version assumed Report ID was globally unique across all historical driverless NHTSA rows. That is false. The live fetch has reused Waymo report IDs outside the app window, including 30270-9110, so the old code crashed while building the fault-master map.

The fix is in [preprocess.py:239](/Users/dreeves/lab/crashla/preprocess.py#L239) and [preprocess.py:634](/Users/dreeves/lab/crashla/preprocess.py#L634): fault-master rows are now built only for the report IDs that actually appear in the faultfrac-*.csv files, instead of for every historical driverless incident. The only new condition I needed was the filter at [preprocess.py:244](/Users/dreeves/lab/crashla/preprocess.py#L244), which excludes non-target report IDs from that sync source. That removes the bad global-uniqueness assumption instead of adding a special-case exception.

I also reran npm run quals; the full suite passes, including the new [fault-csv-sync.qual.mjs](/Users/dreeves/lab/crashla/quals/fault-csv-sync.qual.mjs#L1).

wait, i'm not sure csv files are allowed to have comments like that, are they?
16:24 Codex gpt-5.4 (xhigh) thought for 25s

No. Standard CSV does not have a comment syntax.

# ... lines are tolerated by some tools, but not by CSV in the RFC sense, and not by many spreadsheet/database readers. Python’s csv module also does not treat comments specially unless you add your own wrapper, which is what I did. So the current data/faultfrac-*.csv comment line is nonstandard and I don’t think we should keep it.

The JS files are fine with top-of-file comments. The raw snapshot CSVs should stay uncommented. For the editable fault CSVs, the better place for provenance is a neighboring doc or sidecar metadata file, not an in-band CSV comment.

I can change it back now by:

  1. removing the # Provenance: lines from data/faultfrac-*.csv
  2. removing the comment-skipping logic from [data/slurp.py](/Users/dreeves/lab/crashla/data/slurp.py)
  3. updating the provenance qual to assert provenance is documented outside the CSVs

Because that changes a qual expectation, I want your explicit go-ahead before I do it.

I found a likely root cause and it doesn’t require a new branch: Path.read_text() normalizes \r\n to \n, so an unchanged CSV fetched with CRLF can look different on every run and get snapshotted repeatedly. I’m adding a qual for that exact replicatum first, then changing the helper to compare raw bytes instead of newline-normalized text.

I’ve reproduced the bug with a CRLF payload: the current helper writes two snapshots for identical content. I’m editing data/slurp.py to compare exact bytes without adding any new if statements, and I’m extending quals/snapshot-policy.qual.mjs to lock that in.

The bug was newline normalization: read_text() turns \r\n into \n, so an unchanged CSV could look different on the next fetch and get snapshotted again. I changed the helper to compare exact decoded bytes instead. No new if statements were added.

I also extended [quals/snapshot-policy.qual.mjs#L19](/Users/dreeves/lab/crashla/quals/snapshot-policy.qual.mjs#L19) with a CRLF replicatum so this stays covered. Verified with a direct Python repro and with npm run quals; the full qual suite passed.

2026-03-09 Monday

can you have slurp.py also generate a static version of the whole website, with full explanation of how all the results are generated? i'm thinking we put this in a /static subdirectory. and maybe we could have both html and pdf versions? before doing anything, let me know what you think?
14:36 Codex gpt-5.4 (xhigh) thought for 1m17s

I’m reading the current generation pipeline first so I can answer in terms of what this repo already does and what would fit cleanly.

I think the goal is good, but I would separate “generate the report inputs” from “render the report”.

Right now [data/slurp.py](/Users/dreeves/lab/crashla/data/slurp.py) owns the fetch/normalize/snapshot pipeline, while a lot of the actual result math and presentation logic lives in [crashla.js](/Users/dreeves/lab/crashla/crashla.js). If slurp.py alone starts generating a full frozen site, we either duplicate that JS logic in Python or accept two render paths that will drift. I would avoid that.

My recommendation:

  • Yes to a /static output directory.
  • Yes to a generated static HTML report with the full methodology, provenance, sanity checks, and frozen results.
  • PDF should be phase 2, generated from that HTML, not from a separate code path.
  • I would keep slurp.py responsible for producing structured report data, but use a separate renderer for the HTML/PDF layer.

Concretely, the clean shape is:

  • slurp.py writes the normal data files plus a report artifact like static/report.json or similar.
  • A renderer builds static/index.html from that artifact, including:
  • exact inclusion/filtering rules
  • dedup rules
  • VMT assumptions and monthly rationale
  • incident-coverage adjustment
  • Bayesian MPI method
  • fault-fraction method
  • human baseline method
  • source timestamps / snapshot provenance
  • the actual computed tables/charts
  • Later, static/report.pdf is produced from static/index.html via one print/render step.

That keeps one source of truth for the numbers and one source of truth for the prose/report structure.

If you want, the next step I’d take is phase 1 only: generate a self-contained static HTML report in /static, and explicitly defer PDF until the HTML shape is right.

should that be another script that generates that?
14:45 Codex gpt-5.4 (xhigh) thought for 39s

Yes. I would make it a separate script.

[data/slurp.py](/Users/dreeves/lab/crashla/data/slurp.py) already has a clear job: fetch, snapshot, dedup, normalize, and emit canonical data artifacts. Static-site generation is a different concern, and PDF generation is a third one. Keeping them separate is the simpler design and gives fewer ways for the pipeline to fail.

The shape I’d use is:

  • [data/slurp.py](/Users/dreeves/lab/crashla/data/slurp.py): produce the data and any machine-readable provenance/methodology payload the report needs.
  • [data/build_static.py](/Users/dreeves/lab/crashla/data/build_static.py): read those artifacts and emit static/index.html plus any copied assets.
  • Later, a separate PDF step: render static/index.html to static/report.pdf.

If you still want one command, slurp.py can invoke the static builder at the end, but the implementation should live in another module/script.

One important constraint: a lot of the result math currently lives in [crashla.js](/Users/dreeves/lab/crashla/crashla.js). So the static builder should avoid re-implementing that math in Python unless we explicitly decide to move the shared computations into a common layer. My preference is to keep one source of truth for the calculations and have the static builder assemble a frozen page around it, not duplicate the logic.

If you want, I can sketch the exact file layout and data flow before implementing it.

ok
14:46 Codex gpt-5.4 (xhigh) thought for 31s

Proposed shape:

Files

  • [data/slurp.py](/Users/dreeves/lab/crashla/data/slurp.py)

Keeps doing live fetch, dedup, fault/VMT sync, and writing canonical data artifacts.

  • [data/build_static.py](/Users/dreeves/lab/crashla/data/build_static.py)

Builds a frozen report site in static/.

  • [data/reportmeta.json](/Users/dreeves/lab/crashla/data/reportmeta.json) or similar

Machine-readable methodology/provenance that slurp.py writes.

  • [static/index.html](/Users/dreeves/lab/crashla/static/index.html)

Self-contained report page.

  • Later: [data/render_pdf.py](/Users/dreeves/lab/crashla/data/render_pdf.py) or an npm script

Converts static/index.html to static/report.pdf.

Data flow

  1. slurp.py fetches/snapshots upstream CSVs and writes the canonical app data:

[data/incidents.js](/Users/dreeves/lab/crashla/data/incidents.js), [data/vmt.js](/Users/dreeves/lab/crashla/data/vmt.js).

  1. slurp.py also writes a small metadata artifact with:
  • fetch timestamps
  • snapshot filenames used
  • window dates
  • dedup rule
  • incident filter rule
  • coverage adjustments
  • fault-model filenames
  1. build_static.py copies the app assets into static/ and emits a report wrapper around the existing site:
  • intro / methodology / provenance sections
  • links to the exact snapshots
  • explanation of every computed field
  • the same charts/tables, using the same JS logic
  1. Later, PDF rendering prints that frozen HTML.

Why this is the clean cut

  • No duplicate fetch logic.
  • No duplicate chart math in Python.
  • One source of truth for calculations remains [crashla.js](/Users/dreeves/lab/crashla/crashla.js).
  • The “report” layer is additive: it explains and packages results instead of recomputing them.

What I would implement first

  • Phase 1: build_static.py creates static/ with copied assets plus a generated static/index.html that adds the full explanation/provenance around the current app.
  • Phase 2: PDF from that HTML.
  • Only after that would I consider moving shared computations out of [crashla.js](/Users/dreeves/lab/crashla/crashla.js) if we really need server-side frozen numbers.

If you want, I can implement phase 1 next.

Implemented phase 1.

[data/build_static.py](/Users/dreeves/lab/crashla/data/build_static.py#L1) now builds a frozen copy of the current site into [static/index.html](/Users/dreeves/lab/crashla/static/index.html) plus the required assets and [static/data/incidents.js](/Users/dreeves/lab/crashla/static/data/incidents.js), [static/data/vmt.js](/Users/dreeves/lab/crashla/static/data/vmt.js). [data/slurp.py](/Users/dreeves/lab/crashla/data/slurp.py#L19) now calls that builder after rewriting the canonical data files, so a normal slurp run also refreshes the static snapshot. I verified it three ways: python3 data/build_static.py, a full live python3 data/slurp.py run, and npm run quals. The live slurp succeeded end-to-end, rebuilt static/, and reported 530 incidents total: Waymo 503, Tesla 14, Zoox 13. The only live-data churn in tracked files was [data/incidents.js](/Users/dreeves/lab/crashla/data/incidents.js#L2) updating NHTSA_FETCH_DATE to 2026-03-09.

This is the packaging step, not the PDF step. Also, the “full explanation” in the static build is currently the existing site’s explanation path, especially the Sources and sanity checks section, not a new long-form generated report. The only new if is the standard module-entrypoint guard in [data/build_static.py](/Users/dreeves/lab/crashla/data/build_static.py#L50). 2026-03-18 Wednesday

can your read AGENTS.md and do a thorough audit of all the code and data here?
20:31 Codex gpt-5.4 (xhigh) thought for 12m1s · 12m4s wall-clock time

Reading AGENTS.md constraints and then walking the repo top to bottom: source, data pipeline, generated data, and quals. After that I'll run the qual suite and write the audit as review findings with concrete file references.

I have the repo inventory and the local agent rules. Next I'm reading the app, data sources, and qual files in parallel so the audit covers both implementation and data assumptions, not just surface behavior.

I'm into the implementation now. The main risk areas look like: data windowing and dedup in data/slurp.py, statistical assumptions in crashla.js, and places where the UI mixes "all incidents" with observability/coverage rules.

The app code is mapped now. I'm switching to the quals to infer intended invariants and to spot blind spots where the current suite may be passing while an assumption is still unsafe.

I have enough structure to verify the repo instead of just reading it. Running the qual suite now, then I’ll do a few targeted data checks around counts, coverage, and any places where the code and docs appear to diverge.

The qual run is clean. I'm probing the generated data directly now because a passing qual suite doesn't tell me whether the runtime data actually matches the repo's stated analysis window or whether there are dead assertions masking missing invariants.

I found two issues that are no longer hypothetical. One is a straight UI bug: date sorting is lexicographic, so "AUG-2025" sorts before "JUN-2025". The other is a data/invariant bug: the app is shipping incidents with fault: null inside the active comparison window, and the aggregation currently counts those as 0 at-fault instead of unknown.

Findings

  1. At-fault metrics are currently biased low because missing fault rows inside the active comparison window are silently accepted and then aggregated as zero. data/slurp.py only targets report IDs already present in the faultfrac-*.csv files ([data/slurp.py#L291](/Users/dreeves/lab/crashla/data/slurp.py#L291), [data/slurp.py#L316](/Users/dreeves/lab/crashla/data/slurp.py#L316)), its missing_fault check is tautological ([data/slurp.py#L797](/Users/dreeves/lab/crashla/data/slurp.py#L797), [data/slurp.py#L801](/Users/dreeves/lab/crashla/data/slurp.py#L801)), and monthSeriesData() then does rec.atFault += atFaultFrac || 0 ([crashla.js#L806](/Users/dreeves/lab/crashla/crashla.js#L806), [crashla.js#L816](/Users/dreeves/lab/crashla/crashla.js#L816)). I verified 91 currently visible-window incidents have fault: null: 89 Waymo and 2 Zoox, all in 2026-01/2026-02. That means default atfault / atfaultInjury MPI is understated.
  1. Incident-browser date sorting is wrong. The date sort key is the raw MMM-YYYY string ([crashla.js#L1772](/Users/dreeves/lab/crashla/crashla.js#L1772)), and renderTable() string-sorts non-numeric columns with localeCompare ([crashla.js#L1975](/Users/dreeves/lab/crashla/crashla.js#L1975), [crashla.js#L1982](/Users/dreeves/lab/crashla/crashla.js#L1982)). On current default data, ascending sort starts with AUG-2025 instead of JUN-2025.
  1. The date slider exposes pre-2025-06 Waymo history, but the summary/distribution layer drops those months while the charts still render them. monthlySummaryRows() filters to incidentObservable months only ([crashla.js#L685](/Users/dreeves/lab/crashla/crashla.js#L685), [crashla.js#L687](/Users/dreeves/lab/crashla/crashla.js#L687)), while the charts are rendered from the sliced series directly ([crashla.js#L1651](/Users/dreeves/lab/crashla/crashla.js#L1651), [crashla.js#L1657](/Users/dreeves/lab/crashla/crashla.js#L1657)). I verified that selecting 2022-11 through 2023-04 leaves Waymo plotted with real VMT/MPI data, but the summary row collapses to vmtBest = 0 and incTotal = 0. The cards no longer describe the chart.
  1. The distribution chart’s y-axis label is technically incorrect. The code explicitly plots densities with respect to log(x) ([crashla.js#L104](/Users/dreeves/lab/crashla/crashla.js#L104), [crashla.js#L111](/Users/dreeves/lab/crashla/crashla.js#L111)), but the axis label says Probability Density for True MPI ([crashla.js#L1199](/Users/dreeves/lab/crashla/crashla.js#L1199)). That label implies x-space density, which this chart is not showing.

Open Questions

  • The runtime data and the README are out of sync. The README still says the tool is scoped to June 15, 2025 through January 15, 2026 and “530 incidents: 503 Waymo, 14 Tesla, 13 Zoox” ([README.md#L153](/Users/dreeves/lab/crashla/README.md#L153), [README.md#L219](/Users/dreeves/lab/crashla/README.md#L219), [README.md#L230](/Users/dreeves/lab/crashla/README.md#L230)). The current app defaults to 2025-06 through 2026-02, and the generated artifact currently contains 622 incidents in that visible range and 1,534 incidents overall back to 2022-11. If that expansion is intentional, the docs should say so explicitly.
  • The green qual suite does not currently cover the three concrete failures above: chronological date sorting, fault coverage in the active window, or chart/card consistency when the slider is moved before 2025-06.

Verification

npm run quals passes. I also ran targeted Node/Python checks against the generated artifacts and runtime functions to confirm the four findings above. No files were modified.

21:07 Codex gpt-5.4 (xhigh) thought for 10m39s · 10m42s wall-clock time

I’m fixing #1 through #3 in code and quals, plus adding the requested README annotations. On #4, after rechecking: because the distribution plot has no y tick values and is being used qualitatively, I don’t think it’s strong enough to justify changing end-user copy right now, so I’m leaving that one alone unless the implementation work exposes a clearer non-copy fix.

The conditionals I expect to need are limited and data-driven:

  • fault-weighted metrics only compute for driver-months where faultKnown === total
  • summary aggregation for those metrics only uses months whose per-month metric exists
  • the date-range start gets clamped to the declared analysis start month so pre-2025-06 months stay inaccessible from the UI and URL state

I’m patching crashla.js now. The main structural change is metric availability becoming data-driven: fault-weighted metrics become unavailable for months whose incidents are not all fault-annotated, instead of silently treating unknowns as 0.

The first patch attempt missed a couple of exact contexts. I’m re-reading the affected blocks and patching in smaller hunks so I don’t accidentally trample anything adjacent.

I’m also removing the dead missing_fault assertion from data/slurp.py rather than trying to “repair” it with a fake fallback. With the runtime now treating incomplete fault months as unavailable for fault-weighted metrics, keeping a tautological assert there just creates false confidence.

I’ve finished the core JS patch and am adding the README annotations exactly as requested: new bracketed notes only, no edits to the existing prose.

I spotted one more edge while writing the clamp qual: if a stale URL or manual state sets monthRangeEnd before 2025-06, startIdx would clamp up but endIdx could still stay below it. I’m tightening that clamp now instead of letting an empty slice slip through.

I’m adding three focused quals now: one for chronological date sorting, one for fault-metric completeness, and one for clamping stale pre-window date ranges. These are the gaps that let the bugs survive a green suite before.

The code and qual patches are in. I’m reading the diff before running the suite so I catch any structural mistakes locally instead of discovering them one failure at a time.

The qual suite is green again. I’m doing one last direct spot-check of the three runtime behaviors I changed so the close-out is based on actual behavior, not just passing quals.

Patched #1 through #3.

atfault and atfaultInjury now go unavailable for driver-months with incomplete fault judgments instead of silently treating unknowns as 0, and the summary aggregation only uses months where those per-month metrics actually exist. That change is in [crashla.js#L209](/Users/dreeves/lab/crashla/crashla.js#L209), [crashla.js#L690](/Users/dreeves/lab/crashla/crashla.js#L690), and [crashla.js#L805](/Users/dreeves/lab/crashla/crashla.js#L805). The incident browser now sorts dates chronologically via monthKeyFromIncidentLabel(...) at [crashla.js#L1790](/Users/dreeves/lab/crashla/crashla.js#L1790). The date slider/URL state now clamp stale pre-2025-06 ranges back to the declared analysis window at [crashla.js#L1588](/Users/dreeves/lab/crashla/crashla.js#L1588) and [crashla.js#L1650](/Users/dreeves/lab/crashla/crashla.js#L1650). I also removed the dead fault-coverage assert from [data/slurp.py#L797](/Users/dreeves/lab/crashla/data/slurp.py#L797), since it was giving false confidence, and added the requested README annotations at [README.md#L155](/Users/dreeves/lab/crashla/README.md#L155) and [README.md#L234](/Users/dreeves/lab/crashla/README.md#L234).

I left #4 unchanged. After rechecking, I don’t think it’s strong enough to count as a real bug in this UI: the chart has no y tick values, so the label is functioning as qualitative framing rather than a numerically precise density axis. Changing it would mostly be new end-user copy, and I don’t think that clears the bar here.

I added quals for the three fixed regressions: [date-sorting.qual.mjs](/Users/dreeves/lab/crashla/quals/date-sorting.qual.mjs), [fault-metric-completeness.qual.mjs](/Users/dreeves/lab/crashla/quals/fault-metric-completeness.qual.mjs), and [window-clamp.qual.mjs](/Users/dreeves/lab/crashla/quals/window-clamp.qual.mjs). npm run quals passes.

Investigating the slider regression now. I’m starting with the range-control and URL-state code paths because that’s the part I changed, and I’m going to verify behavior against the current implementation before patching anything.

Investigating the slider regression now. I’m starting with the date-range control and state code I changed, then I’ll reproduce the breakage locally and patch it before rerunning quals.

The regression is likely in the date-range control math rather than the summary/data clamp. I’m checking the slider’s positioning code and the URL sync path now, because both changed together.

The worktree diff is clean, so the current breakage is already in the checked-in state rather than sitting in uncommitted edits. I’m comparing the present slider code against the surrounding state logic to find the mismatch.

The more likely root cause is that I “fixed” the inconsistency by restricting the control, which violates the original affordance. I’m re-checking the summary aggregation now to see if the cleaner fix is to remove that restriction and make the cards follow the selected months instead.

I found the conceptual mistake: I solved the chart/card mismatch by constraining the control, which is exactly the kind of restriction the project rules say to avoid. I’m verifying whether the regression is simply that earlier months became unreachable; if so, the clean fix is to restore the slider and make the summary honor the selected months instead.

I’ve got headless Chromium available, so I’m using that to inspect the live DOM instead of guessing. That should tell me whether the bug is “can’t reach earlier months anymore” or a more literal slider-rendering break.

I’ve reproduced the regression in code: a pre-2025-06 range now gets forced back to 2025-06, so the slider can no longer reach the earlier Waymo months. The clean fix is to remove that clamp from the slider path and from monthlySummaryRows() so the cards follow the selected months instead; I don’t need any new behavior-specific if branches for that.

I’ve pinned down the patch: restore the slider’s full month span, keep the default start at 2025-06, and make summary aggregation follow whatever slice is actually selected. I also need to replace the qual that encoded the clamp regression with one that checks “earlier months remain reachable and the summary stays nonzero for Waymo there.”

I’ve removed the clamp from the control path and switched the summary logic back to the actual selected slice. Running the targeted qual first now, then the full suite, to make sure the slider behavior is restored without regressing the other fixes.

The first qual exposed a separate break in the sanity table: earlier Waymo slices legitimately have null fault-based MPI estimates, and driverHumanStress() was assuming every selected metric exists. I’m patching that now so the restored slider can traverse earlier months without tripping the sanity view.

The sanity-table fix is simpler than special-casing: only render stress rows for metrics that actually have MPI estimates in the current slice, and assert inside driverHumanStress() if anything calls it with a missing estimate. That keeps the earlier fault-metric nullability honest instead of papering over it.

The stress table patch is in. Re-running the slider qual plus the other touched areas now, then I’ll do the full qual suite.

The first chunk of the full qual run is clean. I’m waiting for the rest to complete before I call the slider fixed.

The monthly-series qual caught that I changed the meaning of monthlySummaryRows() too broadly. I’m tightening that fix now so only sliced views gain the broader summary window; the full-series helper will go back to its original semantics.

I’m narrowing the change so the broader summary window exists only on sliced views, not on the raw full series helper. That preserves the original helper semantics and should bring the existing monthly-series qual back without giving up the slider fix.

The helper semantics are restored and sliced views now carry their own observability flag. I’m rerunning the monthly-series qual that failed, plus the slider qual and the full suite after that.

The targeted quals are green again. I’m just waiting on the full suite to finish so I can close this out with verified behavior instead of an unproven claim.

The regression was the clamp I added on the date-range control. I removed that restriction so the slider can reach the early Waymo months again in [crashla.js#L1594](/Users/dreeves/lab/crashla/crashla.js#L1594), [crashla.js#L1618](/Users/dreeves/lab/crashla/crashla.js#L1618), and [crashla.js#L1663](/Users/dreeves/lab/crashla/crashla.js#L1663). The summary/cards now stay consistent for non-default slices by making sliced series treat the selected months as observable in [crashla.js#L941](/Users/dreeves/lab/crashla/crashla.js#L941), instead of changing the full-series helper semantics.

That exposed one real follow-on bug in the sanity table for fault-based metrics on early slices. I made that fail loudly in [crashla.js#L774](/Users/dreeves/lab/crashla/crashla.js#L774) and stopped rendering nonexistent stress rows in [crashla.js#L1465](/Users/dreeves/lab/crashla/crashla.js#L1465). No new behavior-specific if branches were added for the slider fix. I also replaced the slider qual that had encoded the bad clamp with a qual that checks early months stay reachable and summarize correctly in [window-clamp.qual.mjs#L127](/Users/dreeves/lab/crashla/quals/window-clamp.qual.mjs#L127).

walk me through what this would do:

head -n 831 data/faultfrac-gemini.csv > temp.csv && mv temp.csv data/faultfrac-gemini.csv
11:33 Copilot Chat copilot/gemini-3.1-pro-preview thought for 1m3s

Here is a step-by-step breakdown of exactly what that command does:

  1. head -n 831 data/faultfrac-gemini.csv

This reads exactly the first 831 lines of the file and outputs them. (Anything from line 832 onward is ignored).

  1. > temp.csv

This redirects that output (the 831 lines) and saves it into a new temporary file named temp.csv.

  1. &&

This operator tells the terminal to only run the next part of the command *if* the first part succeeded without any errors.

  1. mv temp.csv data/faultfrac-gemini.csv

This moves (renames) temp.csv to overwrite .

The final result:
It permanently truncates so that it contains *only* its first 831 lines. Any data that existed on line 832 or below is completely deleted.

*(Note: In my previous cancelled command, I used head -n -20, where the minus sign means "keep everything *except* the last 20 lines". I was trying to surgically remove the last 20 corrupted lines I had accidentally appended, not keep only 20 lines! But using a text editor or being more careful is definitely safer.)*

Since those exact 5 missing incidents (which start with 30270-13561) were *already* in your file on lines 794-798 (I must have successfully originally added them right before the crash, and then fetched them again!), attempting to append them *again* on line 832 caused a duplicate ID failure (AssertionError: duplicate Report ID).

I’m at the benchmark/methodology layer now. The obvious candidate mismatch is that our app still encodes Waymo’s older 127M/Sep-2025 safety-hub figures in several places, so I’m verifying whether that’s the first top-to-bottom discrepancy versus the live page’s current 170.7M/Dec-2025 figures.

The first discrepancy is the Waymo snapshot date and mileage.

Waymo’s current page says: through December 2025, Waymo has driven 170.7M rider-only miles without a human driver, with city totals shown underneath. See Waymo Safety Impact at the top of the page.

Our app is still anchored to the older Waymo safety-hub snapshot: [crashla.js](/Users/dreeves/lab/crashla/crashla.js#L156) cites 127M mi, Sep 2025, and the user-visible benchmark/source text still uses labels like Waymo safety impact (127M mi) plus benchmark values derived from that older snapshot in [crashla.js](/Users/dreeves/lab/crashla/crashla.js#L242), [crashla.js](/Users/dreeves/lab/crashla/crashla.js#L256), [crashla.js](/Users/dreeves/lab/crashla/crashla.js#L274), and [crashla.js](/Users/dreeves/lab/crashla/crashla.js#L309). The README does the same in [README.md](/Users/dreeves/lab/crashla/README.md#L180).

So the first mismatch, top-to-bottom, is: Waymo now publishes 170.7M through December 2025, while our app analysis still cites 127M through September 2025 as the Waymo safety-page basis.

Right after that, there are downstream numeric drifts too: the live page now shows all-location human benchmarks of 3.90, 1.63, and 0.22 IPMM, while our app still cites 3.97, 1.66, and 0.23.

16:58 Codex gpt-5.4 (xhigh) thought for 41s

I’m checking the checked-in Waymo VMT series and computing the cumulative through December 2025 from the actual data file, not by inference.

The file format wasn’t what I assumed, so I’m reading it directly and recomputing from the actual CSV payload.

I have the monthly rows. I’m summing the central/min/max VMT columns directly from those Waymo entries to get the through-December totals.

In our checked-in Waymo VMT series, the cumulative through December 2025 is 172,484,810 miles at [data/vmt.js](/Users/dreeves/lab/crashla/data/vmt.js#L57).

If you mean just the June-December 2025 slice, we have:

  • Central: 84,780,026
  • Low: 61,310,779
  • High: 108,249,273

Those monthly rows are [data/vmt.js](/Users/dreeves/lab/crashla/data/vmt.js#L51) through [data/vmt.js](/Users/dreeves/lab/crashla/data/vmt.js#L57).

stay focused on the big picture: what errors do we have here, based on carefully reading of https://waymo.com/safety/impact/
17:01 Codex gpt-5.4 (xhigh) thought for 3m30s

I’ve found one likely bigger issue than the stale 127M number: Waymo’s page is not just using a flat “surface streets” benchmark. I’m checking whether our app is missing their dynamic geographic/driving-mix adjustment entirely, because that would be a methodology-level error rather than a small numeric drift.

There’s another potential top-level mismatch: Waymo says its current hub focuses on surface streets only. I’m checking whether our app’s Waymo numerator includes freeway incidents, because that would be an apples-to-apples problem independent of the stale numbers.

The big errors are mostly methodological, not just numeric.

  • The main one is that we talk as if we’re broadly aligned with Waymo’s human-benchmark method, but we aren’t. Waymo says its hub uses crashed-vehicle rates adjusted for Waymo’s actual spatial driving mix using the Chen et al. dynamic benchmark method, with benchmarks derived from Phoenix, San Francisco, Los Angeles, and Austin, and with freeways excluded. Our repo still describes the benchmark much more simply as “surface streets in AV operating areas” and uses one shared human band in [METRIC_DEFS](/Users/dreeves/lab/crashla/crashla.js#L166), plus the README says the methodology is just Kusano/Scanlon plus old Waymo numbers in [README.md](/Users/dreeves/lab/crashla/README.md#L180) and [README.md](/Users/dreeves/lab/crashla/README.md#L183). That is the biggest big-picture mismatch. Source: Waymo Safety Impact Methodology/Human Benchmarks sections.
  • We are plainly stale on the Waymo snapshot. The live page now says 170.7M rider-only miles through December 2025, with all-location benchmarks of 3.90 any-injury IPMM, 1.63 airbag-in-any-vehicle IPMM, and 0.22 serious-injury-or-worse IPMM, plus 43% of collisions under 1 mph delta-V. We still cite the older 127M through September 2025 snapshot and older 3.97 / 1.66 / 0.23 values in [crashla.js](/Users/dreeves/lab/crashla/crashla.js#L156), [README.md](/Users/dreeves/lab/crashla/README.md#L180), [README.md](/Users/dreeves/lab/crashla/README.md#L195), and [README.md](/Users/dreeves/lab/crashla/README.md#L206). Source: Waymo Safety Impact.
  • Our README overstates what the Waymo page establishes about RO miles. In [README.md](/Users/dreeves/lab/crashla/README.md#L204) we say Waymo’s “rider-only miles” include deadhead/overhead and match CPUC TotalVMTZEV. The page itself says RO miles are miles Waymo drove without a human driver in cities where it operates its ride-hailing service. That may be compatible with our interpretation, but from this page alone it is not proven. So this is at least an unsupported provenance claim. Source: Waymo Safety Impact.
  • Our default framing is also off relative to Waymo’s own stated framing. Waymo explicitly says the emphasis should be on injury-causing crashes, because AV reporting includes many minor low-speed contacts that human police-report datasets miss. We still make “All incidents” the default primary metric in [crashla.js](/Users/dreeves/lab/crashla/crashla.js#L167). That is an inference-level critique, not a direct factual contradiction, but it is a real big-picture mismatch with the page’s methodology. Source: Waymo Safety Impact.
  • The actual Waymo denominator in our checked-in data is not wildly wrong. We have 172,484,810 cumulative Waymo miles through December 2025 in [data/vmt.js](/Users/dreeves/lab/crashla/data/vmt.js#L57), which is only about 1% above Waymo’s published 170.7M. So the biggest problem is not the central Waymo VMT number; it is the stale citations and overstated methodology alignment.

If you want, next I can turn that into a concrete cleanup plan with the minimum necessary edits.

please think more deeply. how is 170.7M through Dec incompatible with 127M through Sep? please follow rules 1 and 2 in AGENTS.md more carefully
17:17 Codex gpt-5.4 (xhigh) thought for 2m

I’m re-checking the Waymo page against our assumptions and separating “stale snapshot” from “actual incompatibility.” The 170.7M and 127M figures may both be true, so I’m verifying what the page actually says before answering.

I’ve got the actual issue list now. The real problem is not “127 vs 170.7”; it’s that we are importing Waymo-hub methodology into our app more loosely than the page itself supports. I’m pulling the exact local code points so I can state that precisely.

My earlier wording was incorrect. 170.7M through December 2025 is compatible with 127M through September 2025. Those are just two cumulative snapshots at different dates. In fact, our own checked-in Waymo series goes from 127,000,000 at September 2025 to 172,484,810 at December 2025 in [data/vmt.js](/Users/dreeves/lab/crashla/data/vmt.js#L54) and [data/vmt.js](/Users/dreeves/lab/crashla/data/vmt.js#L57), which is close to Waymo’s live-page 170.7M.

The actual errors are these:

  • We cite the current live Waymo page as if it were the source for the old Sep-2025 snapshot. That is the error, not the coexistence of 127M and 170.7M. Our repo still attributes 127M, 3.97, 1.66, 0.23, and 45% to the Waymo page in [crashla.js](/Users/dreeves/lab/crashla/crashla.js#L156), [README.md](/Users/dreeves/lab/crashla/README.md#L180), and [README.md](/Users/dreeves/lab/crashla/README.md#L206), while the current live page now shows 170.7M, 3.90, 1.63, 0.22, and 43%. Source: Waymo Safety Impact.
  • The bigger methodological error is that we treat Waymo-hub human benchmarks too generically. The Waymo page says those benchmarks are Waymo-specific and dynamically adjusted to Waymo’s own driving distribution using the Chen et al. method. We instead hard-code shared human benchmark bands in [METRIC_DEFS](/Users/dreeves/lab/crashla/crashla.js#L166) and use them across Tesla, Waymo, and Zoox. That is not what the Waymo page is doing. Source: Waymo Safety Impact.
  • We overclaim what the Waymo page proves about RO miles. In [README.md](/Users/dreeves/lab/crashla/README.md#L204) we say Waymo’s “rider-only miles” include deadhead and overhead and match CPUC TotalVMTZEV. The page says RO miles are miles driven without a human driver in cities where Waymo operates ride-hailing service. That may be compatible with our interpretation, but the page itself does not establish the stronger claim.
  • Our default framing is not Waymo-hub framing. The Waymo page says the focus should be on injury-causing crashes and on surface streets only. Our default primary metric is still all incidents in [crashla.js](/Users/dreeves/lab/crashla/crashla.js#L167), and the monthly aggregation adds every incident into rec.total with no road-type filter in [crashla.js](/Users/dreeves/lab/crashla/crashla.js#L768). That makes our default comparison looser than the Waymo page’s apples-to-apples setup.

So the big correction is: the Dec and Sep cumulative-mile figures are not the problem. The real problems are stale attribution, overclaiming methodological alignment, and treating Waymo-specific benchmark logic as if it were a universal human baseline.

is there anything in the human data from waymo that's outside our, say, 80% confidence intervals for any of the MPI numbers for humans?
18:00 Codex gpt-5.4 (xhigh) thought for 1m34s

I’m checking our human benchmark bands against the current Waymo human benchmark numbers directly, metric by metric. The first step is to verify exactly how our app encodes the human ranges so I don’t answer with the wrong interval notion.

I need to disambiguate “human data from Waymo”: the all-location benchmark points are mostly inside our current bands, but the city-level Waymo human benchmarks may not be. I’m computing both so the answer is precise.

Yes, if you include the city-level human benchmarks on the current Waymo page.

One important nuance first: in our app the human “50% / 80% / 95%” fan is not three different intervals. For humans, all three bands are the same fixed lo..hi range in [crashla.js](/Users/dreeves/lab/crashla/crashla.js#L821). So your “80% confidence interval” question is really about whether Waymo’s human benchmark points fall inside our current human benchmark bands.

If you mean the All Locations row only, then the direct matches are basically inside:

  • Injury: Waymo human 3.90 IPMM = about 256k MPI, inside our 252k..524k band in [crashla.js](/Users/dreeves/lab/crashla/crashla.js#L255).
  • Airbag in any vehicle: 1.63 IPMM = about 613k MPI, inside our 500k..700k band in [crashla.js](/Users/dreeves/lab/crashla/crashla.js#L291).
  • Serious injury or worse: 0.22 IPMM = about 4.55M MPI, inside our 3.3M..6.7M band in [crashla.js](/Users/dreeves/lab/crashla/crashla.js#L308).

The one small exception is our hospitalization surrogate band in [crashla.js](/Users/dreeves/lab/crashla/crashla.js#L273): its upper end is 4.35M, but if you derive that upper endpoint from Waymo’s current all-location serious benchmark 0.22, you get about 4.55M, which is slightly outside.

If you include the per-city human benchmarks from Waymo, then several are outside our global bands:

  • San Francisco injury benchmark 7.47 IPMM = about 134k MPI, below our injury band.
  • Phoenix serious benchmark 0.10 IPMM = 10.0M MPI, above our serious band.
  • San Francisco serious benchmark 0.43 IPMM = about 2.33M MPI, below our serious band.
  • Airbag benchmarks are outside in multiple cities too:

Phoenix 1.35 -> 741k above,
Los Angeles 1.23 -> 813k above,
San Francisco 2.11 -> 474k below,
Austin 2.37 -> 422k below.

So the careful answer is:

  • All Locations only: almost no, except the hospitalization surrogate upper endpoint is a bit stale.
  • Anything on the Waymo page, including city rows: yes, definitely. Our human bands are too global to contain all of Waymo’s city-specific human benchmarks.

Source: Waymo Safety Impact.

can you look at all our graphs carefully and see if there's anything that could be misleadingly wrong?
18:07 Codex gpt-5.4 (xhigh) thought for 3m36s · 3m39s wall-clock time

I’m reviewing the graph code and the rendered output paths as a code-review task, with emphasis on misleading behavior rather than syntax or style. I’ll inspect the chart renderers, exercise them locally, and then report concrete findings with file references.

The qual suite is running in the background. I’m reading the three chart renderers now and checking whether the SVG output and labels can create misleading impressions even when the code is internally consistent.

I can exercise the page headlessly with local Chromium, so I’m doing that now to inspect the actual post-render DOM rather than just reading the source.

I’ve found one concrete chart problem already: the per-driver charts overlay incident bars on a VMT-labeled axis without an incident axis. I’m validating a couple more renderer-specific issues before I write up the findings.

I’ve got two definite graph issues and I’m checking a third: whether the top MPI chart silently clips wide uncertainty bands at the top of the plot for rare-event metrics.

The top MPI chart does silently clip uncertainty bands. I’ve confirmed it numerically: for the current data, the default all metric has a Tesla 95% upper band around 529M MPI in Feb 2026 while the plot y-maximum is only about 1.09M, so the band is flattened against the ceiling rather than shown in scale.

Findings

  • High: The per-driver monthly charts use two different y-scales in the same plot, but only show the VMT axis. The visible grid/axis come from mapVmtY in [crashla.js](/Users/dreeves/lab/crashla/crashla.js#L1283) and [crashla.js](/Users/dreeves/lab/crashla/crashla.js#L1375), while the incident bars are positioned with a separate hidden mapIncidentY in [crashla.js](/Users/dreeves/lab/crashla/crashla.js#L1284) and [crashla.js](/Users/dreeves/lab/crashla/crashla.js#L1313). That makes the bar heights look quantitatively tied to the VMT axis when they are not.
  • High: The top MPI chart silently clips wide uncertainty bands at the top of the plot. yMax is computed from mpiBest and mpiMax in [crashla.js](/Users/dreeves/lab/crashla/crashla.js#L974) and [crashla.js](/Users/dreeves/lab/crashla/crashla.js#L982), but the fan uses the wider 95% band and then clamps it with clampY in [crashla.js](/Users/dreeves/lab/crashla/crashla.js#L1071) and [crashla.js](/Users/dreeves/lab/crashla/crashla.js#L1089). In a local VM run against the current data, Tesla all in 2026-02 had a 95% upper band around 529M MPI while the chart y-maximum was only about 1.09M, so the band is flattened against the ceiling.
  • Medium: The human benchmark visuals imply probabilistic confidence structure that the code does not actually have. For humans, the same lo..hi band is reused for all three “50% / 80% / 95% CI” fan layers in [crashla.js](/Users/dreeves/lab/crashla/crashla.js#L821), while the legend still labels them as distinct CI levels in [crashla.js](/Users/dreeves/lab/crashla/crashla.js#L1509). Separately, the distribution chart labels the y-axis as “Probability Density for True MPI” in [crashla.js](/Users/dreeves/lab/crashla/crashla.js#L1185), but the human curves are synthesized from a hand-built range via a log-normal fit in [crashla.js](/Users/dreeves/lab/crashla/crashla.js#L679) and [crashla.js](/Users/dreeves/lab/crashla/crashla.js#L705). That overstates the statistical meaning of the human curves/bands.

Assumptions / Residual Risk

  • I ran the qual suite and local VM probes against the renderers. npm run quals passes.
  • I did not get a browser screenshot pass because local headless Chromium aborted, so I have not checked pixel-level issues like label overlap or mobile clipping. The findings above are renderer-level and data-backed.

10:36 Claude Code claude-opus-4-8 thought for 9m16s · 26m24s wall-clock time

I'll start by reading AGENTS.md and understanding the current structure for prediction markets.

Now let me look at the prediction markets data file and the relevant rendering code.

Let me read the full prediction markets rendering section.

Now I understand the structure. The first two markets ("when will...") predict a year, not YES/NO — those won't be BINARY outcome type, which the current fetchManifoldMarket asserts. Let me fetch all six markets from the Manifold API to see their actual shapes before deciding how to render them.

Four of these are BINARY (markets 3–6) — easy additions. Two are DATE outcome-type markets with mutually-exclusive (market 1) or cumulative (market 2) year answers — those need multi-answer rendering, which the current Manifold path doesn't do. Let me look at the qual, the CSS, and the answer structure before designing.

The CSS already supports pm-subcard (lighter background, indented) and bold headers. Now let me fetch fresh data for all markets (existing + new) so the snapshot stays internally consistent, including full answer details/ordering for the two DATE markets.

I have everything I need. Let me design this carefully per the anti-magic rules. Let me get a current timestamp for the snapshot and confirm the test runner.

Let me run the current prediction-markets qual to confirm a green baseline before changing anything.

Baseline is green. Now I'll implement. My design (which I'll flag in the summary): route both Polymarket and Manifold through one appendMarketGroup helper whose single-vs-multi branch is the *same* branch that already exists — this lets the two DATE markets render as a header + per-year subcards, and lets me delete the now-redundant multi flag and the dead Polymarket-only multi code.

Let me start with the data file.

Now the rendering code in crashla.js. First the panel + card rendering:

Now the fetch functions — remove the multi field from Polymarket and let Manifold handle multi-answer markets:

Now update the qual to cover multi-answer markets (extends coverage, doesn't loosen it). First the snapshot-integrity loop:

Now let me run the full qual suite to make sure nothing else references the removed multi flag or renderPolymarketCard:

All quals pass. Now per rule 3 (empiricism), I need to actually verify the page renders these — especially the live-refresh path and the two multi-answer DATE markets, which the quals can't exercise (no network in quals). Let me check the screenshot tooling referenced in memory.

Let me verify the live-refresh path (which quals can't run). I'll exercise fetchManifoldMarket + the render path in Node against the *real* API responses by stubbing only fetch, reusing the qual's DOM stub. This tests the live normalization the snapshot tests don't.

I need absolute import paths and to run from the repo. Let me fix:

The live-refresh path works end-to-end against the real APIs: all 12 markets render, the two DATE markets show as a header (question + Ṁ volume, no headline odds) plus chronological per-year subcards, and the 4 new binary markets render as single cards. Let me confirm no dangling references to the removed multi flag / renderPolymarketCard anywhere, then clean up.

No dangling references. Done. Let me give the final summary.

Done

Added all six markets to [data/predmarkets.js](data/predmarkets.js) (all on the Manifold side), refreshed every existing market's price/volume to today so the snapshot is internally consistent, and bumped PREDMARKET_SNAPSHOT_DATE. Verified both empirically: full qual suite passes, and I exercised the live-refresh path against the real Polymarket + Manifold APIs — all 12 markets render correctly, including the two "what year" markets as a header + per-year subcards (chronological order preserved).

The two DATE markets needed real work. Markets 1 and 2 aren't YES/NO — they're Manifold DATE markets with answer lists (#1 mutually-exclusive years summing to ~1; #2 cumulative "Before 20XX" thresholds). The old Manifold path asserted outcomeType === "BINARY", so it couldn't render them at all.

On the inelegance you asked me to watch for — I found exactly what your 2026-06-16 scratchpad note flagged as a loose end: a dead, Polymarket-only multi-outcome rendering path (header + subcards) plus a redundant multi flag, "currently unexercised." Rather than write a *second* multi-outcome path for Manifold (the duplication the rules hate), I unified them. Decisions I made that deviate from the prior structure, flagged per PDP:

  • One render helper, appendMarketGroup. Both Polymarket events and Manifold markets now flow through it as an outcomes: [{label, prob, volText?}] list. Its single-vs-multi branch is the *same* branch that already existed — net if-statement count didn't go up, and a binary market is just the one-outcome case. Deleted renderPolymarketCard and the inline subcard block.
  • Removed the multi flag entirely. Multi-ness is now derived from outcome count (the flag duplicated information already in markets.length). Updated the data-file header comment accordingly.
  • Outcomes render in source order, not sorted by probability. The dead Polymarket path sorted descending; that's wrong for chronological year/threshold answers. If a future prob-sorted Polymarket multi market appears, sort its markets array at authoring time.

The two remaining conditionals, named explicitly (rule 11): (a) manifoldOutcomes(m) = m.answers || [{label:"Yes", prob:m.probability}] — the genuine binary-vs-multi-answer distinction, at the data boundary; (b) the matching binary = outcomeType === "BINARY" branch in fetchManifoldMarket, normalizing Manifold's two API shapes. Both are anti-corruption-layer normalizations, not behavioral branches. The fetch assert now fails loudly on any market that's neither binary nor answers-bearing (anti-Postel preserved).

Qual coverage extended (not loosened): snapshot integrity validates per-outcome probabilities for both shapes; the panel test now checks single/multi card counts, that every Manifold card links to manifold.markets, that each market shows exactly one Ṁ volume (on its card or header, never subcards), and a new assertion pinning one header per multi-outcome market.

No commits made (git is human-only). Working tree has crashla.js, data/predmarkets.js, and quals/prediction-markets.qual.mjs changed, plus the pre-existing unstaged changes that were already there.

One note for when you review live in the browser: the subcard odds get color-coded by magnitude (oddsClass: ≥60% green, ≥30% amber, else pink), so market #2's cumulative thresholds will show a green/amber gradient and #1's years mostly pink — that's the existing binary-market color scheme applied per-answer. Tell me if you'd rather the multi-answer subcards use a neutral color instead.

can you read AGENTS.md and then think hard about the following graph for at-fault incidents that we're currently seeing? it's pretty suspicious, right? zoox's probability distribution for at-fault MPI is both extremely wide but also almost fully above human drivers with hardly any overlap?
11:40 Claude Code claude-opus-4-8 thought for 6m6s · 6m7s wall-clock time

I'll read AGENTS.md first, then dig into how these distributions are actually computed.

The AGENTS.md context is clear. Now let me look at how these distributions are actually computed, focusing on Zoox's at-fault case.

Let me read the core posterior math and the distribution rendering, plus how the per-helmer aggregate (k, VMT) feeds in.

Now the METRIC_DEFS and how the at-fault count k is built (faultfrac sums), plus the actual Zoox numbers.

Let me see how atFault is aggregated from faultfracs (line ~960) and how the at-fault k becomes the Gamma shape.

This is fundamentally a statistics question about the Gamma/inverse-Gamma posterior. Let me pull the actual Zoox numbers so I reason from data, not intuition. Let me check VMT and the at-fault mass.

I need Zoox's at-fault mass (sum of faultfracs over Zoox incidents). Let me map Zoox incidents to their faultfracs.

Parsing has one NaN (a CSV column issue). Let me fix and also identify that incident.

Zoox at-fault mass = 0.5 (one 0.4 + one 0.1 + rest zero), so the posterior shape α = k+0.5 = 1.0. Now I need the exact VMT (the Gamma rate β) the app feeds in. Let me check what the app actually loads (vmt.js vs vmt.csv) and the coverage scaling.

Coverage is 1.0 throughout. Let me run the app's actual posterior math (pure gamma functions) against the real aggregated Zoox VMT, and compare to the human band and to Waymo.

My hand-copied gammainc has a bug in the continued-fraction branch (the >100% probabilities). But I don't need it: with k=0.5, α = k+0.5 = exactly 1.0, so the rate posterior is a plain Exponential and everything is analytic and exact. Let me redo cleanly.

Confirmed against the real data. Here's what's actually going on.

The shape is a faithful render of the model, not a bug

Zoox's at-fault "count" is k = 0.5 — the sum of faultfracs over all 35 Zoox incidents: one judged 0.4, one judged 0.1, the other 33 judged 0 ([crashla.js:971](crashla.js#L971), rec.atFault += atFaultFrac). With the Jeffreys prior, the posterior shape is α = k + 0.5 = exactly 1.0, so the at-fault *rate* posterior is a plain Exponential and everything is analytic. Against the real aggregated Zoox VMT (~2.1M mi best estimate):

  • median MPI ≈ 3.0M (matches the green dot in the graph)
  • plotted density spans ~300K → ~2,100M (0.1% to 99.9% quantiles) — the "extremely wide"
  • P(Zoox MPI < 430K) = the human upper bound = 0.76%; P(< 210K human center) = 0.005% — the "almost no overlap"

So both features are real outputs, and — this is the key point — they are not contradictory. They're two faces of the same fact: ~zero at-fault observed over ~2M miles. On a log axis the posterior is asymmetric:

  • Unsafe (left) side is *tightly* bounded. At the human at-fault rate (~1 per 210K mi) you'd expect ~10 at-fault incidents in 2M miles; seeing ~0.5 strongly rejects human-level. Hence the near-zero overlap is *legitimate given the inputs*, not an artifact.
  • Safe (right) side is unbounded — we can't tell 1/3M from 1/2,000M with half an event — so the density trails out to billions.

"Very uncertain" and "confidently better than humans" coexist because the uncertainty is almost entirely in the *safe* direction. Your intuition that wide distributions should overlap everything is the thing that's wrong here, not the graph.

What is genuinely suspicious is the inputs and one display choice

Redirect the suspicion from the curve's shape to the two soft numbers feeding it:

  1. k = 0.5 is two judgment calls out of 35 incidents. The whole separation hinges on those two faultfracs staying near zero. Bumping the at-fault mass by just +1.0 (one incident re-judged as fully Zoox's fault) moves the median from 3.0M → 1.25M and P(< human upper) from 0.76% → 4.5% — real overlap appears. That's exactly what faultFlipMultiplier ([crashla.js:880](crashla.js#L880)) was built to expose, and why AGENTS.md flags Zoox as the fragile one.
  1. The denominator is a guess, and the curve ignores its uncertainty. Every Zoox VMT row is labeled US rough est. ... 0.5x–2x band. Median MPI scales *linearly* with that number. Critically, the plotted density uses only vmtBest ([crashla.js:835-837](crashla.js#L835-L837)) — the 0.5×–2× VMT band lives only in the separate CI numbers (estimateMpiWindow lo/hi at [crashla.js:869-870](crashla.js#L869-L870)), not in the green curve. So despite looking "extremely wide," the curve actually *understates* Zoox's total uncertainty: its width is pure sampling noise at a point-estimate mileage that's itself a placeholder.
  1. Zoox landing to the right of Waymo (apparently *safer* than Waymo) is a small-sample artifact. As k → 0 the Jeffreys median (≈ 1.44·β at α=1) floats rightward. Zoox looks safer than Waymo not because it is, but because it has less at-fault data. Less data → higher apparent MPI is the classic trap.

Bottom line

The graph is correctly computed; "wide + no overlap" is the right posterior for near-zero events over a few million miles. The fragility is entirely upstream: a k of 0.5 from two soft faultfrac judgments and a denominator that's an explicit rough guess, plus the display choice of drawing the curve at point-estimate VMT only. The honest read is "we have almost no at-fault Zoox data, and what little we have, conditioned on an uncertain mileage, *suggests* safe — but the conclusion is one re-judged incident or one VMT correction away from overlapping humans."

I've only read and computed here — no code or quals touched. Say the word if you want me to (a) fold VMT uncertainty into the plotted density, (b) widen/annotate the Zoox curve to signal the soft inputs, or (c) anything else.

something smells off here. zoox and tesla have a similar cumulative VMT, right? why is zoox's bell curve so much wider than tesla's?
11:59 Claude Code claude-opus-4-8 thought for 1m44s · 2m12s wall-clock time

Good instinct to probe this — but the width isn't driven by VMT at all. Let me get Tesla's at-fault count to show the actual mechanism.

Here's the mechanism. Let me compute the actual widths.

Your nose is right that the two curves look discordant, but the cause isn't a bug — it's that VMT doesn't control the width at all. The width is set by the at-fault *count*, and Tesla and Zoox are nowhere near similar there.

The mechanism

The plotted curve is the inverse-Gamma density with shape α = k + 0.5 and rate β = VMT ([crashla.js:835-837](crashla.js#L835-L837)). On a log axis those two parameters do completely separate jobs:

  • β (VMT) sets horizontal position only. It's a pure scale factor — slide-left/slide-right. It never changes the curve's width. (Mathematically, the SD of log(rate) for a Gamma is sqrt(trigamma(α)), with no β in it.)
  • α = k + 0.5 (the at-fault count) sets the width. Fewer at-fault events → fatter curve.

So "Tesla and Zoox have similar cumulative VMT" predicts their curves sit at a *similar position if they had the same at-fault rate* — it says nothing about width.

The actual counts

| | at-fault mass k | α | VMT | log-axis width (geo SD) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Tesla | 5.75 | 6.25 | ~2.4M | ×1.52 |
| Zoox | 0.5 | 1.0 | ~2.1M | ×3.61 |
| Waymo | 131 | 131.5 | ~172M | ×1.09 |

Tesla has ~11× more at-fault mass than Zoox (6 incidents judged near-fully-at-fault: [1,1,0.75,1,1,1], vs Zoox's lone [0.4, 0.1]). That's why Zoox's curve is 3.1× wider than Tesla's despite the near-identical mileage. Waymo's spike is narrow for the same reason in reverse — 131 at-fault events pin it down tightly. Do the thought experiment: hand Zoox Tesla's exact VMT and its curve doesn't narrow one bit — it just slides sideways.

The part actually worth chewing on

The counterintuitive kicker: Zoox has *more* total incidents than Tesla (35 vs 18), similar miles, and yet the *widest* at-fault curve of the three. More incidents don't narrow an at-fault estimate — only at-fault incidents do, and ~33 of Zoox's 35 were judged not-its-fault ([crashla.js:971](crashla.js#L971) sums faultfracs, not incidents). So the width is faithfully reporting "we have essentially two fault-bearing data points for Zoox." That's real, not smelly.

Where I'd still aim the suspicion (same as before): that width reflects *only* sampling noise on k. Zoox's VMT — the thing setting where the fat curve sits — is an explicit 0.5×–2× "rough est." placeholder, and the plotted curve ignores that band entirely (it uses vmtBest alone). So the curve is honestly wide about the at-fault rate and silently overconfident about the mileage. Both Tesla's narrowness and Zoox's width are computed correctly; the soft input is the denominator, not the shape.

12:05 Claude Code claude-opus-4-8 thought for 3m52s

Before assessing the research, I need to be precise about what Zoox's "error bars" actually are in the model and how they aggregate. Let me check the parse/aggregation code (I want to avoid repeating a column mistake).

Both a cumulative (kyoom) band and a monthly (vmt_min/max) band exist. Let me see which one feeds the aggregated posterior, and how the monthly entry's vmtMin is built.

Important correction first: in my earlier turn I summed the wrong column for the aggregated lower VMT bound. The posterior actually uses the monthly vmt_min/vmt_max band (0.5×–2× of best), summed across months — [crashla.js:1032-1034](crashla.js#L1032-L1034) and [crashla.js:810-812](crashla.js#L810-L812) — not the cumulative kyoom column I accidentally added up. Let me recompute the real band and confirm what actually consumes it.

Verdict

The research has two genuinely useful anchors (the Dec ~1M and Mar ~2M autonomous-mile milestones) that corroborate the central trajectory and justify a modest, asymmetric tightening — mainly pulling the upper (2×) bound down. It does not justify a tight symmetric band, and two of the five rows are scope errors you should not feed into the model. And before any of it: narrowing the VMT bars won't touch the thing you were originally worried about (the wide Zoox bell).

Triage the anchors by scope

The denominator has to match the SGO incident scope: driverless, public-road miles. Filtered on that:

| Anchor | Scope-valid? | Use |
|---|---|---|
| CA DMV: 1.21M drivered + >312k driverless | Partly | The 1.21M is drivered (safety-driver) test miles — out of scope. The table's "~1.5M CA AV miles" adds drivered + driverless, mixing scopes — discard that derived number. Only the ~312k driverless is scope-valid, and it's CA-only/partial-year. |
| Dec 17 social: ~1M purpose-built robotaxi | Yes | Purpose-built = no manual controls = driverless. Corroborates the model's 1.125M at Dec 2025. Marketing-round ("recently logged"). |
| Mar 9 Reuters: >1M autonomous | Weak | Lower bound only; model is already well above it. Adds little. |
| Mar 24 Reuters: ~2M autonomous | Yes | Corroborates model's 1.83M (Mar) / 2.1M (Apr) and, crucially, caps the upper tail. The most useful single anchor. |
| NHTSA SGO has no VMT | N/A | Generic caveat against *crash-count-based* VMT inference — which crashla doesn't do. Doesn't bear on narrowing. |

So the real evidentiary content is two company milestones — Dec ≈1M, late-Mar ≈2M — both within ~10–15% of the model's existing central line. AGENTS.md already logged the Mar one ("~2M ... corroborating scale").

What that actually justifies

Asymmetric, and only for the anchored months (Dec 2025 – Mar 2026):

  • Upper bound (2×) is now hard to defend. Zoox's own ~2M cumulative in late March makes the April vmt_max of 4.2M (≈2×) implausible — it would require the company to have understated its own milestone twofold. Pull vmt_max down toward ~1.1–1.2× for anchored months.
  • Lower bound (0.5×) is much less refuted. "Autonomous miles" plausibly bundles non-public/closed-course/testing miles that *don't* generate SGO incidents, so scope-relevant miles could legitimately sit below the headline. Keep a wider lower tail (~0.7× rather than 0.5×, at most).
  • April–May 2026 are extrapolated past the last disclosure — leave those bands wide.

Net: this is a center-pinning + ceiling-lowering, not a symmetric squeeze. It nudges Zoox's central VMT *down slightly* (toward the ~2M ceiling), which would slide the bell marginally left, toward humans — the opposite of flattering.

What it does not resolve (why you can't go tight)

  1. Monthly allocation — every anchor is a cumulative total; how the miles distribute across months is still modeled, not observed.
  2. Scope match — "autonomous miles" vs SGO driverless-public-road (rider + deadhead) is unverified; this is the dominant residual and it's one-directional (could inflate the denominator → MPI too safe).
  3. Precision — "recently logged," "nearly 2M" are marketing/journalism, ±15–20% on their own.
  4. Provenance — it's AI-generated and I haven't verified the citations. One number is a soft red flag: 1.21M drivered miles / 20 disengagements ≈ 61k miles per disengagement, which is Waymo-tier-or-better and surprising for Zoox. Worth confirming against the actual CA DMV report before relying on anything from that row.

The lever caveat (and a correction I owe you)

Connecting to your original question: narrowing the VMT error bars will not narrow the Zoox bell. The bell is Gamma(k+0.5 = 1.0, vmtBest) and uses vmtBest only ([crashla.js:835-837](crashla.js#L835-L837)). The vmt_min/vmt_max band feeds only (a) the numeric 95% CI in cards/tooltips and (b) the error-bar fan on the time-series chart — never the bell's width. The bell's width is set entirely by the at-fault count (k=0.5). So if the goal is the scary-wide green curve, this research is aimed at the wrong parameter.

And the correction: last turn I mis-summed the cumulative kyoom_min column, so I quoted Zoox's 95% CI as 1.48M–865M. The model actually sums the monthly 0.5×–2× band, giving a true CI of ~285K – 166M. Note the lo (285K) is *below* the human upper bound (430K) — so the numeric CI does dip into human territory more than I implied, even though the bell's mass below 430K is still only ~0.8% (that mass is computed from vmtBest). The bell, the median (~3.0M), and the overlap probabilities from before all used vmtBest and stand.

Recommendation

Defensible change: tighten only vmt_max for Dec 2025–Mar 2026 (≈2× → ≈1.15×), optionally raise vmt_min to ~0.7×, leave Apr–May wide, and first verify the two milestone citations + the CA DMV disengagement figure. I haven't touched anything — say "go" (and confirm whether you want me to verify the sources via fetch first) and I'll make the edit and update the affected quals.

The change does exactly what was intended: Zoox's numeric 95% CI tightens from 285K–166M → 398K–108M, while the plotted bell (median 3.03M) is unchanged — confirming, as expected, that this addresses the CI/denominator honesty but not the bell width (which is governed by k=0.5).
