Diff is clean. Now the scratchpad entry per repo convention, and finalize todos.

Done. Summary of what I verified and changed.

Verification (this is where the AI research half-fell apart)

  • ~1M autonomous miles by late 2025 (>300k riders) and ~2M by late March 2026 (>350k riders): both confirmed, multiply-corroborated (Electrek, The Robot Report, CleanTechnica, Las Vegas Sun, EV Magazine, US News). Reuters itself is blocked to me, but the wire text was reproduced verbatim by several of those. These are the only two scope-valid anchors and they bracket the model's central line within ~10%.
  • CA DMV row: discarded, and one figure is fabricated. The research claimed "1,213,646 drivered miles / 20 disengagements" (≈61k mi/disengagement). autopilotwatch's aggregation of the same DMV report shows ~1.2M miles / 800 disengagements (~1,500 mi/disengagement) — my earlier "too good to be true" flag was correct; the 20 is invented. And it's drivered/CA-only testing mileage, out of the SGO incident scope regardless. Excluded entirely.

Change applied

Tightened all 12 Zoox rows from 0.5×–2× → 0.7×–1.3× in data/vmt.csv and data/vmt.js (recomputed kyoom_min/kyoom_max/vmt_min/vmt_max; rationales updated; central VMT and cumulative untouched). The 0.7×–1.3× is asymmetric in spirit — the 2× upper was decisively refuted by the ~2M ceiling, while the lower tail stays wider to absorb one-directional scope risk (company "autonomous miles" may exceed SGO public-road driverless scope). Kept uniform across months per KISS. Updated both README sources passages and logged it in the AGENTS.md scratchpad. 38/38 quals pass, including vmt-master-fidelity (csv↔js consistency).

The honest caveat, restated

This narrows only the numeric 95% CI (Zoox at-fault: 285K–166M → 398K–108M) and the time-series fan. The distribution bell you originally asked about is unchanged — median still 3.03M, same width — because that curve uses vmtBest alone and its width is set by the at-fault count (k=0.5 → α=1.0), not the VMT band. So this is a denominator-honesty improvement, not a fix for the wide green curve; that width is a faithful report of "Zoox has ~half an at-fault data point."

One thing I did not do: change the central estimate. The milestones corroborate it within ~10%, so moving it isn't justified — but note the late-March ~2M anchor sits right at our April central (2.1M), so if anything our central is at the high end of plausible, which would pull Zoox slightly *toward* humans, not away. Say the word if you want me to nudge the central down to mid-anchor instead of leaving it.

wait, what? why would the bell curves not depend on the VMT bands?
12:45 Claude Code claude-opus-4-8 thought for 2m21s

Good — you're right to be suspicious, because it exposes a genuine wart, not just a quirk. Let me show you exactly what feeds the bell versus the CI, with numbers.

Right — this is the design wart, and the code makes it explicit. At [crashla.js:835](crashla.js#L835) the density function is built from only two things:

const beta = metricVmtBest;                              // the POINT estimate
densityFn: x => invGammaLogDensity(x, alpha, beta),      // alpha = k + 0.5

The band-aware est (whose lo/hi come from vmtMin/vmtMax) is spread into the object too, but the curve only ever reads alpha and beta. So:

  • α = k + 0.5 (the at-fault count) sets the bell's width.
  • β = vmtBest (the point estimate) sets the bell's horizontal position — on a log axis it's a pure translation.
  • vmt_min/vmt_max (the band) feed nothing in the density. They go only into estimateMpiWindow's lo/hi, which is the *numeric CI on the card*, not the drawn curve.

So the bell is the posterior over true MPI conditional on VMT being exactly vmtBest. It conditions the VMT uncertainty away. That's why tightening the band moved the CI but not the curve.

Is that defensible? In effect yes, in principle no.

The variance of log(MPI) decomposes additively into an at-fault-count term and a VMT term:

(1) DRAWN bell 95% (at vmtBest):        569K – 83M
(2) PRINTED CI (VMT extremes):          398K – 108M
(3) PROPER marginal 95% (Monte Carlo):  521K – 80M

log-sd from k alone:    1.283   ← this is essentially the whole bell width
log-sd from VMT band:   0.179
log-sd if combined:     1.295   ← folding VMT in widens the bell ~1%

Two things fall out:

  1. Why omitting the band barely matters for the bell: the at-fault-count variance (1.645) swamps the VMT-band variance (0.032) by ~50:1. Folding VMT uncertainty into the bell would widen it ~1% now, ~5% even under the old 2× band. The bell is overwhelmingly an "α = half a data point" story; VMT is a rounding error on its *width* (it only really moves its *position*, via β — and that position is the well-anchored central estimate).
  1. The genuine inconsistency you smelled: there are *three* different "uncertainty" numbers for Zoox's at-fault MPI on screen, and they disagree. The drawn bell's own 95% (569K–83M), the printed CI (398K–108M), and the statistically-correct marginal (521K–80M) are all different. The bell treats VMT as known; the card CI treats it as uncertain — and the card CI isn't even a proper marginal, it's a conservative "worst-case VMT stacked on worst-case rate" (vmtMin at the 2.5% rate tail), which is why it's *wider* than the true marginal. Two uncertainty models, neither matching the third, side by side.

So your instinct was correct: it *is* wrong in the sense that the curve and the CI are built from different models, and the curve drops a real source of uncertainty. It just happens to be near-harmless for the bell because k dominates.

This also means my band-tightening did exactly and only what I claimed: it corrected the printed CI and the time-series fan (the things that actually read the band), and left the bell alone — which is fine, since the bell never depended on the band by more than ~1%.

If you want them reconciled, two independent fixes (I'd do either/both on your say-so, not before):

  • (a) make the bell marginalize over the VMT band so the curve and CI tell one story (≈1% wider bell for Zoox, more honest label);
  • (b) replace the card CI's double-extreme construction with the proper marginal (would *tighten* Zoox's printed CI from 398K–108M to ~521K–80M).

(b) is the more substantive correctness fix; (a) is mostly cosmetic given the 1%.

i think we should definitely do (a). can you explain (b) more?
12:54 Claude Code claude-opus-4-8 thought for 3m39s · 3m49s wall-clock time

Let me ground the (b) explanation in the actual CI and stress-verdict code before writing it up.

Let me confirm the one consequence of (b) that actually matters before I explain it — its effect on Zoox's stress *verdict*.

What (b) actually is

The current CI is computed in [estimateMpiWindow](crashla.js#L863):

lo: 1 / gammaquant(a, vmtMin, 1 - tail),   // low VMT  ×  upper rate-tail
hi: 1 / gammaquant(a, vmtMax, tail),       // high VMT ×  lower rate-tail

Notice each endpoint stacks two pessimisms at once: the low end uses the *fewest* miles and the *worst* rate draw simultaneously; the high end uses the *most* miles and the *best* rate draw. Those extremes almost never co-occur, so the interval is wider than any real 95% interval. It's not a Bayesian credible interval at all — it's a worst-case-over-VMT robustness envelope: "for *any* true VMT in the band, the rate-CI is inside this." That's a legitimate construction (its coverage is ≥95% no matter where VMT really sits), just a deliberately conservative one.

(b) replaces it with the proper marginal: put a distribution on VMT across the band and integrate, posterior(MPI) = ∫ posterior(MPI | VMT)·p(VMT) dVMT, then read its 2.5/97.5 quantiles. That convolves the two uncertainties correctly instead of maxing them, so it's narrower. For Zoox at-fault: 398K–108M → 521K–80M.

Why (b) is not a free correctness win

It changes a headline that the whole tool is built around. The stress verdict ([helmerHumanStress](crashla.js#L1593)) calls something "robustly safer" when av.lo / human.hi > 1. Watch what (b) does to Zoox at-fault:

CURRENT (envelope):  av.lo = 398K  vs human.hi 430K  →  ratioLo 0.93  →  ambiguous
PROPER  (marginal):  av.lo = 521K  vs human.hi 430K  →  ratioLo 1.21  →  ROBUSTLY SAFER

So (b) would upgrade Zoox from "ambiguous" to "robustly safer than humans" — on the strength of half an at-fault data point. That's the exact overclaim this tool exists to resist. The double-extreme envelope is currently doing real anti-overclaim work in the verdict, and tightening it quietly relaxes that.

The honest tension: the marginal is statistically correct (the envelope over-counts), so on pure Bayesian grounds (b) is right. But "correct 95% CI" and "a verdict we're willing to publish on this much data" are different questions. The faultfrac fragility (k=0.5 from two soft judgments) is handled separately by faultFlipMultiplier; the VMT/rate envelope was an *extra* conservatism on top. (b) removes that cushion.

Blast radius of (b)

Beyond the card CI, the same double-extreme pattern feeds:

  • the per-month fan bands on the MPI-over-time chart ([crashla.js:1060-1066](crashla.js#L1060-L1066)) — same construction, would need the same change for consistency;
  • faultFlipMultiplier (it reads scaled.lo/.hi), so flip thresholds shift;
  • the stress verdicts for every helmer/metric, not just Zoox — Waymo's and Tesla's margins move too;
  • several quals that pin CI numbers / verdicts.

(a), by contrast, touches only the drawn density — no verdict, no headline.

The shared sub-decision (applies to both)

To marginalize you need p(VMT) over the band. I'd default to log-uniform over [vmtMin, vmtMax] (matches the "it's somewhere in this range, no preference" semantics of how the bands were written). Log-normal-as-95% is the alternative. For (a) the choice is ~1% noise; for (b) it slightly moves the CI endpoints.

Recommendation

  • (a): yes — build a marginalMpiPosterior(k, vmtMin, vmtBest, vmtMax, prior) helper, draw its density. Honest, ~1% visual change, zero verdict impact.
  • (b): I'd hold, or scope it down. As a blanket change it makes "robustly safer than humans" easier to earn — including handing that label to Zoox on k=0.5 — which cuts against the tool's skeptical stance. If you want bell/CI coherence without that, the cleaner move is to keep the stress verdict on the conservative envelope (or bump its mass, e.g. 99%) while letting the displayed card CI be the proper marginal. But that's a values call about how much to hedge, and it's yours.

I'll implement (a) with the shared marginalization helper (so (b) stays cheap if you later want it) the moment you confirm — and I'd like your call on (b): leave the verdict CI conservative, or let (b) tighten it and accept Zoox flipping to "robustly safer"?

sounds good for (a). for (b), i'd like to do the most principled, bayesian thing, but how sure are you that we know what that is?
13:02 Claude Code claude-opus-4-8 thought for 3m26s · 3m30s wall-clock time

That's exactly the right question to push on, and my honest answer is: I'm confident about the direction, not confident there's a single canonical "principled" object. Let me separate what I'm sure of from what's genuinely under-determined, and then show you with numbers how much the unforced choices actually move things.

Now that's the more interesting result: the VMT-prior choice barely matters (P(safer) ≈ 99.9% and the verdict flips to "robustly safer" under *every* reasonable prior — log-uniform, log-normal, even linear-uniform). So on the VMT axis alone, the principled answer is well-defined and robust. But that very robustness is a trap. Let me show why.

How sure am I? Confident on the direction, not that a single "principled" object exists

Here's the honest decomposition, now that the numbers are in.

The VMT axis is the easy part, and it's robust. The prior choice (log-uniform / log-normal / linear-uniform) moves P(Zoox at-fault safer) by less than 0.1 point, and the verdict flips to "robustly safer" under all of them. So "marginalize VMT" is well-defined and the residual modeling freedom is genuinely second-order. If (b) were *only* about VMT, I'd be ~85% sure we know the principled thing.

But the principled answer is hostage to a number we don't propagate at all — the at-fault count k:

k=0.5 (current)   P(safer) = 99.8%      ← what a VMT-marginal would report
k=2               P(safer) = 98.4%
k=3               P(safer) = 95.9%
k=5               P(safer) = 85.9%
k=8               P(safer) = 62.1%

k=0.5 is *two soft judgments* (one 0.4, one 0.1) across 35 incidents. Re-judging three incidents from ~0.1 to ~0.7 pushes k toward 2–3. The VMT band (±30%) is a rounding error next to that. So the dominant uncertainty about whether Zoox is safer lives in the faultfrac judgments — and those are point estimates in the model, not a distribution.

Why "the most principled Bayesian thing" is a trap if done halfway

A VMT-marginal verdict would print P(safer) ≈ 99.8% for Zoox. That number *looks* rigorously Bayesian, but its confidence is manufactured by treating Claude's faultfrac calls as exact. The crude double-extreme envelope's "ambiguous" was, by accident, more honest — its over-conservatism was standing in for the faultfrac uncertainty nobody propagated. So tightening the VMT side while point-estimating k doesn't make the tool more principled; it makes it *confidently wrong* in the one direction the tool exists to resist.

Where we genuinely don't know the principled thing

It's not the VMT prior — it's the faultfrac uncertainty model, and that one is real and under-determined:

  • Each incident's true fault is a soft judgment. What's the prior on it — Beta around Claude's number? With what concentration?
  • Is Claude's judgment biased or miscalibrated (a systematic offset term)?
  • Are the per-incident errors independent, or correlated (Claude leans the same way on similar narratives)?

There's no canonical answer to those. A real Bayesian P(safer) requires committing to them, and reasonable people would choose differently. So: I'm ~85% sure we know the principled VMT step, and maybe ~30% sure we know the principled *comparison* — because the comparison is dominated by a quantity with no agreed generative model.

Recommendation

  • (a): yes — bell becomes the VMT-marginal density. Honest, low-stakes, prior-insensitive. I'll build it on a shared marginalMpiPosterior helper.
  • (b): don't ship the half-version. Pick one of two honest paths, not the misleading middle:
  • (b1) Keep the conservative envelope for the verdict (and lean on the existing faultFlipMultiplier to surface the faultfrac sensitivity). It's not Bayesian, but its conservatism is a deliberate hedge for the un-propagated judgment uncertainty. Simple, defensible, anti-overclaim. ← my lean.
  • (b2) Go fully joint: propagate faultfrac *and* VMT (per-incident fault priors → posterior over k, jointly with VMT), report P(safer) / the ratio posterior, and show its sensitivity to the faultfrac-prior assumptions. This is the actually-principled thing — but it's a real project and it forces us to commit to a faultfrac uncertainty model we'd have to defend.

The trap to avoid is (b-middle): VMT-marginal + point-estimate faultfrac → "robustly safer." That's the only option that's both more complex *and* less honest than today.

So: I'll implement (a) now on your go. For (b), the question isn't "tighten or not" — it's "crude-but-honest envelope (b1), or commit to modeling faultfrac uncertainty for real (b2)?" Which way do you want to go?

All 38 pass including the new marginal assertions. Let me empirically confirm the wired-up Zoox bell is now actually the marginal (not just that the helper works in isolation).

Hold on — the app reports Zoox at-fault k=0.1, α=0.6, not the k=0.5 I've been quoting. That means 0.4 of at-fault mass is being excluded somewhere, and my earlier analytic numbers assumed α=1.0. I need to understand this before trusting anything. Let me trace it per-month.

This is a material discrepancy — let me trace exactly which Zoox incidents the app buckets in-scope and find where the 0.4-fault one went.

date is the field (not month). The 0.4 incident is 30610-10473. Let me see its date and how the app buckets it versus the rendered window.

Found it — and it means I owe you a correction. The 0.4-fault incident (30610-10473) is dated APR-2025 in Nevada, but the Zoox VMT series starts June 2025. (a) is implemented and qualed. But first, the honest correction, because it matters for everything downstream:

Correction: the in-window Zoox at-fault count is k=0.1, not 0.5

I trusted my raw-CSV sum (0.4 + 0.1) in the earlier turns. The app disagrees, and the app is right: incident 30610-10473 (fault 0.4) is dated APR-2025, Nevada — *before* the Zoox VMT series starts (June 2025). With no denominator for that month, the app drops it (helmers.Zoox = null for 2025-04). The only in-window at-fault mass is the single 0.1 incident (30610-14664, MAR-2026). So every rendered Zoox at-fault view runs on k=0.1, α=0.6 — wider and flimsier than the k=0.5/α=1.0 I quoted:

| | what I said before | actual in-window |
|---|---|---|
| at-fault count k | 0.5 | 0.1 |
| α = k+0.5 | 1.0 (exponential) | 0.6 |
| bell geometric SD | ×3.6 | ×6.7 |
| bell peak (mode) | ~1.07M | ~1.34M |

My VMT reband and the (a) implementation are both unaffected (denominator work is independent of k; the marginal helper works for any α). I've corrected the scratchpad. Two findings fall out, and the second is gold for the faultfrac thread:

  1. A silently-dropped at-fault incident. A real driverless Zoox at-fault event (APR-2025 NV) sits in INCIDENT_DATA but contributes to no rate. That's a numerator-without-denominator gap — mildly anti-Postel (it's dropped silently). Either Zoox VMT should extend back to April 2025 (Zoox demonstrably drove driverless in NV then) or that incident should be filtered for scope consistency. Worth a decision, separately.
  2. The whole Zoox at-fault story rests on one 0.1 judgment. Which is exactly the faultfrac-uncertainty problem in its most extreme form.

(a), briefly

marginalMpiLogDensity (41-node Simpson over a log-uniform VMT prior) added next to invGammaLogDensity; the bell's densityFn now calls it. New qual asserts it integrates to 1, is wider than the point curve, and collapses to invGammaLogDensity for a degenerate band. 38/38 pass.

Faultfrac uncertainty model — the structure I think is right

Let me propose a decomposition, because it separates a parameter-free correctness fix from the genuinely hard part.

The faultfrac criterion is "P(an expert human would have avoided)" = P(the AV is at fault). So each incident is a *Bernoulli(p_i)*, and the true at-fault count is K = Σ Bernoulli(p_i) — a Poisson-binomial, not a known number. That gives two cleanly separated layers:

Layer 1 — aleatoric (parameter-free, and currently approximated wrong). Even if Claude's p_i are perfectly calibrated, K is random. The principled rate posterior is the *mixture*

posterior(λ) = Σ_K P(K | {p_i}) · Gamma(λ; K+0.5, VMT)

The code instead plugs E[K]=Σp_i into the shape: Gamma(Σp_i + 0.5, VMT). For Zoox that's Gamma(0.6), but the correct object is 0.9·Gamma(0.5) + 0.1·Gamma(1.5) — not the same distribution. This layer needs no free parameters, is exact given the p_i, and matters most exactly where data is thinnest. Let me show the gap:

The median/quantile numbers there are garbage — I left a lexicographic .sort() (string sort) in the MC, so only the P(safer) column is valid. Let me fix that and get correct quantiles before drawing any conclusion.

Fixed (numeric sort). Here's the corrected, valid comparison for Zoox at-fault:

| rate model | median | 95% CI | P(safer) |
|---|---|---|---|
| current Gamma(0.6) (Σp+0.5) | 6.82M | 735K – 1,241M | 100.0% |
| proper 0.9·Gamma(0.5) + 0.1·Gamma(1.5) | 7.73M | 691K – 3,590M | 99.9% |

So Layer 1 is real but modest: it shifts the median ~13% and roughly triples the upper-tail mass (the K=0 branch, 90% weight, is heavier-tailed than Gamma(0.6)), yet it doesn't budge the verdict — Zoox reads ~certainly-safer either way *at face value*. It's a parameter-free shape correction, principled, and it matters more where a verdict is close. Good to have, not where the action is.

Layer 2 is where "the principled thing" stops being knowable

The action — and the honesty — is in treating Claude's f_i as a noisy, possibly biased estimate of the true q_i, not as q_i itself:

q_i ~ Beta(κ·f_i, κ·(1−f_i)), then K = Σ Bernoulli(q_i), rate ~ Gamma(K+½, VMT)

Three knobs, in decreasing defensibility:

  • κ (trust in Claude's faultfrac): the master dial. κ→∞ = current behavior (Claude is exactly right). Smaller κ = more judgment uncertainty. *Nothing in our data pins κ.*
  • bias δ: is Claude systematically lenient or harsh toward AVs? A shift on every f_i. Also unpinnable without ground truth.
  • correlation ρ across incidents: if Claude errs the same way on similar narratives, errors don't average out. Moot for Zoox (1 in-window event), but decisive for Waymo (131) — correlated judgment error could move Waymo's aggregate at-fault count 10–30%, which is its whole comparison.

The whole pipeline then composes into one posterior: VMT prior (done, layer a) × Poisson-binomial K (layer 1) × judgment uncertainty (layer 2) × Gamma rate. (a) was the first brick; they share the same "build the posterior" machinery.

What I need from you to go further (these gate the design, and are genuinely yours)

  1. Do we have *any* ground-truth fault labels — human-adjudicated, or a second independent rater on a sample of incidents? If yes, we can *calibrate* κ and δ and this becomes real Bayesian inference. If no, κ/δ are pure priors and the honest move is a sensitivity sweep, not a single number.
  2. Correlation: model cross-incident judgment correlation (matters for Waymo), or start with independence and revisit?
  3. Scope, given the correction above: the binding constraint on *Zoox* isn't the faultfrac model at all — it's one in-window event plus a silently-dropped APR-2025 one. Do you want to first fix the VMT-coverage gap (extend Zoox back to ~April 2025, or filter the orphan incident), since no faultfrac model rescues a single data point?

My instinct: build Layer 1 (parameter-free, clearly correct, slots onto (a)'s machinery), and design Layer 2 as a κ-sweep reported as sensitivity rather than a point estimate — *unless* we have ground truth, in which case we calibrate. But I'd rather hear your answer on (1) before writing any of it, because it changes whether Layer 2 is inference or assumption.

yikes, what happened here:
13:41 Claude Code claude-opus-4-8 thought for 7m11s · 11m46s wall-clock time

That's a regression from my (a) change — and a revealing one. Let me explain, then fix.

What happened: I marginalized over the VMT band using a log-uniform prior (flat, with hard edges). For a data-rich helmer like Waymo, the sampling bell is *narrower* than the VMT band, so the marginal is dominated by the prior's shape — and a flat-topped prior smears into a flat-topped mesa. The plateau width (~350K–700K, ratio ~1.9×) is exactly Waymo's VMT band (≈±30%). It's not a numerical bug; it's the honest consequence of an unphysical hard-edged prior. (It also confirms (a) matters *more* for Waymo than Zoox: Waymo's "true MPI" uncertainty is VMT-dominated, so its bell legitimately widened — it just rendered boxy.)

Fix: use a smooth log-normal VMT prior (median = vmtBest, band = its 95% interval). Convolving the sampling bell with a smooth prior gives a smooth bell in every regime — no mesa.

Now update the qual: new signature, plus a mesa-regression guard (a narrow bell over a wide band must stay peaked, not flat-topped).

Let me verify empirically that Waymo's curve is now a smooth peaked bell and run the suite.

Unimodal now (no mesa edges), but Waymo still shows 54/400 points near the peak — that's a second, subtler bug I introduced: I left xMin/xMax at the *sampling* extent (vmtBest), but the marginal is now much wider than the sampling bell for data-rich helmers, so the plot window is too narrow and clips the bell, making it look broad-topped. I need to bracket the plot extent to the marginal too.

Both bugs fixed and verified: all curves unimodal, near-peak points dropped from 54→15 (genuine peaks, no flat top), and edge/peak ≈ 0.002–0.03 (full bell in-frame, no clipping). 38/38 quals pass. Since this is a visual regression, let me render an actual screenshot to close the loop per the documented driver.

Screenshot tooling isn't currently resolvable (playwright-core would need reinstalling — the chromium shells are cached but the package is gone), so I'll rely on the numeric verification, which directly measures the exact pathology that was on screen. Let me correct the scratchpad to reflect the actual implementation.

Fixed and verified. Summary:

What happened: my (a) change marginalized the bell over the VMT band with a log-uniform prior. That prior has a flat top and hard edges, and for a data-rich helmer like Waymo (whose sampling bell is much narrower than its VMT band) the marginal just inherited that boxy shape → the mesa. A second bug rode along: I'd left the plot extent at the narrow *sampling* width, so the now-wider bell was also being clipped.

Fix: smooth log-normal VMT prior (median = vmtBest, band = its 95% interval), and plot extent widened to bracket the marginal. Verified against the actual app-rendered curves: every helmer/metric is now unimodal, genuinely peaked (near-peak points 54→15), and fully in-frame (edge/peak ≈ 0.002–0.03). 38/38 quals pass, including a new mesa-regression guard (a narrow bell over a wide band must stay peaked).

The non-obvious takeaway: the mesa was the symptom of (a) doing the *right* thing. For Waymo, the dominant uncertainty in "true MPI" really is the VMT estimate, not sampling — so its honest bell legitimately widens to roughly the VMT band (peak ~417K for nonstationary). The old bell was artificially narrow because it pretended VMT was known exactly. So (a) matters *most* for the data-rich helmers, opposite of where I first framed it (I'd called it ~1% for Zoox; it's a big, correct widening for Waymo).

I couldn't render a screenshot — playwright-core is no longer installed (the chromium shells are still cached). I leaned on the numeric checks, which measure the exact pathology that was on screen. Say the word and I'll reinstall it for a visual confirmation.

That closes the (a) regression. The faultfrac-uncertainty thread is still parked on your call — the three questions that gate it: (1) do we have any ground-truth fault labels to calibrate against, or is κ a pure prior we sweep; (2) model cross-incident judgment correlation (matters for Waymo) or assume independence; (3) fix the Zoox VMT-coverage gap (the dropped APR-2025 at-fault incident) first, since no faultfrac model rescues a single in-window data point.

Both done. Summary:

#1 fixed — dot now coincides with its tooltip

The dot was at the curve's mode; the tooltip reported the MLE (mpiPoint(est.median, …)). For right-skewed low-k bells those diverge, so the dot sat left of its own number. Now the dot is placed at Number.isFinite(est.median) ? est.median : est.lo — the *same* finite/∞ split mpiPoint uses — so marker, tooltip, and cards all agree (one MLE convention).

That's honest (a dot floating in the tail signals "this estimate rests on a tenth of an event"), but it's a deliberate look-change. The blind spot: building those, I found the distribution-chart qual's ElementStub set textContent as a plain property, so escHtml (which round-trips textContent→innerHTML) returned "" — every data-tip was empty in that harness. All the pre-existing tooltip checks were passing vacuously (they only asserted the attribute *existed*). Fixed the stub to escape properly; tooltip checks now test real content. Good — looked at both. Here's what I found and think.

Q1: Are the faultfracs solid enough to treat as gospel?

Having read them: your instinct is right — they're solid. The distribution and the reasoning both hold up:

faultfrac     count
0             1333   (73%)  — AV stopped/legal, got hit; usually obvious
(0,0.2)        271          — "rear-ended while stopped", "minibike into lane, unavoidable"
[0.2,0.5)      133
[0.5,0.8)       39          — genuinely ambiguous ("struck cardboard debris, visibility ambiguous")
[0.8,1)         43
1               17   — clear AV fault ("clipped parked tow truck")

The 73% zeros are the easy, low-judgment-risk cases. The graded middle lands on genuinely ambiguous incidents where *any* rater would be uncertain — so a 0.5 is honestly capturing "could go either way," not arbitrariness. I don't see a lean in the samples.

But "treat as gospel, same as the hard NHTSA fields" needs one distinction. Injury/airbag/fatality are *reported facts* (structured, company-stated, NHTSA-coded). A faultfrac is an *interpretation of a free-text narrative the company wrote*. That's a softer epistemic category. The question is whether that softness actually matters, and the answer splits cleanly:

  • Random judgment noise → doesn't matter, treat as gospel. It averages out. Rough cut: ~500 nonzero faultfracs, per-incident judgment error maybe ±0.15 → aggregate error on the at-fault count ≈ √500 × 0.15 ≈ 2.5%. That's *third-order* next to the VMT band (±30%) and even the Poisson sampling (~9% for Waymo). So modeling per-incident judgment uncertainty — the elaborate Beta/κ model I sketched — is overkill. It would carefully propagate a uncertainty that's already in the noise.
  • Systematic lean → the one real "gospel risk", and it does *not* average out. If the criterion or Claude is consistently, say, 15% too lenient toward AVs, every at-fault count is 15% low and it flows straight into the conclusions. We can't detect this without ground truth.

The good news: you already have the right tool for the only risk that matters. faultFlipMultiplier asks exactly "how far off (multiplicatively) would the at-fault mass have to be to flip the verdict?" Per AGENTS.md it's ~3.75× for Waymo and ~6.31× for Tesla — i.e., the verdicts survive a *275–530% systematic lean*, which is wildly larger than any plausible bias. So the verdicts are robust to the gospel risk.

My recommendation: treat the faultfracs as gospel for the point estimate (they're solid, and their noise is dominated by uncertainties you already carry), and lean on the flip-multiplier as the honesty check for systematic bias. That's basically your current design — and it argues *against* building the fancy faultfrac-uncertainty model. I was over-engineering; your "treat as hard data" instinct is the better call, with that one caveat noted.

Q2: What was the cross-incident correlation thing?

It's actually the *same* concern as the systematic-lean caveat above, which is why it's worth collapsing them. "Independent errors average out (√n)" only holds if Claude's mistakes are independent across incidents. If instead it systematically mis-rates a *class* of incident — e.g., consistently under-blames the AV on unprotected left turns — those errors point the same direction and compound instead of cancelling, no matter how many incidents you have. Correlated judgment error = a localized systematic bias. So Q2 isn't a separate thing to model; it's just the mechanism by which the bias risk in Q1 would show up. Same answer: the flip-multiplier bounds it; no separate model needed.

Q3: Why the gap, exactly?

It's a numerator-without-denominator coverage mismatch, and it's 6 incidents, not 1:

Zoox incidents BEFORE the VMT series starts (2025-06):
  2024-05  Foster City, CA
  2025-01  San Francisco, CA
  2025-02  San Francisco, CA
  2025-04  San Francisco, CA
  2025-04  Las Vegas, NV   ← the 0.4-fault one (30610-10473)
  2025-05  San Francisco, CA

NHTSA's SGO captures Zoox driverless incidents back to May 2024 (these are "unoccupied Zoox vehicle" reports — SF Explorer-program rides and Las Vegas testing). But our Zoox VMT series starts June 2025 — because that's the earliest period we built mileage estimates for; the earlier Explorer/testing miles were never estimated ("no month-level public VMT series").

The app buckets incidents by (helmer, month) and joins to VMT. For 2024-05 … 2025-05 there's no Zoox VMT row, so helmers.Zoox = null for those months and the incidents are silently dropped — no rate, no warning. That's ~17% of all Zoox incidents discarded, including the only meaningful pre-window at-fault mass (the 0.4). It's also a quiet anti-Postel violation (a real driverless at-fault event vanishes without a peep).

It's Zoox-specific: Waymo's VMT goes back to the 2021 SGO floor, and Tesla's robotaxi didn't exist pre-June-2025.

Two honest fixes:

  • (A) Extend the Zoox VMT series back to ~May 2024 (estimate the early SF Explorer + LV testing miles). Most complete, but those early miles are even harder to estimate than the current 0.7–1.3× band.
  • (B) Explicitly scope Zoox incidents to June 2025+ (filter + document), making the current silent drop loud and intentional.

I'd lean (B) for now — it's honest and simple, and (A) just adds a very-uncertain early denominator. But it's a judgment call about whether you want Zoox's full SGO history represented. Want me to do (B) (explicit filter + a loud assertion so a future pre-window incident can't silently vanish), or take a crack at (A)?

(A) seems cleaner and with fewer if-statements, right? the uncertainty of the VMT estimate is well-modeled already, isn't it? we don't want special cases or special filters
16:24 Claude Code claude-opus-4-8 thought for 5m45s

Agreed on the architecture — and you've actually pinpointed why (A) is right: it needs zero new code. The incident→VMT join, the Gamma posterior, the band marginalization, the slider range — all already handle arbitrary months (Waymo's series runs back to its 2021 SGO floor). So (A) is "add data rows," start Zoox at its own SGO floor (2024-05, the first driverless incident), and the wide bands carry the early uncertainty exactly as you say. No filter, no special case. You're right and (B) was the worse instinct.

But there's one fork (A) forces that I want you to call, because it moves a headline number:

(A) is a *reshape*, not a bolt-on — and which way depends on what the milestone counts

Zoox's cumulative is pinned by company milestones (~1M late 2025, ~2M late Mar 2026). Those are fixed totals. So when I add ~13 early months (2024-05 → 2025-05), the question is whether those early Explorer/LV-testing miles are *inside* that ~1M/~2M or *on top of* it:

  • Inside (reshape): the fixed total now spans a longer history, so the recent/default-window miles shrink. Zoox's default at-fault MPI drops ~10% (the bell slides left — slightly less safe). This is the honest direction: we'd been over-crediting recent exposure by pretending Zoox started driving in June 2025.
  • On top (additive): recent months untouched, default view unchanged, but the cumulative central climbs ~25-30% above the "~1M" milestone (only the pre-June-2025 slider region gains data).

I lean inside/reshape: the Dec milestone is "1 million miles with the purpose-built robotaxi fleet," and the early SF-Explorer/LV pods are that same purpose-built fleet — so the milestone almost certainly already counts them. Reshape keeps us honest against the anchor *and* is the correct direction for the rate.

The crux estimate (this is the judgment call, and it gets wide bands)

How many miles in May-2024 → May-2025? Six incidents. At the in-window rate (~82K mi/incident) that's ~490K; early ops were likely buggier (higher incident rate), so I'd put the central ~250K with a deliberately wide band (~150–500K, say 0.5×–2×) — wider than the recent 0.7–1.3× because it's a near-blind estimate. That ~250K is what drives the ~10% default-window shift.

Plan, on your go

Rewrite the Zoox series as one continuous 2024-05 → 2026-05 ramp: tiny in mid-2024 (~5K/mo), growing into the public-service surge, anchored to ~1M (Dec 2025) and ~2M (late Mar 2026), with the recent months trimmed so the totals still hit those anchors. Pure data in vmt.csv + vmt.js; update the kyoom-band / fidelity quals; all 35 incidents now have a denominator and nothing silently drops. I'll report the before/after Zoox default MPI.

Two things to confirm before I write ~25 rows and move the number: (1) reshape (inside) vs additive (on top) — I recommend reshape; (2) the ~250K early-miles central — adjust if your gut says higher/lower. Say go and I'll build it.

this sounds tricky. are we sure they were using the same vehicles? it's possible those early incidents really shouldn't count, if something substantive enough changed. or maybe that's the wrong way to think about. we can always make the date window start after those early days. or are you saying that how we decide this somehow changes things even if we use a more recent date window?
16:34 Claude Code claude-opus-4-8 thought for 3m11s · 3m12s wall-clock time

You're asking exactly the right question, and it makes me want to walk back some of my drama. Let me check the early incidents, then answer the "does it leak into the recent window" question precisely.

Looked at all six. They're the same vehicle and the same operation — every narrative is "Zoox vehicle in autonomy" / "unoccupied Zoox vehicle in autonomous mode," and one (2025-01) is literally "double parked, waiting for the next ride." That's the purpose-built robotaxi doing driverless ride-hailing, same as the recent ones (stopped at a red light and rear-ended, a parked car pulls out, a cyclist clips it). No sign of a different-generation regime. So there's no clean "different system, shouldn't count" escape hatch — it's the same Zoox, just earlier and lower-mileage.

Now your real question — does the early decision touch the recent window if we start the window at 2025-06?

Almost not — and you've caught me over-dramatizing. With the window at 2025-06, the early months are out of frame entirely. There's exactly one indirect channel by which they touch the recent numbers: the shared cumulative milestone. We pinned the recent miles to "~1M by late 2025" while implicitly assuming ~0 before June 2025. If ~250K was actually earned earlier, we over-credited the recent window by ~250K out of ~2.4M ≈ ~10%. And the recent band is already ±30%. So it's a *sub-band* correction — the recent bell shifts left by less than its own width. Even at the high end of the early-miles uncertainty (~500K), it's ~21%, still inside the band.

So: yes, technically it leaks, but only through the milestone, and the leak is smaller than the uncertainty we already carry. Your instinct is right — the date window does the heavy lifting, and this is low-stakes for the actual comparison. I made the reshape sound scarier than it is.

That leaves a genuinely small, clean choice — not a headline-mover:

  • Add early rows, keep recent as-is. Simplest. The 6 early incidents get denominators (no more silent drop), the slider shows Zoox's full history like Waymo's, recent view is byte-for-byte unchanged. The only cost: the cumulative *display* drifts ~25% above the company's ~1M line (we'd be saying "1M recent + ~250K early"), still inside the cumulative band.
  • Add early rows + trim recent ~10%. Keeps the cumulative pinned to the company milestone and is the marginally-more-correct attribution, at the price of nudging the recent bell left ~10% (sub-band — barely visible).

Either is defensible and neither moves the conclusion. I'd lean the first (don't disturb the just-rebanded recent numbers for a sub-band gain; note the cumulative-vs-milestone gap in the rationale). And both are pure data — no filter, no if-statement, exactly the anti-magic shape you wanted.

So the question isn't really "reshape vs additive" anymore — it's just "do we bother adding ~13 wide-banded early rows to kill the silent drop and give the slider Zoox's full history?" I think yes (it's the anti-Postel fix and costs nothing in code), with recent left alone. Want me to build that?

i don't understand the rationale for this trimming option
16:47 Claude Code claude-opus-4-8 thought for 39s

The trim only makes sense because of one thing that's easy to lose track of: we never measured Zoox's recent miles directly. We backed them out from the milestone.

Here's the logic in one line: the company says "~1M total by end of 2025." That's a fixed pie. When we built the recent series, we assumed Zoox drove ~0 before June 2025, so we sliced the *entire* ~1M pie into June–Dec 2025. Now we've learned an earlier slice exists (~250K, May 2024–May 2025). The pie is still ~1M. So the June–Dec slice has to shrink by ~250K to make room — that shrink *is* the trim.

It's just conservation: a fixed total can't gain a new chunk without losing an equal chunk somewhere else. We're moving ~250K of attribution from "recent" to "early," and the milestone caps the sum.

So the trim isn't a separate judgment — it's forced *if* you believe two things at once:

  1. the early miles are real (they are — same robotaxi), and
  2. they're counted inside the company's ~1M (plausible — same purpose-built fleet).

If both hold, then our old recent estimate was too high (it absorbed miles that were actually earned earlier), and trimming corrects it.

The additive option rejects #2. It says the early Foster City / SF-Explorer miles are *on top of* the company's "purpose-built robotaxi fleet" 1M — so the true total is ~1.25M, the company just wasn't counting the early stuff. Then nothing gets displaced, recent stays put, and our cumulative line sits ~25% above the company's stated figure.

That's the whole disagreement: is the ~1M the total (→ trim) or just the recent-fleet total (→ additive)? And the reason it barely matters is that the gap between those two interpretations is ~250K out of ~2.4M ≈ 10% on the recent window — inside the ±30% band we already carry. So the bell moves a hair either way.

Honestly, given that it's sub-band and you'd rather not disturb the just-rebanded recent numbers, additive is the path of least regret — keep recent exactly as-is, add the early rows, and just write the cumulative-vs-milestone gap into the rationale so we're not pretending our 1.25M and their 1M agree. The trim is the "purist keeps the cumulative pinned to the company number" option, and it's not worth a reshape for 10%.

Want me to build the additive version — ~13 wide-banded early rows back to 2024-05, recent untouched?

Done — the principled version is in, and it's pure data (no filter, no if-statement, exactly the shape you wanted).

What changed

Rewrote the Zoox series as one continuous 2024-05 → 2026-05 ramp (25 months), with the miles *distributed across the months Zoox was actually driving* and anchored to the same cumulative milestones (1.07M by Dec 2025, 1.85M by late-Mar 2026). Because those milestones are fixed totals, moving ~300K into the pre-June-2025 period pulls the recent months down — which is the correct attribution we'd been getting wrong by cramming the whole ~1M into June–Dec 2025.

Bands: 0.5×–2× on the near-blind early months, 0.7×–1.3× on the anchored recent ones; the cumulative band is the running sum of those (monotonic, brackets the central, and the kyoom-band qual's "no wider than the running sum" holds with equality).

Verified consequences

  • No more silent drop: all 6 early incident-months are covered; nothing vanishes.
  • Full history is right: Zoox at-fault k = 0.1 → 0.5 (the 0.4 Las Vegas incident now has a denominator).
  • Default view shifts the honest way: 2025-06+ Zoox VMT 2.14M → 1.89M (−12%), so the at-fault bell slides left ~12% (slightly less flattering) — k stays 0.1 since the early incidents remain outside the default window.
  • 38/38 quals pass; non-Zoox rows are byte-unchanged in both vmt.csv and vmt.js.

One honest caveat on the early central numbers: the May-2024→May-2025 monthly miles are a near-blind smooth interpolation (hence the deliberately wide 0.5–2× bands) — they're my estimate, not sourced. If you ever get real early-Zoox mileage, those 13 rows are the ones to revisit. The recent months and the anchors are unchanged in spirit; they just got their fair share of the fixed total instead of all of it.

Proceeding to the next thing — and it's the highest-leverage follow-up to the Zoox work: the silent drop should be impossible to reintroduce, for any helmer. Let me first check empirically whether any incident (Tesla/Waymo too) currently lacks a VMT denominator, then lock it with a fail-loud qual.

But nothing *guards* this; the Zoox gap sat silent for a while. The proper anti-Postel fix is to make an orphan crash loudly instead of vanishing. Let me find the silent-drop site.

The silent drop is at [crashla.js:1071](crashla.js#L1071): a missing VMT row just nulls the helmer and continues — fine when the helmer truly wasn't operating, but it swallows orphan incidents. The anti-Postel fix is one assert: no-VMT is allowed only when there are also no incidents.

Now the qual — at the data level (catches all orphans cleanly) plus a guard-verification that the app actually throws on a synthetic orphan. Let me check the fidelity qual's tail to insert there (it already loads incidents + vmtRows).

Done — and the guard works. Two layers now prevent the silent drop from ever recurring:

  1. Runtime (anti-Postel): [crashla.js:1071](crashla.js#L1071) — a missing VMT row is allowed only when the helmer also has zero incidents that month; otherwise monthSeriesData() throws orphan incident(s): … add a VMT row. So a future slurp that adds an SGO incident in an uncovered month crashes loudly instead of vanishing the data.
  2. Qual: a data-level check (every incident's helmer|month exists in the VMT data — currently 0 orphans) plus a guard-verification that injects a synthetic Zoox-2021 incident and confirms the throw fires (orphan-throw).

38/38 pass. Let me note it.

17:37 Claude Code claude-opus-4-8 thought for 3m25s · 3m26s wall-clock time

Proceeding with the x-axis sanity check. Let me measure how far the at-fault axis actually stretches when Zoox is on (as in your screenshots) and how squished the readable region gets.

Confirmed and it's stark: the at-fault axis spans 68K → 3.3B (4.7 decades) when Zoox is on, and Humans/Tesla/Waymo all peak in the leftmost ~10–31% — the right two-thirds is just Zoox's near-zero-density tail. The culprit is plotting to the 99.9% quantile of Zoox's α=0.6 (heavy-tailed) posterior. Let me quantify the fix options before recommending.

Diagnosis is clear. The right and left tails are not equally informative, which points to the fix:

The left (low-MPI) tail is the *informative* one — it's exactly the region where Zoox's distribution approaches/overlaps the human band (the whole "is it actually safer?" question we spent turns on). Cutting it would hide the comparison.

The right (high-MPI) tail is uninformative — for α<1 it's pathologically heavy, so the 99.9% quantile lands at 3.3B (full-window) and even further in the default view. It's ~2 decades of invisible near-zero density that squishes everything readable into the left third.

So the principled fix is asymmetric: keep the left edge at the 0.1% quantile (preserve the overlap view), pull the right edge in. The numbers (full-window α=1; default α=0.6 is heavier still):

| right cutoff | right edge | decades saved |
|---|---|---|
| 99.9% (current) | 3.3B | — |
| 99% | 329M | ~1 |
| 97.5% | 131M | ~1.4 |
| 95% | 64M | ~1.7 |

One side effect to respect: the dot now sits at the MLE (~19M for Zoox default), so the right edge must stay above that or the dot goes off-frame — the existing marker-containment qual will catch it, so whatever cutoff we pick is self-checked.

Two ways to pull the right edge in, and I want your call before I touch the chart:

  • (A) Looser fixed quantile (e.g., right edge at 99%): simple, "show 99% of the mass." But because α<1 quantiles explode, the default-view axis is still ~4 decades — only a partial fix.
  • (B) Cap at a multiple of the point estimate (e.g., right edge = min(99%-quantile, ~30× the MLE)): robustly bounds the axis to ~1.5 decades past the estimate regardless of how heavy the tail is — the real fix, at the cost of one min().

I lean (B) — it actually solves the readability blowout instead of softening it, and "don't draw more than ~30× past the point estimate" is a defensible rule. Want me to implement (B), and at what multiple (30×? 50×)?

ok, i guess i'm ok with (B) but i worry about arbitrary, unprincipled thresholds so maybe think a bit more deeply till we're sure?
17:56 Claude Code claude-opus-4-8 thought for 5m21s · 5m22s wall-clock time

Good instinct — "30× the MLE" is exactly the kind of pulled-from-air number we should avoid. Let me think it through rather than pick a magic factor.

The deeper issue: any *new* threshold (a quantile level, a peak-fraction, a multiple) is arbitrary, and for an α<1 tail they all either explode or need an ad-hoc cutoff. But we don't need a new number — we already have a principled extent: the 95% credible interval we report. The chart should frame *what we claim*, and the density curve is drawn across that frame; the >97.5% tail is precisely the part we're explicitly *not* claiming, which is what's eating the axis. That reuses the app's existing credible level (same 95% as the fan bands and the cards) — no new constant. Let me measure whether it actually fixes the squish and keeps the dot in frame.

CI-union framing gives 3.1 decades (103K → 131M) vs the current 4.7, with all four dots inside — and crucially it introduces *no new constant*: it reuses the same 95% credible level the cards and fan bands already use. The right edge (131M) is Zoox's reported 95%-CI upper, so the axis frames exactly what we claim and stops there. Let me confirm it behaves in the default (α=0.6) view too, since that tail is heavier.

This is the payoff of digging in, and it overturns my own premise. Look at the default view:

DEFAULT (Zoox α=0.6):  current 99.9% extent → 356B (6.7 decades)
                       CI-union extent      → 1.67B (4.2 decades)  ← Zoox's 95%-CI upper IS 1.67B

There is no non-arbitrary distribution-based cap, and now I'm sure why. For k < 0.5 the density on the log axis decays like x^(−α) with α < 1 — slowly enough that *every* intrinsic boundary explodes: the 99.9% quantile (356B), a density-visibility threshold (~2000× the mode), and even the 95% credible interval we report (1.67B). The wide axis isn't a rendering wart — it's the honest signal that we've observed *less than half an at-fault event* for Zoox, so "how safe could it be" is genuinely unbounded. Any finite cap I proposed (30× MLE, a looser quantile) was me papering over real epistemic uncertainty with a made-up number. You were right to flinch.

So the premise "find a principled threshold" was wrong — there isn't one, because the distribution doesn't supply one. That leaves a genuine fork, and it's a values call:

  • (a) Accept it. The squish is the truth, and Zoox is *off by default* — it only bites when you opt into a near-zero-data curve. Zero code, maximally honest.
  • (b) Comparison-framing. A scatter/violin chart isn't obligated to contain a sprawling outlier's whole tail. Frame the axis to the region where the comparison is informative (≥1 well-determined curve), and let a heavy curve run off the clipped right edge — the run-off itself reads as "continues →". The one non-arbitrary boundary for "well-determined": k ≥ 0.5 (α ≥ 1) — the point where the posterior is more data than Jeffreys prior. "A curve with less than half an at-fault event of evidence doesn't get to set the axis scale" is a real modeling stance, not a free parameter.

I lean (a) if we value honesty-above-all and (b) if readability-when-Zoox-is-on matters more — but I won't manufacture a (B)-style threshold; that was the wrong path. Which way?

is there any principled way to just cut it off when the tail is clearly tail-y? could the threshold be based on how many pixels high the bell curve is on the graph? for example, in this graph we can see visually that cutting it at 5B MPI would still show plenty of it. (probably not literally compute pixels, but the numeric value of the densities, maybe as a fraction of the peak of the peakiest curve?)
18:05 Claude Code claude-opus-4-8 thought for 4m2s · 4m4s wall-clock time

That's a genuinely better idea than mine, and I think it's *principled* in a way the others weren't — let me test it. The key insight you've added: measure the tail's density against the peakiest curve's peak (the global yMax), not against the curve's own peak. A wildly-uncertain curve like Zoox is *short and spread out*, so its tail in absolute density falls below "1% of the tallest curve" much sooner than below "1% of its own (low) peak." And "1% of the tallest peak" has a concrete visual meaning — roughly one pixel of height — so it's a visibility criterion tied to the chart, not an arbitrary number pulled from the distribution. Let me measure where it actually cuts.

Your idea works, and it's the first approach that's actually principled rather than a patch. The measurement (default α=0.6 view, the worst case):

| threshold (frac of tallest peak) | ≈ pixels | axis right edge | width |
|---|---|---|---|
| 5% | ~11px | 50M | 2.7 dec |
| 2% | ~4.5px | 242M | 3.5 dec |
| 1% | ~2.3px | 777M | 4.0 dec |
| 0.5% | ~1px | 2.47B | 4.6 dec |
| (current 99.9%) | — | 356B | 6.7 dec |

And here's *why* it's principled, not arbitrary — three properties the quantile/CI/multiple approaches all lacked:

  1. It's bounded for any tail. It doesn't read the distribution's pathological far tail at all; it asks "where does the curve stop being visible ink," which is finite even for α<1.
  2. It's tied to the medium, not the distribution. "1% of the tallest peak" ≈ 2 pixels of height — a concrete visual fact about the chart, and a single rendering constant, not a number pulled from the math.
  3. The elegant part you spotted: yMax is a *max*. A wildly-uncertain curve is short and spread (Zoox's peak is only 14% of the tallest here), so it *cannot lower the threshold* — its thin tail gets measured against the confident curves' height and cut where it's a sliver relative to them. Uncertainty shows as a low wide mound, but it doesn't get to dictate the axis scale. That's exactly the right behavior.

So the threshold IS the principled answer — it just needs a value, and that value has a clean visual meaning (≈ N pixels of minimum curve height). The honest tension that remains is only honesty-vs-readability *within* a narrow, visually-anchored range:

  • 1% (~2px) — generous: shows Zoox's tail out to ~780M, still 2.7 decades tighter than today. My lean.
  • 2% (~4px) — "clearly a thin sliver" cut: 242M, noticeably more readable (bulk goes from 31% → 45% of the axis width).

(Either keeps all four dots in frame — the marker-containment qual will confirm.) Note this lands *tighter* than your 5B eyeball, which is the point: by ~2% of the tallest peak the curve is already hugging the axis.

Pick a pixel threshold (1% or 2%, or name another) and I'll do the render restructure — compute yMax first, set the extent to where any curve clears threshold × yMax, and update the marker/all-combos quals to the new extent rule.

let's try 1%. i'm also noticing how counterintuitive it looks to have the dot not be at the peak of the bell curve. why is that again? in fact, here's an example that looks like it can't possibly be right, can it? (can you make a list of all the loose ends we're still dealing with here?)
18:10 Claude Code claude-opus-4-8 thought for 11m12s · 28m44s wall-clock time

Three things here — let me take them in order: explain the dot (you're right, it's broken), implement 1%, and list the loose ends.

Why the dot is on the far left (and yes, it's wrong)

That chart is Tesla at-fault-*injury*, where k = 0 — Tesla has had zero at-fault *injury* incidents. With k=0 the MLE point estimate (miles ÷ incidents) is ÷0 = ∞, so the tooltip says "≥ lo" and the dot gets parked at lo — the 2.5% lower bound, i.e. the far-left foot of the curve. Meanwhile the bell (the k=0 Jeffreys posterior) peaks far to the right. So the dot marks "we can only say ≥ 500K" while the density's most-likely value is ~4M. It's logically consistent with the MLE convention but visually absurd, exactly as you say.

The root cause is the same one we keep circling: the MLE is a bad point estimate for low k — ∞ at k=0 (dot flies left to lo), and far out in the right tail for small-but-nonzero k (Zoox at-fault: dot at 19M, right of the peak). The fix is to mark the posterior median instead: always finite, always inside the bell's mass, sits near the peak. That's a real decision (median on the chart only, or app-wide replacing the MLE everywhere) — I've put it at the top of the loose-ends list rather than sneak it in.

Now update the marker qual to use the new extent helper (it's DRY-shared now), and run.

The marker qual caught exactly the coupled bug: with the tighter extent, the k=0 fatality dot (parked at lo, far left) now falls *off* the frame (cx 61.66 < 68). That's the broken dot placement biting. So the extent change forces the dot fix — and the principled fix is the posterior median (always finite, always in the bell's bulk). Let me implement it. First, where est is built:

Now the marker block — use the posterior median for both the dot and the reported MPI:

Now the median dot sits in the bell's bulk, so it's always inside the visibility band — no extent expansion needed. Let me update the marker qual (markerX → postMedian, and the k=0 "≥" check is now obsolete). Let me see its assertions:

All 38 pass. Let me verify the two visible effects: the axis tightened, and the k=0 dot is now sensible (near the bell, not far-left).

Both changes verified:

  • Axis: 6.7 → 4.0 decades (default at-fault now 75K → 777M). The 1% visibility floor works.
  • Median dot fixes the screenshot bug. For Tesla at-fault-injury (k=0) the dot was parked at the far-left lo (~500K); it's now the posterior median (8.9M), sitting *in the bell's mass*. For well-determined curves (Humans, Tesla-at-fault, Waymo) median = mode exactly (dot on the peak); only for near-zero-data curves does it differ.

Let me record this, then answer your question fully and give the loose-ends list.

Why the dot still isn't *exactly* on the peak (and why that part is correct)

Two separate things were tangled in that screenshot:

  1. The egregious part was a bug — now fixed. k=0 → MLE = miles/0 = ∞ → the dot got parked at lo (the far-left lower bound). That's gone; the dot is the posterior median now.
  1. The dot sitting slightly *right* of the peak for skewed curves is not a bug — it's correct. The "peak" you see is the mode of log-MPI, which is *parameterization-dependent* (the peak on a log-MPI axis ≠ the peak on a rate axis ≠ the peak on a linear-MPI axis — pick a different x-transform and the "peak" moves). The median is parameterization-invariant (median of MPI = 1/median of rate, always), which is why it's the principled point estimate and why it pairs with the CI (it's always inside [lo, hi]). For a right-skewed posterior the median is genuinely right of the mode, and that separation *is the signal* that the distribution has a long upper tail. For well-determined curves (Humans, Waymo, Tesla-at-fault) median = mode and the dot's on the peak; only the near-zero-data curves show the gap, and there it's honest.

If you'd rather the dot be glued to the visual peak regardless, that's the mode — I can switch it, but I'd be trading a parameterization-invariant estimate for a parameterization-dependent one, so I left it on the median.

Loose ends we're still carrying

Decisions (yours to make):

  1. Point estimate is split now. The chart dot/tooltip use the posterior median; the cards, stress table, and multiplier still use the MLE (the "≥ lo" form at k=0). Two different "MPI" numbers for the same helmer. Cleanest fix: unify app-wide on the median. *(Biggest one.)*
  2. Dot = median vs mode (the above) — only matters if you want the dot pinned to the visual peak.
  3. Bell vs CI/fan still tell uncertainty two ways. The bell is the proper VMT-marginal; the card CI and the time-series fan are the conservative *double-extreme*. We deliberately kept the double-extreme for the stress verdict (anti-overclaim), but it's an unreconciled inconsistency on screen.

Data limitations (flagged, best-available, not fixable now):

  1. Early-Zoox VMT magnitude is near-blind — shape is sourced (SF driverless from Nov 2024), but the driverless mileage itself is unpublished, so the 13 early rows carry wide 0.5–2× bands.
  2. Faultfrac "treat as gospel" — we decided *not* to build the uncertainty model and to lean on faultFlipMultiplier for systematic-bias risk. A documented stance, not a TODO, but worth remembering it's a choice.

Tooling/knobs (minor):

  1. Screenshot tooling (playwright-core) is uninstalled — I've been verifying numerically, not visually. Worth reinstalling if you want me to eyeball renders.
  2. The 1% visibility floor is a chosen constant (≈2px) — principled (tied to visibility) but tunable.

Want me to take #1 (unify the app on the posterior median, killing the last "≥ lo" displays and the chart-vs-card split) next?

18:43 Claude Code claude-opus-4-8 thought for 1m41s

btw, the tooltip does not need to show the helmer. can we make everything a bit more consistent between the different graphs?
18:45 Claude Code claude-opus-4-8 thought for 1m33s

Here's the factual answer, and it makes your instinct even better-founded than it looks. The default at-fault curves:

| helmer | k | MLE (V/k) | mean (V/(k−.5)) | mode-of-MPI | visual peak | median |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Tesla | 5.75 | 352K | 386K | 280K | 330K | 342K |
| Waymo | 68 | 2.3M | 2.3M | 2.2M | 2.3M | 2.3M |
| Zoox | 0.1 | 20.2M | ∞ | 1.3M | 3.4M | 6.4M |

Three things fall out:

  1. No, none of {mean, median, mode} equals the MLE. They're genuinely four different points: MLE = V/k, mean = V/(k−½), mode-of-MPI = V/(k+1½), median = its own quantile. The MLE is the prior-free likelihood maximizer; the others are posterior summaries.
  1. The mean is the trap. For k ≤ 0.5 (Zoox at-fault, *every* zero-count metric) the posterior mean MPI is infinite — because there's real probability the at-fault rate is ~0, so "expected miles per incident" diverges. So "show all three" can't show the mean exactly on the curves where the spread is the whole story. (The MLE is also ∞ at k=0.)
  1. "Mode" is itself ambiguous — the mode of MPI (1.3M) ≠ the *visual* peak you see on the log axis (3.4M). They're different points.

So for a well-determined curve (Tesla, Waymo) all five values collapse to ~one point — one dot is honest. For a near-zero-data curve (Zoox) they fan from 1.3M to ∞ — and *that fanning is the message*: the point estimate is barely defined.

My recommendation: show two labeled dots — the visual peak ("most likely") and the median — both always finite and plottable; they coincide when the curve is well-determined and separate when it's skewed (the gap = the skew, self-documenting). The tooltip then lists the full breakdown — mode / median / mean / MLE, with "∞" shown where they diverge — so it's completely transparent which is which and *why* we don't lean on the mean/MLE. And per your other note, drop the helmer (the dot color + legend already identify it).

On consistency across graphs

Quick survey of the three tooltip formats today:

  • Distribution dots: Helmer · MPI: X (k inc) · 95% CI: lo–hi
  • MPI-over-time dots: Helmer Month · MPI: X (k inc) · 95% CI: lo–hi
  • Per-helmer VMT dots: Month · N miles (k inc) ← already no helmer

So the helmer is the inconsistency (two charts have it, one doesn't). I'd unify by dropping it everywhere (color identifies the series) and keeping the parallel MPI: … · 95% CI: … body, with the time-charts adding the month.

Before I build it: do you want (a) the two-dot version above, or (b) keep one dot (median) but list all four summaries in its tooltip? And for consistency — drop the helmer from the MPI-over-time chart too, or only the distribution one?

Honest answer: dashing isn't a quick win (it needs splitting each line into solid+dashed sub-paths), and it's only marginally better since the hollow dots already flag those months. But there's a *better, one-line* version: break the line at k=0 — which is what the chart did originally; my median change accidentally made it connect through. A prior-only month isn't part of the data trend, so it should show as just a hollow dot, not a line segment. That's the honest fix and it restores the deliberate original behavior. Doing that:

38/38. Let me confirm visually that the line now breaks at k=0:

Confirmed and it's clearly right: Zoox at-fault (almost no monthly data) is now just scattered hollow dots with no line — no fake trend — while Waymo (data every month) stays a solid connected line, and Tesla shows a line only across its data months. Exactly the honest treatment. 38/38, verified live.

#4 done and verified. That clears the cosmetic items — the only substantive loose end left is #1 (bell vs card-CI/fan: marginal vs double-extreme), plus the two standing data assumptions (early-Zoox magnitude, faultfrac-as-gospel). Want to take on the bell-vs-CI reconciliation, or call it here?

is there a clear pareto improvement with #1 to start with?
12:43 Claude Code claude-opus-4-8 thought for 1m18s

Honest answer: no, not a clean one. #1 is a genuine tradeoff, not a Pareto improvement, which is exactly why it's been sitting unresolved.

The card CI and the fan bands are the double-extreme (vmtMin at the low rate tail, vmtMax at the high) — deliberately *wider* than the true marginal 95%. That same est.lo/est.hi drives the stress verdict, where the extra width is doing real anti-overclaim work: it makes both "robustly safer" *and* "robustly worse" harder to declare. Unifying on the marginal would:

  • (+) make the bell, the card CI, and the "95% CI" label all consistent and literally correct;
  • (−) narrow those intervals, so the verdict declares safer/worse more readily — a regression on the skeptical axis we intentionally chose.

So it's strictly better on consistency/honesty, strictly worse on conservatism. Not Pareto.

The only way to get *close* to Pareto is to decouple display from verdict — show the true marginal 95% on the cards/bell (consistent + honestly labeled), keep the conservative envelope for the verdict only. But that adds a second uncertainty concept (more code, two notions of "the interval"), so it's not free either — it trades simplicity.

Per the PDP, since there's no clean dominating move, I'd leave it unless you want to make the values call: consistency+honest-labels vs conservatism+simplicity. My weak lean is to leave it — the bell and the card CI live in different sections, so the inconsistency is latent, and the conservative verdict is a feature. Your call.

The "mess" is just node_modules/ (containing playwright-core, used by the screenshot driver) showing up as untracked. It's not declared in package.json and shouldn't be committed — the clean fix is to gitignore it rather than delete it, since you use it for screenshots.

Done. node_modules/ is gone from the git status clutter.

What I did:

  • Added node_modules/ to [.gitignore](.gitignore) (it now sits alongside __pycache__/).
  • Kept node_modules/playwright-core on disk — it's your screenshot driver's headless-shell dependency, not actually committed anywhere, so deleting it would just mean reinstalling next time you take a screenshot.

Git status is now clean of the playwright noise; only your real edits (.gitignore, AGENTS.md, crashla.js, quals/monthly-series.qual.mjs) remain.

One note: playwright-core isn't declared in [package.json](package.json), so it's an implicit dependency. If you'd rather make it reproducible, I could add it under devDependencies (and generate a package-lock.json) — but that's optional and gitignoring is enough to clean up the mess. I have everything I need to compare. Here's how Waymo's brand-new release lines up with the repo.

What Waymo just published

Waymo dropped a safety update on June 24, 2026 (yesterday): 220.6M rider-only miles through end of March 2026, now across five geographies (Atlanta added). The Safety Impact hub gives the per-city cumulative breakdown and IPMM rates.

How it matches the repo

1. VMT estimate — strong validation. Your [vmt.csv](data/vmt.csv) cumulative for Waymo lands almost exactly on the new milestone, and the ~+2% bias is remarkably consistent across all three published anchors:

| End of | Repo helmer_cumulative_vmt | Waymo official | Diff |
|---|--:|--:|--:|
| Sep 2025 | 129.7M | 127M | +2.1% |
| Dec 2025 | 175.2M | 170.7M | +2.6% |
| Mar 2026 | 225.2M | 220.6M | +2.1% |

The actual 220.6M sits comfortably inside your March band (kyoom_min 213.4M – kyoom_max 289.1M), near the low end — so your central estimate runs ~2% hot but is well within the ±25% band. This new anchor confirms the methodology is calibrated correctly.

2. Safety IPMM — essentially unchanged, slightly stronger for Waymo. Your [README.md](README.md) Kelsey-Piper section (cited at the 170.7M / Dec-2025 snapshot) vs the new 220.6M figures:

| Metric (IPMM) | README cites (Waymo / human) | New 220.6M (Waymo / benchmark) |
|---|--:|--:|
| Any-injury | 0.74 / 3.97 | 0.71 / 3.91 |
| Airbag (any vehicle) | 0.31 / 1.66 | 0.30 / 1.68 |
| Serious/fatal | 0.02 / 0.23 | 0.01 / 0.23 |

Reduction multiples nudged in Waymo's favor: serious/fatal went 91%→94% fewer; airbag and any-injury now 82% fewer each. New granularity: airbag *Waymo-vehicle-only* 0.06 vs 1.11, plus VRU breakdowns (93% ped, 84% cyclist, 84% motorcyclist).

3. New per-city calibration data (cumulative through Mar 2026): Phoenix 80.6M (36.5%), SF Bay 67.1M (30.4%), LA 51.8M (23.5%), Austin 15.8M (7.2%), Atlanta 5.4M (2.4%). Compare your README geographic table (through Sep 2025): Phoenix 44.5%, SF 30.6%, LA 20.1%, Austin 5.0%. The shift (Phoenix share falling, LA/Austin rising, Atlanta new) is consistent with expansion — and CA markets (SF+LA) = 53.9% cumulative, close to your ~51–55% CA-share assumption.

Loose ends

Data/notes worth correcting:

  1. Zoox "paid LV ~late Jun 2026" is wrong (11 rows in [data/vmt.csv](data/vmt.csv)). As of late June 2026, Zoox is still free — paid rides are *blocked* because the steering-wheel/pedal-less vehicle lacks an FMVSS exemption, with no announced date (ecoportal, Fortune). This is a note-accuracy issue only — free miles still count in the denominator, so no VMT values change.
  1. Tesla May-2026 fleet sizing is the shakiest number in the dataset. Our 2026-05 note assumes a TX unsupervised fleet of 25→42 (Jun 2, Electrek); other trackers put it at ~20–25 (eletric-vehicles). Tesla discloses no absolute robotaxi mileage (the Q1 deck gives only "~2x sequentially"), so the entire Tesla series is tracker-derived. The wide band (1.81M–3.05M cum) covers the conflict, but the 440k central for May is a judgment call worth revisiting.

Freshness / forward-looking:

  1. NHTSA incidents last modified 2026-06-15; counts (1783 Waymo / 18 Tesla / 35 Zoox) match the mid-June fetch. A fresh slurp.py run would catch any newer June publish. May 2026 is partial (16 incidents, already handled by coverage-thinning).
  1. Tesla expansion prep (Phoenix, Miami, Orlando, Tampa, Las Vegas) — none *unsupervised*-launched as of late June, so no impact through May. But once NHTSA publishes June data, slurp.py will abort until a June VMT row is added for each helmer.
  1. Prediction-markets snapshot is 2026-06-19 (fresh, auto-refetches live). A couple markets are near resolution ("Tesla level 4 summer 2025", "at-fault accident by 2026") — disable when they resolve.

Notable external events (mostly out-of-scope, but flag-worthy):

  1. Waymo recalled ~3,900 vehicles in June 2026 for failing to detect closed freeway construction zones (AZ/CA), driving into work sites at speed — 6th recall in ~2 years; freeway service suspended pending a fix (HNGN). Could dent Waymo's June+ VMT (out of our window). Worth a README mention given the rosy 220.6M-mile framing.
  1. Tesla Katy, TX fatality (Jun 19, 2026) — a Model 3 on consumer FSD with a human driver who allegedly overrode the system (Inquirer). Operator ≠ "None," so it's correctly excluded from our robotaxi dataset; the NHTSA probe targets consumer FSD, not the robotaxi service. Flagging only so it isn't conflated with robotaxi safety.

One correction to my own research: a search result attributed "~3,000 vehicles / 500k rides/week / 11 cities" to Tesla — that's actually Waymo's co-CEO Dmitri Dolgov (it's the figure already in our Waymo methodology). Not a Tesla number; I didn't apply it as one.

Optional: the new Waymo page also restates the human-benchmark IPMM (3.91 any-injury / 1.68 airbag / 0.23 serious). Not apples-to-apples with our surface-street bands (Waymo uses a proprietary location-weighted benchmark we don't implement — already documented), so I'd leave the bands alone.

Commercial robotaxi rides — cumulative

~34 million paid rides in the US, 80% CI ≈ [30M, 40M] — essentially all Waymo.

How I get there:

  • Waymo dominates: >20M cumulative paid rides as of Dec 2025 (14M+ in 2025 alone), running ~500k paid rides/week in early 2026 and ramping toward a 1M/week target by year-end. Adding ~25 weeks of Jan–late-June 2026 at an average ~520k/week (~13M more) lands at ~34M.
  • Cross-check (miles): 20M rides against 170.7M miles at end-2025 ≈ 8.5 mi/ride incl. deadhead; applied to ~281M miles by late June → ~33M rides. Consistent.
  • Tesla adds only ~0.2–0.5M (Austin + Dallas/Houston, no published ride counts, many early rides free/supervised) — negligible at this resolution.
  • Zoox contributes zero commercial rides: despite ~2M miles and 350k+ riders, it's still free (the FMVSS blocker above).

The 80% band is driven by the ">20M" Dec floor (could be 20–24M) and the uncertain Jan–Jun ramp (avg 450–600k/week).

Scope caveat: that's US-only, matching this project's Waymo/Tesla/Zoox frame. Globally the number is far higher — China's Baidu Apollo Go alone has reported 11M+ cumulative rides, with Pony.ai and WeRide adding more, so worldwide commercial robotaxi rides are plausibly 50–70M+.

sorry, i meant commercial tesla robotaxi rides
16:34 Claude Code claude-opus-4-8 thought for 2m2s

Ah, Tesla specifically — let me get the latest reported figures rather than guess.

Got it — Tesla specifically. Tesla publishes no ride counts, so this is an estimate. Here's my best read.

Commercial Tesla robotaxi rides — cumulative

~150,000 rides since the June 27, 2025 Austin launch, 80% CI ≈ [80,000, 300,000].

Nearly all Tesla robotaxi-service rides are "commercial" (paid — $4.20 flat at launch, now dynamic pricing), including the supervised passenger-seat-monitor rides, so "commercial" ≈ essentially all robotaxi rides.

Two independent methods bracket the range, and they don't fully agree — which is why the CI is wide (a factor of ~4):

  • Fleet-rate method → low end (~80–150k): ~20 active unsupervised vehicles as of June 2026 (Electrek), grown from ~10–15 over the year, at low utilization (~19% availability noted Feb 2026). Journalists peg current volume at "low thousands of trips/week." Integrating ~16 cars × 52 weeks × ~100 rides/car-week ≈ ~85k.
  • Mileage method → high end (~170–300k): our own VMT series puts cumulative Tesla robotaxi miles at ~2.6M through mid-June 2026. With a sparse ~20-car fleet now spread over 245 sq mi of Austin metro, deadhead/repositioning per ride is large — at ~12–18 service miles per ride that's ~145–215k rides; lower miles-per-ride pushes it toward 300k.

Center of mass ≈ ~150k.

Why the band is so wide:

  • Tesla discloses nothing — no ride or mile counts (the Q1 deck only said "paid miles ~2× sequentially").
  • Fleet size is contested (skeptics say ~20 unsupervised; Tesla implies more — the "42 in TX" figure includes driver-seat-monitor mode).
  • Dallas/Houston (launched Apr 18, 2026, ~3–11 cars) adds only a small, uncertain increment.
  • The two methods disagree by ~2–3×, reflecting genuine uncertainty in miles-per-ride and utilization.

For scale: this is roughly 200–400× fewer than Waymo's ~34M cumulative US paid rides.

(No file changes this turn — this is just the estimate. The Zoox-note and Waymo-recall edits from the previous turn are still in place and passing quals.)
