using all the data we have here, plus additional web research, can you generate probability distributions for the size of each helmer's ADS fleat on Jan 1, 2027?
12:45 Claude Code claude-opus-4-8 thought for 6m8s · 1h11m21s wall-clock time

I'll start by exploring the repo to understand what data we have and what "helmers" and "ADS fleet" mean in this context.

This is a robotaxi-safety analysis repo (Tesla/Waymo/Zoox = the "helmers"). The user wants fleet-size (vehicle count) distributions for Jan 1, 2027 — a forecasting task. Let me pull the relevant data: VMT trajectories, prediction markets, and any fleet-size mentions.

Now I have the repo's data. Key fleet-size anchors I can already see: Waymo ~3,000 cars (Apr–May 2026); Tesla TX unsupervised fleet 25→42 vehicles, ~58 total active TX (May–Jun 2026); Zoox has no vehicle count in-repo. Let me do web research for current fleet sizes and announced 2026→2027 expansion plans.

Let me pull specifics from the most useful sources to pin down current numbers and stated end-of-2026 targets.

The Driverless Digest piece is a year old (May 2025). Let me get more recent figures and the blocked sources via alternates.

Strong anchors now. The Manifold market "Tesla > Waymo AVs by Jan 2 2027" at 23% is directly relevant to Tesla's tail. Let me do one more confirmation pass on the most recent (mid-2026) Waymo and Tesla scaling signals.

I have enough to build the distributions. One scoping note first, then the answer.

Scope I'm using

The repo measures VMT and incidents, not vehicle counts, so "fleet size" is an extension built from repo anchors + web research. I'm defining "ADS fleet" the same way the project defines its numerator: vehicles in genuine driverless/ADS robotaxi service (NHTSA "operator = none," plus Tesla's driver‑monitor "In‑Vehicle Commercial/Test" mode). That means Tesla's ~150–300 *safety‑driver* Bay Area Model Ys are excluded (they're SAE L2‑supervised, out of the project's scope). I flag below how Tesla's number moves if you want the looser definition.

Horizon: today (2026‑06‑30) → Jan 1, 2027 ≈ 6 months out.

---

Summary: fleet-size distributions, Jan 1 2027

| Helmer | Median | 90% CI | Shape |
|---|---:|---:|---|
| Waymo | ~4,700 | 3,300 – 6,750 | Lognormal (σ≈0.22) |
| Tesla | ~200 | 90 – 3,300 | Bimodal mixture (see below) |
| Zoox | ~150 | 75 – 310 | Lognormal (σ≈0.45) |

Full quantiles:

| Pctile | Waymo | Tesla | Zoox |
|---|---:|---:|---:|
| 5% | 3,300 | 70 | 75 |
| 10% | 3,500 | 90 | 88 |
| 25% | 4,050 | 125 | 113 |
| 50% | 4,700 | 200 | 150 |
| 75% | 5,450 | 850 | 200 |
| 90% | 6,200 | 3,300 | 257 |
| 95% | 6,750 | 5,200 | 300 |

---

Reasoning per helmer

Waymo — lognormal, median ≈ 4,700

Anchors: ~1,500 (May 2025) → ~2,500 (Nov 2025) → 3,067 (Dec 2025) → "~3,000" cited through Feb–May 2026; repo VMT uses ~3,000 cars for Apr–May 2026 (and VMT grew ~34% Dec→May, so effective capacity is rising even where the headcount reads flat). Growth drivers for H2 2026: the Mesa/Magna plant (tens of thousands/yr capacity), final Jaguar I‑PACE shipment + 2,000 more I‑PACE retrofits, new platforms (Zeekr RT 6th‑gen, Ioniq 5), a $16B Feb‑2026 round ($126B valuation), and 6+ new cities.

The honest tension: H1 2026 looks like a plateau near 3,000 while they wait on new platforms, against a clear, funded pipeline to add ~2,000+ vehicles. I center on resumed-but-not-explosive growth (~+50% over the year), with the band spanning "plateau persists" (~3,300) to "Mesa ramps hard" (~6,750). This is the tightest distribution of the three — Waymo's growth is supply-constrained and therefore relatively predictable.

Tesla — bimodal mixture, median ≈ 200 with a fat right tail

Anchors: TX fleet 25 (end‑Apr) → 42 registered (Jun 2), of which only ~20–25 are *active unsupervised*; repo notes ~58 total active TX incl. driver‑monitor mode. Austin ~20, Dallas 3, Houston 6. Musk himself says robotaxi revenue is immaterial in 2026, material "next year." Reality has run ~25–40× below his "1,000 cars in a month" launch projection.

But Tesla is structurally different from the other two: it has no vehicle‑supply constraint. Millions of FSD‑capable Model Ys exist, plus ~85 Cybercabs already built at Giga Texas (ramping). The only gates are (1) Tesla's internal confidence to pull the safety monitor and (2) regulators. Musk targets unsupervised FSD to customers "probably Q4 2026." So the outcome is event‑driven, not a smooth curve — which is why I model it as a mixture, not a lognormal:

  • ~75% "slow ramp persists": lognormal, median ~160 (range ~65–400). Tesla keeps cautiously adding Model‑Y robotaxis across TX/new metros.
  • ~25% "unsupervised greenlight / flood": lognormal, median ~3,000 (range ~1,000–9,000). If Q4 unsupervised approval lands and Tesla enrolls existing Model Ys + Cybercabs, the fleet jumps into the thousands almost overnight.

That tail is what produces the huge gap between the 50th (200) and 90th (3,300) percentiles.

If you use the looser definition (count supervised Bay Area Model Ys as "fleet"): add roughly +150–350 across the board.

Zoox — lognormal, median ≈ 150

Anchors: ~50 robotaxis (Jan 2026) → expanding to ~100 across Las Vegas + SF (Mar 2026); redesigned production‑intent robotaxi unveiled Jun 24–25 2026, series production "later in 2026." Hayward factory capacity up to 100/week (10k/yr) when scaled. Amazon‑funded. Gated by the NHTSA Part 555 / FMVSS exemption (no steering wheel) for paid rides — "optimistic, coming months" — plus CA DMV/CPUC for SF/LA. Joining Uber in LV summer 2026.

So the floor is fairly firm (~100, already achieved + deep pockets), and the upside depends on whether the new vehicle's series production + the federal exemption both land in H2 2026. Central ~150, with a right tail to ~400 if production ramps.

---

Cross-checks & caveats

  • Prediction‑market check (from the repo's snapshot): Manifold "Tesla has more AVs providing ridehailing than Waymo by Jan 2 2027" sits at 23%, and "Tesla serves more fully autonomous rides than Waymo in 2026" at 10%. My Tesla mixture puts only ~6–10% mass above Waymo's median — *lower* than the market's 23%. The gap is probably part market optimism on the Tesla "flip the switch" tail and part a looser vehicle definition (the market likely counts supervised cars). I left my numbers grounded in the ADS‑only scope rather than over‑fitting one thin market, but it's a reason to take Tesla's upper tail seriously, not dismiss it.
  • Biggest single uncertainty: the Tesla Q4 unsupervised‑FSD decision. It's a discrete regulatory/confidence event, not a growth rate — hence the bimodality.
  • Definitional fragility: "fleet size" reporting mixes registered vs. active vs. in‑service vehicles; ±20% noise on any point estimate just from that.
  • These are my forecasts, not anything the repo asserts — the repo tops out at May 2026 VMT and tracks no vehicle counts directly.

Reproducible spec: Waymo = Lognormal(median 4700, σ 0.22); Zoox = Lognormal(median 150, σ 0.45); Tesla = 0.75·Lognormal(median 160, σ 0.55) + 0.25·Lognormal(median 3000, σ 0.75).

Want me to (a) turn this into a Monte Carlo script in the scratchpad to sample/plot them, or (b) write it up as a section/data file in the repo (e.g., a fleet‑forecast note alongside the VMT methodology)?

Sources:

  • Driverless Digest — Waymo Plans to Add 2,000 More Robotaxis
  • Carbon Credits — Waymo Hits 2,500 Robotaxis
  • Waymo — Scaling our fleet through U.S. manufacturing (Mesa/Magna)
  • Assembly Mag — Waymo to Double the Size of its Fleet
  • electrive — Tesla robotaxi fleet in Texas reaches only 42 vehicles
  • Electrek — Tesla robotaxi expands to entire Austin metro, only ~20 vehicles
  • Basenor — 85 Cybercabs Spotted at Giga Texas
  • Electrek — Tesla confirms Cybercab production started
  • Forbes — Zoox Expands Robotaxi Fleet As It Awaits Approval
  • CNBC — Amazon's Zoox unveils redesigned robotaxi ahead of expansion

1. Toggle: miles / rides / fleet

The growth extrapolator now has a radio toggle at its top. One metric-generic renderer drives all three:

  • Fleet size — the researched vehicle-count anchors.
  • Miles / month — straight from the repo's own data/vmt.csv monthly VMT (the rigorous series), so this one is well-grounded.
  • Rides / month — rough: only Waymo's (~250k→500k+/week) is well-sourced; Tesla/Zoox are low-confidence guesses. The chart shows a caveat note saying exactly that.

All are per-month run-rates (not cumulative) so they share the "how big is each helmer right now" framing. Each forecast endpoint lands at Jan 1 2027 and, for the fleet metric, ties to the distribution chart's mixture by construction.

Decision I made (flagging it): per-month run-rate, not cumulative. If you'd rather see cumulative miles/rides (the "220M miles" style headline number), say so and I'll switch the basis.

2. Tesla "insufficiently bimodal" → now trimodal

You're right. Tesla now has a third mode for exactly your scenario — eyes-off FSD enabled across the HW4 fleet — so its distribution is 0.52·LN(180) + 0.33·LN(9000) + 0.15·LN(600000):

  • median 476, but the 90% CI is now 88 → ~903,000 vehicles.
  • The distribution chart visibly shows three humps (~180, ~9k, ~600k), and the trajectory band balloons toward ~1M at Jan 2027.

Two honest caveats baked into the code comments:

  • Scope shift: mode C counts *ADS-operating personal HW4 cars*, which is a broader "fleet" than the NHTSA robotaxi-service scope Waymo/Zoox are counted under. That's the apples-to-oranges price of capturing the "all HW4 goes ADS" scenario — I documented it rather than hide it.
  • I put 15% weight on it ("decent chance, minority outcome"). Tell me if your number is higher and I'll re-weight.

Implementation notes: kept the human's English copy (Median / 90% CI / Fleet size); wrote the new rides caveat in English to match the section (rule-8 note in the code); added two quals (fleet-forecast, fleet-timeseries) covering normalization, the trimodal shape, the HW4 six-figure tail, all three toggle states, and an anti-Postel weight/ordering guard.

Side question: teslafsdtracker disengagement details

Short answer: you can get the breakdown by *type*, but not a per-event detail feed.

  • It does categorize every disengagement: *Critical Safety* (avoid accident, ran a red/stop, wrong side of road, unsafe action), *Non-critical* (wrong lane, courtesy, merge), and *Interventions* (accelerator tap, scroll wheel, turn signal). The dashboards have a "Top Causations for Disengagements" chart plus filters by HW3/HW4, city vs highway, FSD version, and geography. That's the closest thing to "what's causing the criticals."
  • It deliberately does not record routes — only distance traveled + the categorized event — so there's no per-event location or narrative, and no published per-event list, notes feed, or documented API/export.
  • It's crowdsourced and self-classified (submitted via the MATT3R K3Y extension, the EVGuyCanada in-car app, or manual entry validated by a VIN+firmware screenshot). So "critical" is whatever each submitter decided, with selection bias and small per-version samples — treat the rate as soft.

Ways to actually get more granularity:

  1. Run a logger yourself (EVGuyCanada / MATT3R) — your own drives give you event-level logs you control, just for your car.
  2. Ask the maintainer for raw submission data — it exists behind the aggregates; the Tesla Motors Club thread is the contact path.
  3. There's no official per-disengagement dataset for consumer FSD: NHTSA SGO only captures *crashes* (not disengagements), and California DMV disengagement reports only cover permitted AV *testing*, which Tesla's consumer FSD isn't filed under. So the crowdsourced tracker is essentially the only game in town.

Sources: teslafsdtracker help · Main dashboard · TMC discussion

Let me prioritize the VMT chart issue — that's a potential bug. Let me diagnose it properly before answering. I'll look at the Waymo cumulative data and how the kyoom band is built.

Confirmed the symptom: Waymo's cumulative best-estimate dot sits at 1–26% of the way up the band — at published-milestone months (Sep'25, Feb–Mar'26) it's pinned right at the floor. Let me find how the kyoom band is constructed to explain *why* and whether it's a bug.

I've diagnosed the VMT issue. Let me confirm the kyoom band is display-only (not feeding the MPI math) so I can tell you accurately how risky a fix is:

Confirmed: the kyoom band (lines 1987–1988) is used only in the cumulative VMT chart's error bars. The MPI/safety math uses the monthly vmt_min/vmt_max, never kyoom. So this is purely a display-band issue — fixing it can't move any safety number.

Here's the diagnosis, then I'll do (a), explain (b), and tee up (c).

Why the Waymo cumulative dot sits at the bottom of the band

It's not a rendering bug — it's how the cumulative ("kyoom") band is authored, and it's asymmetric by construction:

  • Lower bound (kyoom_min) is tightened up to Waymo's published cumulative milestones (127M, 170.7M, 220.6M…), which the methodology treats as floors — "Waymo has driven *at least* this much" (they under-state; e.g. "over 200M"). So kyoom_min ≈ the published number.
  • Upper bound (kyoom_max) was left as the running cumulative sum of the monthly +25–30% maxima — loose and compounding (~+28%). It was never re-tightened to the milestones.
  • The best-estimate dot is pinned to the milestone too (cume ≈ kyoom_min).

Net band ≈ [milestone, milestone×1.28] → tiny below, big above → dot at the bottom (I measured 1–26% up the band, and ~0–1% at the published-milestone months Sep'25 / Feb'26 / Mar'26).

This is documented (README: "published figures are encoded as lower-bound floors"), but there's a real inconsistency: the README also calls the Dec-2025 and Mar-2026 anchors "exact central estimates." If they're central, the +28% one-sided upper band around them is wrong — it should be roughly symmetric and tight there.

Recommended fix (F1), if you want it: tighten kyoom_max near the published anchors so the band is symmetric/tight at milestones and only bulges *between* them. It's a display-only edit to data/vmt.csv (kyoom feeds nothing but this chart — no MPI/safety number moves), and the kyoom-band qual still passes (kyoom_max stays ≥ cume and ≤ the running-sum). I did not apply it — it's a documented modeling choice, so your call. Alternatives: leave it and rely on the "floor" framing, or just annotate the chart.

Want me to apply F1?

(b) What "re-weight the HW4 mode" means

Tesla's distribution is a mixture of three scenarios with probabilities that sum to 1:

  • A — slow robotaxi ramp: 52%
  • B — aggressive robotaxi scale-up: 33%
  • C — eyes-off FSD across the HW4 fleet (the hundreds-of-thousands-to-millions case): 15%

"Re-weighting" = changing those probabilities. Right now I put 15% on the all-HW4 scenario. If you think it's likelier (say 25–30%), I bump C's weight (and trim A/B), which fattens the upper hump and pulls the median up. What probability would you put on "a large fraction of HW4 Teslas operating as ADS by Jan 1, 2027"? That number directly becomes the weight — and it's also the weight of the separate curve in (c).

Searched Polymarket thoroughly (gamma API + search endpoint + web). Here's the honest picture.

Polymarket doesn't have a good market for *this*

Polymarket's Tesla-autonomy markets are all launch-timing or California-approval, not fleet-scale — and the on-point ones are either resolved or thin:

| Market | Current | Volume | Use for our weight? |
|---|---|---|---|
| Tesla launches unsupervised FSD by… | resolved YES (Q1 2026, via the empty-seat Austin robotaxi) | $15M | No — already happened |
| Tesla driverless robotaxi by Jun 30 '26 | resolved YES | — | No |
| Tesla robotaxis in California by Jun 30 | ~0.5% Yes (deadline passed) | $114k | No |
| Tesla robotaxis in California by Dec 31 '26 | 21.5% Yes | $292 (thin) | Closest, but off-target |

The Dec-31 California market (21.5%) is the only *forward-looking* one, but it measures "does a CA robotaxi service launch," which is much easier than "a large fraction of HW4 Teslas operate as ADS." And $292 of volume is basically noise.

So: no Polymarket cleanly prices the HW4-fleet-goes-ADS scenario. The scale question just isn't listed there — Polymarket bettors have concentrated on binary launch/date/geography bets.

The best market anchor stays Manifold

For scenario C specifically, the most on-point market is still the Manifold one:

  • "Millions of Teslas at level 3 autonomy in 2026?" → 4.3% (resolves on >1M Teslas at L3 by end-2026) — this *is* the fleet-scale question. (Thin too: ~15 traders.)
  • Supporting: "read a book while driving a private car" end-2026 bucket → 8.2%.

Caveat worth stating: every market that actually targets this is thin/low-liquidity, so treat these as weak priors, not precise probabilities. The thick markets ($15M) are the resolved launch-timing ones.

My recommendation for the weight

Anchor scenario C at ~5% (the 4.3% "millions of Teslas at L3" market, rounded up slightly for the softer "large fraction, not literally millions" reading). If you think the markets underrate Tesla, 8–10% is defensible; your original "decent chance" intuition (15%+) is well above where any market sits.

On adding a Polymarket

The truthful result: there's no usable Polymarket for this. The only forward-looking candidate — "Will Tesla launch robotaxis in California by December 31?" (21.5%) — is thin ($292 of volume), off-target (a CA *launch*, not fleet-scale ADS), *and* its gamma-API slug doesn't refetch cleanly, so embedding it threw a console error every page load. I reverted it rather than ship a broken, misleading card.

The good news: the two markets that actually price this are already live in the app's panel — Manifold "Millions of Teslas at level 3 autonomy in 2026?" (4.3%) and "Tesla has more AVs than Waymo by Jan 2 2027" (23%), both enabled. So the provenance is already on the page.

(If you want the CA Polymarket anyway, I can embed it *snapshot-only* — a small noRefetch flag so it renders the frozen 21.5% without the failing live call. Say the word.)

Principled ways to pick the probability

Ranked by how much I'd trust them here:

1. Fit the model to the markets already on the page (my pick). Don't hand-pick one weight — make the distribution *reproduce* the observable market probabilities:

  • P(Tesla fleet > Waymo fleet, Jan 2027) ≈ 23% — the "Tesla > Waymo" market, and the most liquid relevant one ($25k).
  • P(millions/L3-scale) ≈ 4–5% — the "millions at L3" market.

Those two over-determine modes B and C: C ≈ 5% (matches the millions market) and B ≈ 24% (so that B's above-Waymo mass + C ≈ 23%), leaving A ≈ 71%. Elegant because the chart becomes internally consistent with the app's own prediction-markets panel.

2. Reference-class base rate (outside view). Tesla's hit rate on ambitious autonomy timelines is ~0-on-schedule (there's literally a Wikipedia list of Musk's missed FSD predictions). A reference class of "Musk autonomy milestone hit within his stated ~6-month window" argues for low single digits — a strong prior against your original 15%.

3. Fermi decomposition. Multiply the necessary conditions:
P(ships eyes-off FSD to consumer cars by Jan'27 ≈ 0.35) × P(regulators allow personal-car operator-none somewhere ≈ 0.5) × P(a *large fraction* actually operates that fast ≈ 0.3) ≈ 5%. Converges with the market.

4. Market aggregation / extremizing. Combine the relevant markets weighted by liquidity × relevance (geometric-mean-ish), which down-weights the thin ones. Lands ~5–8%.

5. Sensitivity instead of a point. Sidestep false precision: render the Tesla curve for C ∈ {5%, 15%, 30%} (or a tiny slider) and let the reader see how much it matters — consistent with the stress-testing the app already does elsewhere.

My recommendation: go with #1 — it's the most defensible and ties the chart to the markets. It yields concrete weights (A 71% / B 24% / C 5%), which I can plug straight into (c).

Want me to ship (c) with those market-fitted weights (A/B/C = 71/24/5)? Or pick a C weight yourself and I'll use it.

What shipped

  1. HW4 fork on the trajectory — the fleet trajectory now draws the conditional Tesla all-HW4 ADS (~5%) scenario as an orange dashed fork off the last Tesla history point, shooting up to ~600k (band to ~2.9M). The mainline Tesla robotaxi (~95%) stays the solid red apples-to-apples line. (Fleet-only — miles/rides don't fork.)

Fleet shows the four-curve mixture (with the Tesla scope split); miles and rides each render three per-helmer densities built as split-lognormals from their {best, lo, hi} bands (asymmetric, so Tesla's huge HW4 upside shows as a long right tail rather than a fake symmetric bell). Refactor notes: the trajectory is now lane-based (spec.lanes()), which cleanly supports the fork (a branchOnly lane) and keeps miles/rides as one lane per helmer. Added splitLogNormalLogDensity + fleetDistributionCurves for the metric-driven distribution. Updated both quals (fleet trajectory now expects 4 lanes / 4 dashed segments incl. the fork; the toggle qual checks both charts render 4-or-3 curves per metric).

One thing to flag

On miles/rides, Tesla's distribution curve is very flat/faint — that's the honest consequence of its enormous band (e.g. miles 4M→500M): a split-lognormal that wide has low peak height, so it reads as a nearly-flat line with just the median dot visible. It's correct, but if you'd rather it be legible I can either cap the drawn range or render Tesla's miles/rides as a two-scope split (like fleet) so the HW4 mass becomes a distinct low bump instead of a flat tail. can you estimate the chances that waymo hockeysticks? can you look into forecasts of their ojai (sp?) production etc? i'm worried the current extrapolation is just extending the line on the log plot, not factoring in the possibility of a step change
19:00 Claude Code claude-opus-4-8 thought for 3m45s · 4m40s wall-clock time

Good research question — and you're right that the current Waymo line is a naive log-linear extrapolation with no step-change mode. Let me look into Waymo's production/scaling plans (I'll figure out what "ojai" refers to) and the hockeystick question. This is analysis, so I'll research and estimate — not touch the model yet.

"Ojai" is real — it's Waymo's 6th‑gen Zeekr‑built robotaxi (Zeekr builds the base in Ningbo, China; Waymo+Magna integrate it at the Mesa, AZ plant), which began public rides May 28, 2026. That plus the Ioniq 5 deal is exactly the hockeystick setup. Let me pin down the production ramp timing and check for a market anchor.

Got the picture. Let me close out the shipped work and give you the analysis (no model changes yet — this is the estimate you asked for).

"Ojai" and the production picture

"Ojai" is real (good ear): it's Waymo's 6th‑gen robotaxi, built on the Zeekr RT platform — Zeekr makes the base vehicle in Ningbo, China, and Waymo+Magna integrate the Driver at the Mesa, AZ plant. It started carrying public riders May 28, 2026 (PHX/LA/SF). The production stack behind a potential step change:

  • Mesa/Magna plant — ~239k sq ft, scaling toward "tens of thousands of vehicles/year" at full capacity. But near‑term (through 2026) it's modest: ~2,000 I‑PACE retrofits + ~2,000 built. Starts with Ojai, then Ioniq 5.
  • Hyundai Ioniq 5 — a reported 50,000 units by 2028 ($2.5B), built at Hyundai's Georgia Metaplant. Volume is a 2027–2028 story, not 2026.
  • Waymo's own stated 2026 goal: 1,000,000 paid rides/week by year‑end (from ~500k now) — which co‑CEO Mawakana calls the "inflection point."

The hockeystick math

The 1M‑weekly‑rides target needs ~7,200 vehicles. Reality check:

  • Now: ~3,500–3,900 vehicles, growing at ~265–300/month.
  • Linear pace → ~5,900–6,000 by year‑end (≈840k rides) — short of the million.
  • Hitting 7,200+ requires acceleration — the Mesa/Ojai/Ioniq ramp kicking in hard in H2 2026. Analysts think the fleet reaches "at least 10,000 over the next year."

So the enablers exist but are early: Ojai just launched, Ioniq volume is 2027+. The *full* hockeystick (tens of thousands) is a 2027–2028 phenomenon, not something that lands by Jan 1 2027.

My estimate for the Jan 1, 2027 cross‑section:

  • Base case (linear‑ish, Ojai trickling in): ~5,500–6,000
  • Hockeystick (accelerate toward the 1M‑rides fleet, ~7,000+): ~25%
  • Strong surprise (Mesa near full tilt, >8,500): ~10–12%

You're right about the model — two problems

  1. The median is arguably too low. My current Waymo forecast is median 4,700 — but the production‑pace base case is ~5,800. The naive log‑linear line is anchored to my ~3,300 May estimate, while newer sources put Waymo at ~3,900 in June growing ~280/mo.
  2. No step‑change mode. Waymo is a single tight lognormal (σ 0.22), hi90 6,749 — which just *grazes* the 7,200 hockeystick threshold and has no mass beyond it. Nothing captures the Mesa/Ojai/Ioniq upside.

Proposed fix (want me to ship it?)

Make Waymo a 2‑component mixture — same as I'd do for any "step‑change" story — but drawn as one curve (it's the same robotaxi scope, not a Tesla‑style scope split):

  • Mainline (~78%): median ~5,300, σ 0.20 — the base production pace, nudged up to match ~280/mo.
  • Hockeystick (~22%): median ~9,000, σ 0.28 — the Mesa/Ojai/Ioniq ramp toward (and past) the 1M‑rides fleet.

Net: Waymo becomes mildly right‑skewed, median ~5,600, hi90 ~10,000 — so the distribution chart shows a fat upper tail and the trajectory band fans up toward a step change instead of hugging the log‑linear line.

Two decisions for you:

  1. Weight for the hockeystick mode — my ~22% (from the pace‑vs‑target gap), or your own number?
  2. Should I also raise the base‑case median (4,700 → ~5,300) to match the ~280/mo pace, or leave the center and *only* add the upside tail?

Say go with your preferences and I'll implement + re‑verify.

Sources: Electrek — Waymo Ojai 6th‑gen rides · TechCrunch — Waymo's Chinese‑made Ojai · Waymo — Mesa manufacturing · Electrek — Hyundai 50k Ioniq 5 · Forbes/Ohnsman — 1M rides/week target & fleet math · Automotive World — 1M weekly as the 2026 metric

Done — researched to a clear answer and shipped it. All 47 quals pass, verified live, no console errors, nothing committed.

What the research settled

FutureSearch (a professional forecasting firm) is the decisive anchor — their Waymo model projects end‑2026 fleet ~5,900–6,000 (central) at the current ~265–300 cars/month pace, with weekly rides p50 775k / p10 500k / p90 1.15M, and explicitly says the 1M‑ride target "falls short" without a faster ramp (needs ~7,200 vehicles). Cross‑checked against Forbes/Ohnsman (same fleet math) and the production timeline: the Ojai (Zeekr 6th‑gen) only started public rides May 28 2026, and Ioniq 5 volume is a 2027–2028 story — so the *full* hockeystick is beyond the Jan‑1‑2027 horizon, but a partial step‑up is live.

Both of your questions, answered:

  1. Hockeystick weight → 18%. FutureSearch's ride p90 of 1.15M ⇒ P(rides ≥ 1M) ≈ 20%, and the fleet‑equivalent (~7,000+) ⇒ ~18%.
  2. Raise the base median → yes. 4,700 was too low; FutureSearch's central is ~5,900.

What changed

Waymo is now a two‑mode mixture drawn as one curve (same robotaxi scope — a change in growth *rate*, not scope, so no Tesla‑style split):

  • Base "production pace" — 82%, median 5,800, σ 0.16 (the ~280 cars/month line).
  • Hockeystick — 18%, median 7,500, σ 0.25 (Mesa/Magna + Ojai/Zeekr + Ioniq‑5 ramp toward the 1M‑rides fleet).

Result: Waymo median 5,967, 90% CI [4,501, 8,823] (was 4,700 / [3,273, 6,749]). The distribution now shows a right‑skewed fat upper tail, and the trajectory band fans up toward a step change instead of hugging the log‑linear line — exactly your worry, addressed. I also nudged the recent Waymo history anchors up (May ~3,300 → 3,750) to match the ~3,600 Q1 / ~3,871 June data.

One thing I checked so you don't have to

Raising Waymo could have broken the Tesla calibration (P(Tesla > Waymo) was fit to the 23% market with Waymo at 4,700). It didn't — it stays ~22%, because Tesla's >Waymo mass comes from its mode‑B (median 9,000) and the HW4 tail, both comfortably above Waymo whether it's 4,700 or 6,000. So no Tesla re‑fit needed.

Quals updated: the fleet‑forecast qual now expects Waymo unimodal with median ~6,000 and a tail past 8,000.

Sources: FutureSearch — Waymo forecast · Forbes/Ohnsman — fleet math & 1M target · Electrek — Ojai 6th‑gen live May 28 · Automotive World — 1M weekly as the 2026 metric

Done. This was a CSS-only consistency pass on [style.css](style.css) — zero copy, DOM, or JS changes. All 47 quals pass, and I verified every section by playwright screenshot on desktop and mobile with no console errors. Changes are in the working tree for you to review and commit.

What made it look vibe-coded was accumulated styling drift, so the core change is a :root design-token block that everything now draws from:

  • One palette instead of five. Text grays went from 8 values to 3 (--ink/--ink-soft/--ink-faint); blues from 5 to one accent family; the two separate green/red verdict pairs merged into one (--safer/--worse/--iffy), so prediction-market odds, stress badges, and freshness dots now agree; the prediction-market panel's Stripe-derived colors and the tan/dark-red source-table theme both moved onto the shared palette.
  • Deliberate type scale. h1 down to 1.6rem with tighter tracking, h2 to 1.2rem with a 1px hairline instead of the 2px GitHub-README underline, links get one color (they were default browser blue).
  • The abstract callout lost its blue-left-bar-docs-site look; now a plain hairline-bordered card.
  • Summary cards went from 6 cramped ~140px flex columns to a 3-per-row grid — which lands as one row of human cohorts over one row of AV companies.
  • Deleted ~65 lines of dead CSS: .ai-explainer (yellow Courier box), the .ci-bar-* "Rate estimator" block, .month-tri — zero references in the JS, HTML, quals, or README.

Two things flagged for you:

  1. The tan source-table theme matched the deleted .ai-explainer styling, so I read it as a leftover rather than a choice. If it was a deliberate "sources look different" cue, that hunk is easy to revert.

15:00 Claude Code claude-fable-5 thought for 13m17s · 53m48s wall-clock time

Good question to ask now — the correctness-critical statistical core got a heavy review pass in late June, but the newest code (the fleet forecast from the "Forecast graphs" commit) hasn't been audited, and there are a few latent items the scratchpad flags. Let me do a fresh review pass: newest code first, then scripted data-integrity checks that the quals don't already cover. I'll report findings only, not change anything.

The "Forecast graphs" commit also rewrote 178 lines of vmt.csv — unusual for a chart feature. Let me see what changed in the data there:

Anchor months unchanged. Let me see exactly which rows changed numerically:

Numeric core columns are identical — the 178-line vmt.csv diff was only in bands/rationale columns. Let me check those:

Let me check the CSV header to interpret the columns correctly before judging:

This forecast code is dense — let me finish reading the trajectory chart and then check a few numerical suspicions empirically:

Confirmed the missing period after "more minor events" ([crashla.js:3215](crashla.js:3215)). Now the dispersion test and the stats core:

The dispersion test is sound. Let me check what the existing data quals already cover so I only hunt for new gaps:

The invariants qual expects a 13-column VMT header with coverage fields, but data/vmt.csv has 9 columns. Let me see how these relate:

slurp.py injects the coverage columns — consistent. Now let me run fresh data-integrity checks over the incident data itself:

Yes — several real ones. I reviewed the newest code (the fleet forecast), cross-checked the data against Waymo's published milestones, and ran integrity checks over the incident data. Nothing changes the headline conclusions (default window unaffected in all cases), but three findings are substantive. Report only — I haven't changed anything.

Data errors

1. Early Waymo VMT contradicts Waymo's own published milestones — by up to 4x.
The series is anchored correctly from ~2024 onward but overshoots badly before that:

| Date | App cumulative | Published | Ratio |
|---|---|---|---|
| end Jan 2023 | 4,217,018 | ~1M (crossed Jan 2023) | ~4.2x |
| end Oct 2023 | 9,813,999 | 7.14M (the 7.1M paper) | ~1.4x |
| end Jan 2025 | 59,374,000 | 56.7M (Kusano & Scanlon) | 1.05x |

The 2021–22 rows' own rationale *cites* the ~1M-Jan-2023 milestone, but the column sums to 3.67M by Dec 2022. Even the kyoom_min band (~2.1M at Jan 2023) excludes the true ~1M. This also explains an anomaly I found independently: 13 months in 2021–22 have VMT but zero Waymo incidents — 2.23M claimed miles that would predict ~15 incidents at the fleet's own overall rate (P(0) ≈ e⁻¹⁵). With VMT corrected ~4x down, expectation drops to ~4, which is merely unlucky rather than impossible. Impact: early points on the full-history MPI chart read ~4x too safe; window totals and the default view are unaffected since later months are anchor-pinned.

2. The Jan-2025 SF fatality has vehiclesInvolved = 2; its narrative describes six vehicles.
The narrative (report 30270-9724) lists the AV, the car behind it, the SUV, a fourth car, plus "at least two other vehicles" per SFPD, and "five passengers in four of the vehicles" injured. [slurp.py:130](data/slurp.py:130)'s own rule says to override the default of 2 "when the narrative reveals more vehicles were involved" — but VEHICLES_INVOLVED contains only the Sep-2025 Tempe fatality (set to 3). Under the Koopman fractional-death method this death should count ~1/6 on Waymo's account, not 1/2: full-history Waymo fatality mass 0.833 → 0.5, i.e. fatality MPI currently understated ~1.67x. The error is in the anti-Waymo (conservative) direction, and the default window is unaffected (JAN-2025 is outside it). Note the 2026-06-27 review entry assumed both fatalities had explicit overrides; only one does.

3. Waymo cumulative rides history sits below published milestones — outside its own bands.
RIDES_HISTORY.Waymo has 7M [6M–8.5M] at Jun 2025, but Waymo announced 10M cumulative paid trips on May 20, 2025; and 17M [14M–21M] at Dec 2025 vs ~20M lifetime by end of 2025. The section self-describes as "loosely sourced" from weekly rates, but these cumulative milestones were headline announcements and fall outside the authored 90% bands. The Jan-2027 forecast (48M) is roughly consistent with the *true* trajectory, so the drawn dashed extrapolation has the wrong slope rather than the wrong endpoint.

4. First-month kyoom band incoherence (introduced in the Forecast graphs commit).
That commit tightened cumulative bands to ~1.5x on the estimate-era rows while leaving monthly bands at 2x. At each series' first month, cumulative *is* the monthly value, so the two bands describe the same physical quantity and disagree: zoox 2024-05 kyoom [750, 2250] vs monthly [750, 3000]; waymo 2021-07 kyoom [27.5k, 82.5k] vs monthly [27.5k, 110k]. (Tesla's first row is coherent.) The kyoom-band qual's "anchors only tighten" invariant can't catch the month-1 equality case.

Analysis issues

5. The miles/rides forecast tooltips label the mode "Median".
For those metrics each curve is a two-piece log-normal with best at the *mode*, but the dot's tooltip reads "Median: …". For Tesla miles {8M, lo 4M, hi 500M} I computed the drawn density's actual median at ~31.7M — the labeled "median" sits at its 14th percentile. Same for Tesla rides. Negligible for near-symmetric Waymo/Zoox; the fleet-metric curves compute true mixture medians and are fine. This is the same mode-vs-median distinction the app deliberately unified in June. (The "90% CI" label is defensible: the lo–hi interval holds exactly 90% of the drawn mass, just not equal-tailed — 1.4% below, 8.6% above.)

6. Comment/data drift in FLEET_HISTORY.
The comment above the table says "~3,000 (Feb 2026) → ~3,300 (May)" for Waymo; the rows say 3,300 (Feb) and 3,750 (May). The forecast block cites a third set (~3,600 Q1, ~3,871 Jun). One of these is stale.

7. Soft point on the Tesla weight calibration.
B = 0.24 is fit so P(Tesla > Waymo) ≈ the ~23% Manifold market, and the arithmetic works out (0.05 + 0.24×0.73 ≈ 0.23) — but only if mode C's mass counts, and the market asks about vehicles "providing ridehailing," which the code's own scope note says C's personal HW4 cars are not. Excluding C, B would need to be ~0.31. Judgment call, but the calibration and the scope note are in tension.

Nits

  • Missing period: "…reports more minor events This inflates…" at [crashla.js:3215](crashla.js:3215).
  • The sanity table's "Property damage only" column is computed as "not an injury," so the 16 Unknown-severity incidents count as property-damage-only.

Things I checked that came up clean: no duplicate report IDs, all 1,836 faultfracs in [0,1] with 1:1 faultfrac.csv coverage, all dates/speeds well-formed, the recent Waymo VMT anchors (127M/170.7M/220.6M) intact through the Forecast commit, the fleet mixture math (normalization, quantile inversion, the ~23% market fit), the dispersion test formula, and the Tesla/Zoox VMT-vs-incident timing.

Happy to fix any subset of these on your go — #1 and #3 mean re-deriving the early Waymo VMT ramp and rides history against the milestone list, #2 is a one-line override plus a qual, and I'd suggest pinning #1's milestones in a qual the same way human-benchmark-provenance does.

Sources: Waymo first-million blog, 7.1M-mile paper, CNBC 10M trips, Waymo 2025 year in review

All nine fixes are in, one at a time, each with a red-then-green qual where one made sense. 49 quals pass (47 + two new files), and I verified the affected charts and tables live with no console errors. Everything is in the working tree, uncommitted — the file-to-fix mapping below should let you commit them separately if you want.

#1 Early Waymo VMT (data/vmt.csv, data/vmt.js, new quals/waymo-vmt-provenance.qual.mjs) — Rewrote the 47 waymo rows 2021-07..2025-05 as exponential segments pinned to five published milestones: ~1M crossed Jan 2023, 7.14M through Oct 2023, 25.3M through Jul 2024 (Swiss Re study), 56.7M through Jan 2025, minus ~150k pre-series miles. Cumulative at 2025-05 is preserved to the mile, so every 2025-06+ row — and all default-window numbers — are byte-identical. The new qual pins all seven milestones (it also caught its own first draft being wrong about the mid-month 100M pin). This dissolves the zero-incident anomaly: expected incidents in those early months drop from ~15 to ~4.

#2 Fatality divisor (data/slurp.py, data/incidents.js, quals/fatality-guard.qual.mjs) — Added the VEHICLES_INVOLVED override (6) for the Jan-2025 SF fatality; full-history Waymo fatality mass goes 0.833 → 0.5. The guard qual now pins both fatalities' divisors and will trip on any new fatality before it silently divides by the default 2.

#3 Waymo rides (crashla.js, new quals/rides-provenance.qual.mjs) — History re-derived to bracket 10M trips May 2025 and ~20M end-2025; forecast 48M → 50M. Qual pins the milestones and cumulative monotonicity.

#4 First-month kyoom (data/vmt.csv/js, quals/kyoom-band.qual.mjs) — zoox 2024-05 kyoom_max 2250 → 3000; the qual now asserts every helmer's first row has identical cumulative and monthly bands.

#5 Mode-vs-median (crashla.js, quals/fleet-forecast.qual.mjs) — Extracted a generic quantilesFromLogDensity, and the miles/rides curves now display computed [q05, median, q95] instead of the raw {best, lo, hi} parameters. Visible change to flag: Tesla's "Median" moves from 8M to ~32M miles (and ~800k to ~2.8M rides) — those are the honest medians of the densities you authored, since best is the mode by construction. If you *meant* best as your median, the right fix is a different density family instead; say the word.

#6–#8 (crashla.js) — FLEET_HISTORY anchor comment aligned with its rows; the scope-C-counts-toward-the-market assumption documented in the Tesla weight fit (with the B≈0.32 alternative noted in-comment); the missing period added.

#9 Unknown severity (crashla.js, quals/sanity-values.qual.mjs) — SEVERITY_INFO.Unknown gains an unk flag; both sanity tables exclude Unknowns from "Property damage only", and the severity-breakdown table gains an "Unknown" column so the buckets partition Total (verified live: Waymo 782+64+32+1+2 = 881). English header per your standing sanity-section-is-English call, matching the passenger table's existing "Unknown" column.

One residual I deliberately did not touch: the existing 2025-06/07 Waymo rows imply ~103M cumulative at Jul 15, 2025 vs the 100M milestone (~3% high). Fixing it means reshaping rows inside the default window, which moves your headline denominators — flagged in the scratchpad entry for you to decide.

So the CPUC-scaled monthly profile your methodology documents was right all along; only the level was wrong. The fix honors that:

This removes the ~1.7% conservative bias the README itself had predicted the offset was causing. Tesla, Zoox, and all human benchmarks are unchanged.

what's your recommendation on the judgment calls?
13:40 Claude Code claude-fable-5 thought for 1m31s

#7 (weight fit): keep it as-is. The documented assumption — scenario C implies the market resolves YES — is the more defensible reading. If Tesla flips eyes-off across ~hundreds of thousands of HW4 cars, the market's "vehicles providing ridehailing" threshold (~6k, Waymo's fleet) is cleared by even ~1% owner participation in Tesla Network, and a C-world also unthrottles Tesla's first-party robotaxi fleet, since the binding constraint (software validation) is gone by hypothesis. So P(YES | C) is ~0.9–1, not 0, and the arithmetic barely moves: 0.24×0.73 + 0.05×0.95 ≈ 0.22 ≈ the market. Bumping B to 0.32 would distort the robotaxi-scope curve on the strength of the *less* plausible reading.

#5 (forecast best): the current fix is consistent but I'd go one step further — model Tesla's miles/rides as the scenario mixture, like the fleet metric already does. Here's the actual problem: your belief about Tesla's Jan-2027 miles isn't a one-humped distribution. It's the push-forward of the A/B/C scenarios — ~71% "boring, ~8M", ~24% "aggressive, tens of millions", ~5% "HW4, hundreds of millions". The two-piece log-normal can't represent that, and it distorts the median in a specific way: it smears the 29% non-A mass into one huge fat tail, which drags the computed median out to ~32M. Under the true mixture, the median would sit just above the A-scenario — probably ~9–12M, close to your authored 8M. So neither the old display (8M labeled "Median", 14th percentile of the drawn curve) nor the current one (~32M, honest for the drawn curve but the drawn curve misrepresents the belief) is really right; the *density* is the thing to fix.

The upgrade is cheap and makes the three metrics uniform: give Tesla's miles and rides the same mixture structure as FLEET_FORECAST — reuse the A/B/C weights (0.71/0.24/0.05) with per-scenario miles/rides log-normals, and split the display into the same two lanes (robotaxi-scope solid = A+B, HW4-scope dashed conditional = C). That also fixes a scope inconsistency for free: right now the 500M hi bakes C's personal-car miles into Tesla's *single* miles curve, while the fleet chart carefully quarantines C into a dashed conditional lane. All the machinery exists (fleetMixtureLogDensity/Quantiles, the lane splitter); what's needed from you is per-scenario medians, though they can be derived mechanically — e.g. miles ≈ current 3.4M cumulative + fleet-scenario trajectory × miles-per-vehicle-month calibrated from today's fleet (~40–60 vehicles doing ~430k mi/mo → ~8–10k mi/vehicle-month), giving roughly A ≈ 7.5M, B ≈ 20–30M, C ≈ 300–600M.

If you say go, I'll build it with derived parameters and show you the numbers for sign-off before they land. Until then, what's in the tree is internally consistent and qual-pinned — just built on the unimodal simplification.

The scenario bands underneath are my derivations (A: fleet 50→180 at the observed ~8.5k mi/vehicle-month; B: Q4-weighted Cybercab ramp, {40M, 15M–120M}; C: eyes-off HW4 at {400M, 100M–1.5B} miles / {15M, 2M–120M} Tesla-Network rides) — they're plain constants in MILES_FORECAST/RIDES_FORECAST if you want to turn any of the knobs; the quals pin structure and quantile-honesty, not those specific values (except the robotaxi-median-lands-in-A regression at [7M, 15M]).

Implementation-wise this deleted more than it added conceptually: forecastLanes now does scope-splitting for all three metrics, trajectoryLanes replaced the separate fleet/helmer lane builders, and the mixture quantiles reuse the same generic numeric-CDF helper throughout. Nothing is broken — the error is slurp.py's intentional stale-VMT guard doing its job. NHTSA has published June 2026 incidents, but [vmt.csv](data/vmt.csv) currently ends at 2026-05, so the script aborts at [slurp.py:802](data/slurp.py#L802) rather than silently dropping the new crashes.

What I found when I ran it:

  • The fetch and snapshot steps succeed; the failure is the deliberate must(not stale, ...) check that fires when NHTSA reports incidents in months with no VMT row.
  • The stale month is 2026-06, with 17 newly published incidents: 16 from Waymo and 1 from Tesla (report 13781-15342).
  • The VMT master has tesla/waymo/zoox rows through 2026-05 only, so this is just the usual monthly-update trigger: NHTSA's data has moved past your denominator.

The fix is the one the error message prescribes: add 2026-06 rows to data/vmt.csv (tesla, waymo, and zoox for parallel coverage) and re-run. That means fresh VMT estimates with rationales — e.g., whether Waymo has published a newer cumulative-miles anchor than the 220.6M end-of-March figure the recent rows extrapolate from, and where Tesla's TX fleet ramp stands for June.

  • Waymo: 21.5M (band 15.05M–27.95M, ±30%). No anchor newer than 220.6M-through-March exists — the Safety Impact hub still shows it — so June continues the April/May extrapolation at ~5M rider-only mi/week, consistent with 500k weekly rides in late March doubling toward the 1M year-end target. The San Diego/Las Vegas/Tampa/Denver launches started July 8, so they don't move June.
  • Tesla: 450k (band 200k–680k, wide). Geofence jumped to the full Austin metro June 3, but with only ~20 active unsupervised cars (42 cumulative ever); ramp deferred to FSD v15, Phoenix still staging, Dallas/Houston geofences tiny. So roughly flat vs May.
  • Zoox: 300k (0.7–1.3× band). The commercial FMVSS exemption is still undecided so rides remain free; LV + SF-east expansion continue per the March announcements.

The run then surfaced the real monthly workload: NHTSA's mid-July drop contained 107 unrated incidents (not just June's 17) — the May Monthly wave: 100 Waymo, 4 Zoox, 3 Tesla. I read all 107 narratives and assessed each under the locked P(expert-human-avoids) criterion and scenario map, then appended them to [faultfrac.csv](data/faultfrac.csv). Distribution: 80 zeros (the usual stopped-AV-rear-ended/clipped dominance), the rest 0.05–0.3 for ROW violations against the AV, four 0.8 undercarriage-groundings at lot entrances, and three high-fault ones worth your eyes:

  • 30270-15296 (Waymo, Austin, June) = 0.9 — the AV initiated a right turn out of a parking lot into a bus actively passing in front of it, hitting the bus's rear quarter. New pattern, added to the memory map.
  • 30270-15029 (Waymo, SF) = 0.9 — squeezed past a construction a-frame sign and hit it (curb-strike precedent).
  • 13781-15399 (Tesla, Dallas) = 1.0 — drove into a chain across a lot entrance, exactly repeating Tesla's 13781-11687 precedent.

One qual re-pinned: Tesla crossed the 20-incident threshold in [crashla.js:3241](crashla.js#L3241), so its Poisson-dispersion verdict legitimately flipped from "too few incidents to tell" to "overdispersed" (index ≈ 15.5, driven by the wild early tiny-fleet months). I updated the pinned expectation in [sanity-checks.qual.mjs](quals/sanity-checks.qual.mjs) with a comment explaining why — flagging since that's a test-expectation change.

Final state: slurp.py runs clean (June incident coverage correctly estimated at ~0.15 since June Monthlies are only partially filed), all 51 quals pass, and the dataset now stands at 1,943 incidents (Waymo 1,883 / Zoox 39 / Tesla 21). Nothing committed — the changes are in the working tree ([faultfrac.csv](data/faultfrac.csv) +107, [vmt.csv](data/vmt.csv) +3, regenerated [incidents.js](data/incidents.js)/[vmt.js](data/vmt.js), one qual) whenever you want to review and commit.

did you include miami robotaxi miles?
12:36 Claude Code claude-fable-5 thought for 39s

Yes — implicitly, by construction, and that's the right scope. Details:

  • The Waymo row is a single US-wide series ("US est."), not a sum of per-city terms. It's anchored to Waymo's published cumulative *rider-only miles*, which Waymo defines fleet-wide across every city it operates in — so Miami (and Nashville, Miami Beach) miles are inside the June 21.5M estimate, just not as a separate line item. The growth I assumed over the late-March ~4–5M mi/week pace is exactly where the new-market ramp lives.
  • That matters for numerator/denominator matching: the new May/June batch includes incidents in Miami, Miami Beach, and Nashville (e.g. 30270-15263, 30270-15260, 30270-15392), and a US-wide denominator covers them by definition. If the denominator were per-city (PHX/SF/LA/ATX/ATL only), those incidents would be orphaned — the vmt-master-fidelity qual's "no orphan incidents" check and the US-scope convention are what keep this honest.

One caveat worth knowing: in the March 220.6M anchor, the five published per-city figures (80.551 + 67.078 + 51.816 + 15.789 + 5.379) sum to the full 220.6M — so Miami's contribution through March was negligible/under rounding. That means the *anchor* contains essentially no Miami miles, and Miami's Q2 ramp is pure extrapolation territory. It's small relative to the ~21M/month total and well inside the ±30% band, but if Waymo's next Safety Impact update adds Miami (or Nashville) to the per-city breakdown, that'd be the moment to recalibrate.

I had repeated an unverified sentence from a search-result summary. Now corrected in [vmt.csv](data/vmt.csv), regenerated into vmt.js (slurp re-run was snapshot-stable, 0 field updates on the fault sync), quals all green.

What I audited that came up clean before finding it:

  • Arithmetic: all three June rows' cumulative sums, additive kyoom bands, and 0.7×/1.3× monthly bands check out exactly (the kyoom-band qual independently validates bracketing/monotonicity).
  • Judgments vs the locked map: re-checked all 27 nonzero calls against the narratives and scenario map; they hold. Closest quibble: 30270-15095 (AV passed a stopped pickup+trailer in a slip lane) got 0.25 via the mid-maneuver-pass rule; 0.2 via the pass-a-stopped-vehicle rule is arguable.
  • CSV mechanics: structured columns match the NHTSA master exactly.

And one borderline finding you should rule on rather than me: the new batch brought four severity="Unknown" incidents, and three have narratives that resolve it — 30270-15022, 30270-15096, 30270-15301 all state someone was transported to a hospital by ambulance, which under your existing SEVERITY_OVERRIDE precedents (2908275d904dec6, bb1ec8d2c85745a) would become "Minor W/ Hospitalization" and flip them into the hospitalization metric (30610-15295, driver "pretty bruised" but no transport, would be "Minor W/O Hospitalization"). I didn't add overrides because 16 older Unknowns also sit unresolved — so either the practice is deliberately selective, or there's a standing backlog and my three are additions to it. If you want, I'll check the 16 old narratives and propose overrides for every resolvable one.

12:44 Claude Code claude-fable-5 thought for 9m15s · 10m7s wall-clock time

Two threads: (1) resolve the severity-Unknown backlog, (2) explain and fix the bracket-wrapped narrative plus any other formatting inconsistencies. Starting by pulling the 16 old Unknown narratives and scanning all narratives for artifacts:

The edit went through, but I need to verify byte-exactly what landed in the file — the keys involve invisible characters and a wrong key here would corrupt narratives:

File state confirmed good. Now the narrative-processing block and the severity overrides:

Now extending the boilerplate qual to cover the new normalizations, so regressions fail loudly:

All done and verified. Summary of this round:

Severity backlog — all 20 Unknowns resolved. Every one was resolvable from its narrative, so SEVERITY_OVERRIDE in [slurp.py](data/slurp.py) now has 20 new entries (each with a one-line justification), following the existing precedents:

  • 8 → Minor W/ Hospitalization — narrative states someone was transported to a hospital (incl. the three from the new batch, plus e.g. 30270-8243's high-speed rear-end with airbags and both cars towed).
  • 8 → Minor W/O Hospitalization — alleged/unspecified injuries without transport (incl. the doored cyclist who independently went to urgent care, and both Zoox after-the-fact injury claims).
  • 4 → Property Damage — no human injury mentioned (two are no-contact incidents where cars collided *behind* the AV).

This grows the hospitalization metric's numerator and, satisfyingly, moved our full-history Waymo injury rate from 0.84× to 0.91× of Waymo's published figure — tighter agreement, still within the reconciliation qual's bounds. One deliberate non-change: the severity column in [faultfrac.csv](data/faultfrac.csv) still shows Unknown for these because it mirrors the raw NHTSA master by design; overrides apply when incidents.js is built.

The square-bracket mystery: NHTSA's raw CSV cell for 13781-15342 is literally [Summary: ...] — Tesla filed that one narrative wrapped in brackets while all its other filings use a bare Summary: prefix. slurp now unwraps that case and strips the contentless Summary: label from the five Tesla narratives that had it, so Tesla blurbs read like everyone else's.

Bonus finds from the consistency sweep, all now normalized at ingestion:

  • 30270-13372 had unmapped mojibake — curly quotes as raw UTF-8-bytes-read-as-Latin-1, one with its leading byte lost entirely. The new NARRATIVE_MOJIBAKE entries are written as visible \u escapes (the existing keys contain invisible control characters, which is also why they look garbled in an editor).
  • Redaction-marker typos {XXX}, ]XXX], [XXX[ and short [XX] markers (7 narratives) → uniform [XXX], via a new exact-keyed NARRATIVE_TYPOS map.

Guardrails: [narrative-boilerplate.qual.mjs](quals/narrative-boilerplate.qual.mjs) now also asserts every typo variant is absent from the artifact and that no narrative opens with Summary: or [Summary: (the mojibake loop picks up the new map entries automatically). Full suite green; changes are in the working tree, uncommitted.

14:26 Claude Code claude-fable-5 thought for 2m20s · 2m40s wall-clock time

You're right to push back — the tracker's full time series tells a different story than the point-in-time articles I anchored on. Let me check whether there's reported cause for the wind-down, then fix the number.

Updating the memory note to match, then summarizing:

Fixed — Tesla June is now 280k (0.65× May), down from my 450k. Good catch, and to answer "are we sure?": no, we weren't, and the revision is the defensible call.

Where I went wrong: I built the June estimate from two point-in-time snapshots (Electrek June 3: ~20 active unsupervised; full-metro geofence) and read that as "roughly flat vs May, geofence up → nudge up." Your tracker chart shows what the snapshots hid: a continuous drawdown across the whole month — Austin active falling from ~40 toward ~20, unsupervised from its ~35 May peak toward the single digits it shows now (6 active-7d), Dallas/Houston never growing. Since tracker-anchored miles scale with active fleet × utilization, a month whose average fleet is ~35% smaller should have ~35% fewer miles. Electrek (May 26) and autoevolution both corroborate the wind-down.

The new row: 280,000 central, band 140k–560k (wide and top-skewed since Q2 earnings on ~July 22 could reveal utilization the tracker can't see), cumulative 2,691,047, rationale citing the tracker drawdown. Downstream effects, all regenerated and green across the qual suite:

  • Tesla's June MPI dot drops accordingly (1 incident on fewer miles).
  • The pooled June incident-coverage estimate ticked up slightly (0.153 → 0.157) since fewer expected Tesla miles means fewer expected incidents — the machinery handled it.

One heads-up for the July row next month: if the tracker's single-digit unsupervised count persists, July's Tesla VMT will be a steep further drop — and Q2 earnings should finally give a hard anchor for Q2 as a whole, which may warrant recalibrating April–June together the way Q1 was.

Fixed (in the working tree — everything else you'd already committed):

  1. [README.md:234](README.md#L234) stale counts — still said "1,836 incidents: 1,783 Waymo, 18 Tesla, 35 Zoox" from the June refresh. Now 1,943 (1,883 / 21 / 39) with the fetch date. This one's on me: the June batch updated the README and my July batch didn't.

hmm, the june VMT for tesla still seems suspicious. can you re-review all the numbers and remind me what the solid anchors are?
15:03 Claude Code claude-fable-5 thought for 1m38s

Fair — let me lay out the whole Tesla chain, separating what's actually anchored from what's estimate, because the honest answer is that June's absolute level rests on a assumption worth scrutinizing.

The anchor inventory

Hard anchors (Tesla-stated, recorded by robotaxitracker as "known cumulative milestones"):

| Date | Cumulative miles | Source | vmt.csv agreement |
|---|---|---|---|
| Jul 23, 2025 | 7,000 | Q2-2025 earnings | end-Jul cume = 7,000 ✓ |
| Oct 22, 2025 | 250,000 | Q3-2025 earnings | end-Oct cume = 250,000 (~9-day smear to month-end) |
| Dec 31, 2025 | 650,000 | Q4 disclosure | end-Dec cume = 650,000 ✓ (verified against tracker in the 2026-05-22 audit) |

Semi-anchor: Q1-2026 earnings said paid robotaxi miles roughly *doubled sequentially*. The series applies that as a ratio transfer: Q4-TX (481,584) × ~2 → Q1-TX ≈ 913,047, cume 1,563,047 end-March. This is documented in the Jan–Mar rationales, but note what it assumes: that the growth ratio of whatever Tesla meant by "paid robotaxi miles" (possibly including the ~120-car Bay Area safety-driver service, which is *excluded* from our denominator) transfers to the TX-only scope.

No anchor at all: April, May, June. Pure estimates informed by tracker fleet counts. The next real anchor arrives with Q2 earnings (~July 22, next week).

The cross-check that makes June "suspicious"

Implied utilization = monthly miles ÷ days ÷ average active TX fleet:

  • 2025 anchored era: Sep–Oct ran ~85–92k/month on ~25–30 active cars → ~115 mi/vehicle/day (this is the figure the trackers themselves use, straight from the 250k anchor).
  • 2026 series: Mar 350k on ~40 active → ~280 mi/veh/day. May 440k on ~50 → ~285. June 280k on ~33 average → ~283 mi/veh/day.

So the whole 2026 series runs at ~2.5× the anchored 2025 utilization. There are two readings:

  1. It's real: Sep-2025+ highway rides (driver-seat monitor mode, in scope) add high-speed miles, geofences grew ~10×, and 280 mi/veh/day is not absurd — Waymo averages ~190 including deadhead. Under this reading, June 280k is fine and internally consistent: it's 0.64× May, tracking the fleet drawdown you pointed out.
  2. It's inherited inflation: if Tesla's "~2× Q1" statement was mostly Bay-Area-driven, the Q1 level is too high, and Apr–Jun inherited it. Under this reading June's floor is more like fleet × 115 ≈ 115k — which is essentially where our band floor sits (140k), but the central would be too high by ~2×.

I can't distinguish these with public data today; nothing Tesla has said since January bears on TX-only miles. That's the genuine state of knowledge: June's *relative* level (0.64× May) is now well-grounded in the tracker trend; its *absolute* level hangs on the Q1 ratio-transfer assumption, same as April and May.

What I'd do

Wait the week: Q2 earnings should give a paid-robotaxi-miles figure (and possibly a TX/Bay split or cumulative number), which would let us recalibrate all three Q2 months at once the same way Q1 was — and retroactively test the ratio-transfer assumption against reality. Revising June alone today would just be swapping one unanchored guess for another inside a band that already spans both readings (140k–560k).

If you'd rather not carry the optimistic reading even for a week, the defensible conservative move is centering June near the geometric middle of the two readings (~180k) — say the word and I'll make that edit. Otherwise I'd leave the numbers as they stand and flag the Q1 ratio-transfer caveat in the Tesla rationale text so the assumption is visible in the audit trail.

well, i want to be neither optimistic nor conservative, just perfectly calibrated for the data we have at this point. what do you recommend?
15:13 Claude Code claude-fable-5 thought for 1m31s

Good framing — and forcing myself to actually compute the calibrated answer changed my mind about my own "two readings" story from last turn. The conservative reading mostly dissolves when you check it against the hard anchors. Here's the math:

The utilization tension, re-derived from anchors only

The "115 mi/vehicle/day" figure (which the safety trackers still quote) comes from the 250k @ Oct 22, 2025 anchor era — i.e. before highway rides scaled. But we have a *later* hard anchor window:

  • Oct 22 → Dec 31, 2025: 650k − 250k = 400k miles over 70 days ≈ 5,700 mi/day on a tracker-observed active fleet of ~20–30 → ~200–330 mi/vehicle/day, already in that fully-anchored window.
  • Q1 2026 (semi-anchored by the ~2× earnings statement): 913k over 90 days ≈ 10,100/day on ~35 avg active → ~290 mi/vehicle/day — corroborating, not inflating. And note the ratio-transfer needs only modest per-vehicle growth once you account for the fleet growing ~1.4× that quarter.

So the ~280 mi/veh/day regime is anchor-confirmed from November 2025 onward (highway/monitor-mode miles explain the step up from ~115, and those are in-scope). My worry that the 2026 level was "inherited inflation" from a Bay-Area-contaminated Q1 statement was overweighting a stale number: applying 115 to June would contradict the December 31 hard anchor, not respect it.

What that implies for June

Calibrated June = anchored utilization regime × tracker-observed June fleet:

  • June average active TX fleet (your chart): Austin ~40 declining to ~20, plus Dallas/Houston ~5 → ~30–35 average
  • × 260–290 mi/veh/day × 30 days → ~250–290k, point estimate ≈ 270k

The current central is 280k — within ~4% of the calibrated point, and that residual is far below chart-reading noise (is the June average fleet 30 or 35?), let alone the honest band. The one directional consideration left is whether a wind-down cuts per-car utilization; the "long waits" reporting suggests demand still exceeds supply, so holding utilization flat is the neutral call.

Recommendation

  1. Keep 280k. After correcting my framing, it's not the optimistic number — it's the one consistent with the last hard anchor and the fleet trend. Moving to ~270k would be false precision; moving toward ~180k (the midpoint I floated last turn) would actually *miscalibrate* against the Dec 31 anchor.
  2. Fix the audit trail, not the number: the rationale currently justifies 280k only as "~0.65x May." I'd add the real basis — anchored utilization (~200–330 mi/veh/day from the Oct 22→Dec 31 window, Q1-corroborated) × June fleet — so the next audit doesn't re-derive the same false tension I did.
  3. Recalibrate all of Q2 in one pass when Q2 earnings land (~July 22) — that's the next real anchor, and it retroactively tests both the Q1 ratio-transfer and this utilization regime.

Want me to make the rationale edit (no number changes)?

what about robotaxitracker.com? that's real evidence pushing us downward, right? (i know we'll know in 2 weeks but it has to be our true best estimate in the meantime)
15:25 Claude Code claude-fable-5 thought for 1m45s

Right — and let's be precise about *which* tracker evidence is new versus already priced in. Its fleet-activity series is exactly what drove 450k→280k. But the tracker also runs its own mileage model ("anchor-constrained, activity-weighted") — if it publishes a current estimate, that's a direct comparable. Let me try to pull their actual number:

The tracker publishes no mileage estimate for 2026 — only the methodology and the three 2025 milestones we already use. So there's no tracker *number* to defer to; the question is what its underlying evidence implies. Worked through carefully, here's where it lands:

The tracker's evidence is already the load-bearing input. Its fleet-activity series is precisely what moved June from 450k to 280k. The remaining question is whether it pushes further down, and this is where the decomposition matters:

  • Only the product (fleet × miles-per-vehicle) is anchored. The Oct 22 → Dec 31 hard window fixes ~5,700 mi/day. Whether that was ~25 spotted cars at ~280 mi/day each, or ~50 true cars (including monitor-mode vehicles spotters miss) at ~115 each, is unresolvable from public data — spotting-based counts are a floor, not a census. The "115 vs 280" tension I raised isn't two states of the world with different June totals; it's two bookkeepings of the *same* anchored total.
  • Ratio scaling cancels the ambiguity. Projecting June as May × (June activity ÷ May activity) is independent of which decomposition is true, as long as spotted activity is roughly proportional to the real fleet. That's also exactly what the tracker's own "anchor-constrained, activity-weighted" model does between anchors — so if they did publish a June figure, it would be built the same way.
  • Taken at face value, the activity ratio is ~0.7, not lower. May averaged ~45 active TX vehicles on your chart; June ~30–32. Pure activity scaling gives 440k × ~0.71 ≈ 310k. Our 280k (0.64×) is already *below* that — the extra ~10% is an implicit allowance for utilization sagging during a wind-down, which the "long waits" reporting (supply-limited, cars still maxed out) doesn't even clearly support.

So the calibrated read: robotaxitracker's data pushes June down relative to May — by ~30–36% — and we've applied slightly more than that. It does not support the ~115–150k reading, because that reading takes the stale per-vehicle figure from one decomposition and the spotted-fleet floor from the other, double-counting the pessimism against a hard anchor that contradicts the combination.

Best estimate today: 280k, band 140k–560k, stands — centered in the defensible 250–310k activity-scaled range, wide enough for both the steep-early-June-decline scenario (~240k) and the spotting-undercount scenario (~310k+). What would legitimately move it before earnings: the tracker publishing its model output, or evidence the June decline was front-loaded rather than roughly linear.

Still happy to write this calibration basis into the June rationale (no number change) so the reasoning survives the next audit — say the word.
